philosophy: Overwhelm opponents with exponential Goblin token generation, leveraging red’s direct damage and combat tricks to close games quickly. It wins by racing faster than control and combo can answer, and out-scaling midrange with relentless board presence.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Krenko, and at least one token generator or cheap red spell. Mulligan if you have no lands, only high-cost spells, or no way to generate initial tokens. Prioritize hands with Krenko + 1-2 mana sources + a way to make the first 2 Goblins.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, pass. Turn 2: Play Krenko. If you have 2 mana, tap him for 2 Goblins. Turn 3: Attack with Goblins, untap, tap Krenko again for exponential growth, or cast Impact Tremors/cheap red spells to trigger damage. Land sequencing: Prioritize red mana sources early; basic Mountains are fine, but fetches/duals help consistency. Don't overextend into known board wipes.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in board wipes (Pyroclasm, Blasphemous Act), cheap removal (Shock, Lightning Strike), and Lightning Greaves. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more token doublers, Goblin Piledriver, Goblin Bombardment, and protection. vs Mirror: Bring in more removal, Goblin Chainwhirler, and Impact Tremors/Rite of Flame.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is critical; always keep 1-2 mana open for instant-speed responses or Impact Tremors triggers. Don't overcommit all Goblins if you have no way to protect them. Use the graveyard effectively if your list includes sacrifice outlets or recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear path to lethal or when the opponent has no board wipes. Hold back a few Goblins as insurance against sweepers. If the opponent has a known sweeper, save Krenko and key doublers for post-sweeper turns.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (sweepers, artifact/enchantment destruction), targeted removal on Krenko, and hand disruption. Key tells: Opponent holding up mana on their turn often signals a counter or removal. Opponent tapping out on turn 3-4 often signals a combo or sweeper.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Krenko's untapped ability generating 10+ Goblins, often paired with Impact Tremors, Rite of Flame, or combat tricks like Goblin Piledriver/Goblin Chainwhirler. Win by attacking with the full army or using direct damage from token entry.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have no viable board wipes in play, the opponent has low life total, or you've established a clear advantage in board state. Also commit when you can protect Krenko (e.g., with Lightning Greaves or hexproof effects).
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be interrupted, if you're swept multiple times with no recovery tools, or if you're dead to hand disruption with no card draw. Also concede if you're out of mana and the opponent has a locked board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient spells. Cast Krenko on your turn, then use instant-speed token generation or damage spells. If possible, use protection (Lightning Greaves) or cast Krenko when the opponent has no mana up.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure before they can cast their big spells. Use board wipes to reset their board. Target their mana rocks with Lightning Strike or Shock.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal (Shock, Lightning Strike) and board wipes (Pyroclasm, Blasphemous Act). Use Goblins to trade efficiently and close out the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early tokens. Don't let them stabilize by winning the card advantage war. Use Impact Tremors to deal damage while they try to sweep. Out-value them with token doublers and card draw.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by going first, having more efficient token generation, or better removal. Prioritize protecting Krenko, using Impact Tremors to race, and out-resourcing with doublers. Track their board wipes carefully.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw token generation to resilience. Add more interaction (removal, board wipes, protection) and adjust your curve to be more resilient to hate. Prioritize cards that can generate value even after a board wipe, and consider adding card draw to avoid running out of steam.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without considering board wipes. Always keep a few Goblins in reserve. Another: Forgetting to use Impact Tremors on your own tokens. Skirk Prospector synergizes by being a cheap damage source or sacrifice outlet; tap Krenko for Goblins, then tap Prospector to deal damage or sacrifice it to fuel other effects. Mono-red has limited card draw, so hand management and sequencing are crucial to avoid dead draws.

philosophy: Leverage Beledros Witherbloom’s spell-triggered mana acceleration and token generation to fuel Blech’s attack/death value and Pest Infestation’s massive trample swing, winning through overwhelming board presence and card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including Beledros if possible), Blech, and early mana sources. Mulligan if you lack B/G mana, have no Blech/Beledros, or only single lands with no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Blech if mana allows. Turn 2: Attack with Blech or hold for value. Turn 3: Cast Beledros Witherbloom, tap creatures for mana, and begin generating tokens on spell casts. Sequence lands to ensure B/G mana by turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +1 Blood Moon/Chalice of Vitiation, +1 Graveyard hate, -2 Beledros (too slow). vs Control: +1 Card draw, +1 targeted removal, -2 aggro hate. vs Mirror/Combo: +1 Board wipe, +1 targeted removal, -2 redundant tokens.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting spells to trigger Beledros. Manage mana to tap two creatures for three mana of any color to cast Pest Infestation. Don't overcommit Blech if it can be removed for value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Blech to attack when safe or when you can follow up. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers. Use Beledros as both a mana source and a threat.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Fatal Push), and hand disruption. Track if they're holding up counters/removal for Blech or Beledros.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Pest Infestation is the primary finisher. Cast it when you have 3+ creatures to sacrifice, swing with trample tokens, and draw into game-ending threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Beledros online, enough creatures to sacrifice for Pest Infestation, and the opponent has no sweepers or low life total.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (Stax, empty board wipes with no recovery), have no mana for Beledros/Blech, or the opponent is at 1 life with no way to swing.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast Blech/Beledros on the opponent's turn or with protection. Use Pest Infestation's card draw to out-value counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Use Beledros to accelerate. Prioritize early Blech to pressure. Don't let them stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Hold interaction for Blech/Beledros. Use Pest Infestation to stabilize the board and swing back. Mulligan aggressively for early threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw. Cast spells to trigger Beledros. Pressure with Blech to force interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Track their Beledros and Pest Infestation count. Prioritize targeted removal over sweepers. Win by out-drawing with Pest Infestation and protecting your own Beledros.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Generally, bring in hate against combo/control, keep aggro hate against fast decks, and maintain card draw/removal. Prioritize protecting Beledros and ensuring mana consistency.
notes: Common mistake: Casting Pest Infestation without enough creatures to sacrifice, or dying to a sweeper before casting it. Unique interaction: Beledros's mana ability can be activated in response to Blech dying to get the 2/2 token, or to cast Pest Infestation. Don't tap Beledros's creatures if you need to cast spells to trigger it.

philosophy: Cast cheap instants and sorceries to generate a relentless army of 1/1 haste Wizard tokens, then sacrifice them to pump your commander or a secondary threat for lethal. This deck wins by out-casting opponents with instant-speed interaction and overwhelming board presence before control or combo can stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics or fetchable duals), at least one mana acceleration piece (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or a ramp spell), and 2-3 cheap instants/sorceries or a sacrifice outlet. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no acceleration, only expensive spells, or no way to cast your commander early.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet. Turn 2: Cast a cheap instant or sorcery to trigger the commander, creating a 1/1 haste token. Sequence mana to ensure you can cast multiple spells per turn as soon as possible, prioritizing cheap, efficient spells that draw cards or deal damage.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells, and early board wipes. Against control: Bring in more cheap spells, card draw, and direct damage. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counters, and early interaction. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counters, and additional sacrifice outlets to disrupt their token generation.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting instants/sorceries over hoarding cards. Use mana rocks and ramp to maintain a high spell density. Don't overcommit to pumping if you lack sacrifice outlets, and always preserve mana for flash protection on your commander.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough tokens to trade favorably or when the opponent has no removal or sweepers. Hold back if they have board wipes or targeted removal. Use flash to protect your commander and surprise-block or attack.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, etc.), targeted removal on your commander, and counterspells. Note when opponents tap out for big spells versus holding up mana for interaction, and track how many instants/sorceries they've cast to gauge their hand size.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The commander itself (pumped via sacrifice), or a separate big threat fueled by token sacrifice. Lethal comes from a single massive swing or repeated pump-and-swing turns once you have consistent sacrifice outlets.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 3+ tokens, a reliable sacrifice outlet, and the opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand. Also commit when you can close the game before their next turn or when you can trade your tokens for their key permanents.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one life, have no way to generate tokens, the opponent has a board wipe on stack, or you're facing a hard-lock combo with no interaction. Also concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has stabilized with a win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash, split second (if any), or cast spells in response to their counterspells. Use cheap spells to trade up. Rely on instant-speed interaction to clear the way and pressure before they can cast their own spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them. Use counterspells or removal to stop their mana dorks/rituals. Don't let them stabilize; force them to interact with your board while you push for lethal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use cheap removal and counterspells to stall. Build a board of tokens to trade into their attackers. Use pump effects to survive combat and stabilize before going on the offensive.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage and cheap spells. Use flash to bypass their removal windows. Push for lethal before they can find their win condition, and use instant-speed interaction to disrupt their plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: It's a race to cast more spells and find better sacrifice outlets. Prioritize counterspells, targeted removal, and card draw. Win by disrupting their key spells, out-resourcing them in the late game, or finding a unique finisher they can't answer.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure spell density to interaction and hate cards. Against slow decks, bring in more finishers and card draw. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Always keep at least one sacrifice outlet and your commander, and adjust your mana base if needed for specific matchups.
notes: Common mistake: Over-pumping without enough sacrifice outlets. Remember that the commander's ability is a sacrifice outlet itself, but you'll need more for consistent pressure. Prismari spells often have additional effects, so prioritize those that draw cards or deal damage. Flash and haste synergy is key, and always protect your commander with counterspells or protection spells.

philosophy: A midrange-control archetype that leverages Y'shtola's noncreature spell synergy to generate a resilient army of flying Spirits while using targeted removal and bounce to neutralize threats. It aims to out-value aggressive and combo decks through card advantage and board control, closing out games with a resilient token army or a late-game finisher.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocks if available), Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, and at least one early noncreature spell or removal. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no color fixers, or only creatures with no early interaction. Prioritize hands that can play a land + mana rock on turn 1, or land + noncreature spell on turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land, then Sol Ring/Arcane Signet. Turn 2: Play a dual land or fetch/shock, cast a cheap noncreature spell (e.g., bounce, counterspell, or draw) to trigger Y'shtola or build board presence. If opening with a creature, play land, then creature, holding mana for interaction. Sequence lands to ensure B/U/W availability by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in cheap removal, bounce spells, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control/combo: Bring in counterspells, card draw, and graveyard hate. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, bounce, and hand disruption. Against ramp: Bring in artifact destruction, counterspells, and early board wipes.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until turn 4. Use noncreature spells efficiently to trigger Y'shtola without overcommitting. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary recursion unless necessary. Keep a hand of 3-4 cards to navigate disruption.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or bounce ready for the opponent's response. Use Y'shtola's -1/-1 ability to neutralize key threats without spending mana. Hold back on casting expensive noncreature spells if the board is unstable.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, and targeted removal. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or combo pieces. Track graveyard usage for recursion decks. Pay attention to mana development to predict their next play.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Y'shtola herself with a board of flying Spirits, or a late-game spell like a board wipe + draw engine to reset and rebuild. Finish with a combination of token pressure and targeted removal to clear the path.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their key threats, and enough mana to cast multiple noncreature spells in a turn. Commit when the opponent is tapped out or has no answers to flying.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no lands, no interaction, and the opponent has a clear win condition. Concede if you're locked out of your colors or have a dead hand against a fast deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target, or instant-speed noncreature spells. Bounce or remove the counter source. Use Y'shtola's -1/-1 to neutralize blockers. Cast spells in response to their counterspells if possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction is key. Use bounce and cheap removal to slow them down. Bring in artifact destruction and counterspells. Don't let them stabilize; pressure early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap removal and bounce. Use Y'shtola's -1/-1 to neutralize key attackers. Hold up mana for interaction on opponent's turn. Stabilize with a board wipe if available.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with noncreature spells and tokens. Force them to use resources on your threats. Use card draw to out-value them. Attack with flying Spirits to bypass ground blockers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use hand disruption to limit their noncreature spells. Prioritize targeted removal over board wipes. Use Y'shtola's bounce to steal tempo. Control the board with -1/-1 and force them to overcommit.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the specific matchup. Against control, reduce cheap removal and increase counterspells/card draw. Against aggro, add early game stoppers and artifact hate. In the mirror, prioritize hand disruption and targeted removal. Always keep a balance of removal and card advantage.
notes: Y'shtola is vulnerable to board wipes and targeted removal; protect her or have a backup plan. The deck thrives on noncreature spell synergy, so avoid overcommitting to creatures early. Watch for format-specific hate cards. Practice sequencing mana rocks and dual lands to avoid flooding. Common mistake: casting too many noncreature spells without board presence, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. As a fringe archetype, expect unfamiliar hate cards; prioritize information gathering in the first few turns.

philosophy: The deck aims to generate massive amounts of mana, cast a high-impact Dragon, and then chain together multiple Dragon spells to trigger The Ur-Dragon’s ability, creating an exponentially growing army of flying, haste creatures to overwhelm opponents through combat or finish with a game-ending X-spell.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana acceleration piece (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or ramp spell), and an early-game Dragon or removal. Mulligan if you have 4+ lands with no acceleration, or 3+ acceleration with no lands. Keep any hand with The Ur-Dragon if you have 2+ mana sources.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or cast a cheap ramp spell. Turn 2: Cast a 2-3 mana Dragon or another ramp piece. If you have 4 mana, cast a cheap Dragon to start triggering The Ur-Dragon. Prioritize fixing your mana base early to ensure you can cast 5-color spells by turn 4-5.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes, flying removal, and early interaction. Against control/combo: Bring in card draw, protection, and additional finishers. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, card advantage engines, and additional token doublers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana until you can cast a high-impact Dragon or a massive X-spell. Use card draw to maintain hand size, as casting multiple Dragons per turn is key. Manage your graveyard carefully; if you use exile effects, ensure you have recursion for key Dragons. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection or a clear win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have mana advantage or when the board is clear. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or targeted removal. Use flying and haste to bypass ground blockers. Prioritize casting Dragons that provide immediate value to maintain board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on The Ur-Dragon, and combo win conditions. Pay attention to opponents' mana bases; if they're holding up blue, assume counterspells. Track graveyard hate if you rely on recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The Ur-Dragon itself (once you have 5+ mana, cast it and follow up with 2+ Dragons for a lethal army), massive X-spells, or token doublers to swing combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 10+ mana, a clear path to combat, and no immediate sweepers in play. Commit when you can cast The Ur-Dragon and 2+ Dragons in a single turn, or when you have a guaranteed lethal with your current board state.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard-lock combo that you can't disrupt, if you're down to 1-2 lands with no acceleration, or if the board is completely wiped and you have no recursion or card draw to rebuild.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cantrips and cheap Dragons to trade up. Cast Dragons with haste or flying to bypass counters if possible. Use mana rocks to cast multiple spells in a turn, forcing counterspells to be spent on non-essential pieces. Consider bringing in protection or alternative win conditions.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with flying Dragons and removal. Don't let them get to their massive finishers. Use board wipes to reset their board. If they out-ramp you, focus on card advantage and recursion to stay in the game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and board wipes. Use flying Dragons to trade efficiently. Don't overextend; hold back mana for interaction. Once stabilized, pivot to your combo/finisher.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap Dragons and cantrips to out-value their removal. Use card draw to out-resource them. If they lock you out, use alternative win conditions or bring in additional finishers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win on card advantage and board presence. Prioritize drawing extra cards and protecting The Ur-Dragon. Use targeted removal to eliminate their key Dragons. If they go wide, use sweepers or targeted removal to clear the board before striking.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, increase card draw and protection. Against aggro, prioritize early removal and sweepers. In mirrors, focus on card advantage and targeted removal. Ensure your mana base remains consistent by keeping fetch lands and duals that fix to all five colors. Remove redundant acceleration if you're facing slower decks, and add more interaction if you're facing disruptive strategies.
notes: The Ur-Dragon's ability triggers on casting, not entering, so you can cast Dragons from exile or the graveyard if you have recursion. Avoid casting Dragons with X in your mana pool unless you're ready to commit; you can't change X after casting. Flying and haste make your tokens highly evasive, but be wary of board wipes. Always track opponents' mana to anticipate sweepers or counterspells. Common mistake: overcommitting to the board without protection or a clear win condition, leading to a wiped board and no comeback.

philosophy: This archetype is a fast mana and ramp strategy that leverages Witherbloom, the Balancer as both a flexible mana source and a late-game pump engine. It aims to outpace opponents by accelerating into massive threats or pumping a single creature to lethal, thriving against slower control and combo decks while struggling against hyper-aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands plus at least one mana dweeb (Llanowar Elves/Elvish Mystic) or a turn-2 play. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no ramp, or 1 land with any other card. Prioritize hands that can play a land and a dweeb on turn 1, or a land and Witherbloom on turn 2/3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, tap Llanowar/Elvish for green. Turn 2: Play another land, tap dweeb + land for {G}, cast another dweeb or low-cost ramp spell. If opening with Witherbloom, play land, tap for {G}, and cast it on turn 2 if possible, or hold for turn 3. Always prioritize untapped lands to enable Witherbloom's {T} ability for flexible mana or pump.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in efficient removal (e.g., Go for the Throat, Nature's Claim) and early blockers. Against control/combo: Bring in card draw, protection spells, and faster finishers. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and graveyard hate. Keep core ramp engine intact in all matchups.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always maintain at least 3-4 available for Witherbloom's pump or responses. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection. Use graveyard strategically if included, but track opponent interaction to avoid dead cards.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a way to protect them or when the board is clear of sweepers. Hold back if opponents have removal or board wipes. Witherbloom's 4/4 body allows it to trade, but saving it for a pump finish is often superior.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (board wipes), targeted removal (Duress, Fatal Push), and combo pieces. Note if opponents are tapping out for counters or protection. Track graveyard activity if playing against graveyard decks, and monitor their mana development to time your attacks.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Witherbloom pumped to lethal (often 10+ power), or a separate massive creature accelerated via ramp. Use pump spells or Witherbloom's ability to close out games. Combine with direct damage or combat tricks if available.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough mana to pump Witherbloom to lethal, or when you've established board dominance and opponents lack sweepers. Also commit when you can protect your pump target or when the opponent is at low life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control/combo deck with no interaction, or if you're down to a dead hand against aggro with no removal/ramp. Also concede if opponents have multiple sweepers and you cannot establish a board or close the game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (e.g., mana dweebs, instant-speed ramp). Save Witherbloom for when counters are on cooldown. Use protection or pump in response to counters, and attack with multiple threats to force counter trade-offs.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on their key ramp creatures. Out-value them with efficient threats and card advantage. Don't let them stabilize; keep the board state unfavorable.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and board wipes. Mulligan aggressively for interaction. Use Witherbloom as a blocker or pump a smaller creature to trade favorably. Stabilize before going for the win, and don't overextend into combat traps.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with efficient threats. Don't overextend into sweepers. Use Witherbloom's pump to break through blockers. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than their win conditions, and attack with multiple creatures to pressure their life total.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and efficient removal. Control the board with sweepers if available. Use Witherbloom's pump to out-value their creatures. Track their mana to know when they can pump, and don't overcommit to a board they can wipe.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate while preserving your core ramp engine. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and faster interaction. Against control, bring in protection and card advantage. Against aggro, bring in more removal and early blockers. Always keep at least 2-3 lands and your primary ramp creatures in the main deck.
notes: Common mistake: Tapping Witherbloom for mana when you should be saving it for a pump, or overcommitting to the board without protection. Unique interaction: Witherbloom's pump scales with mana spent to cast it, so casting it for its converted mana cost or using it as a pump source is key. Always balance its dual role as a mana source and finisher based on game state.

philosophy: Dina is a midrange-combo hybrid that sacrifices creatures to generate escalating Spirit tokens from the graveyard, winning through combat damage or synergistic damage outlets like Zulaport Cutthroat. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that struggle to answer multiple large threats or manage sacrifice synergies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one Forest or dual land that enters untapped), Sakura-Tribe Elder, and Dina or a sacrifice outlet. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no ramp/sacrifice outlets, or too many high-cost creatures without Dina.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sakura-Tribe Elder (or another ramp creature). Turn 2: Play a second land, search for a basic Forest with Elder, cast Dina or a second sacrifice outlet. Sequence ramp into Dina as fast as possible to start generating tokens.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Loafing Pest): Bring in board wipes, efficient removal (Fatal Push, Go for the Throat), and artifact hate. vs Control/Combo (Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in graveyard hate (Leyline of the Void, Rest in Peace), additional removal, and threat protection. vs Mirror: Bring in extra sacrifice outlets and hand disruption to slow their recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping Dina and key sacrifice outlets in play. Use Sakura-Tribe Elder early to smooth mana. Don't overcommit to the graveyard if graveyard hate is present; keep a hand of threats. Manage token production to avoid flooding your board.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Dina and early sacrifice outlets to establish a board presence. Hold back on sacrificing your entire hand if you lack answers to opponent's removal. Use tokens to trade efficiently against smaller threats and preserve your key pieces.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for graveyard hate (Leyline, Rest in Peace, Blood Moon), board wipes, and targeted removal on Dina. Note if opponents are playing discard or hand disruption, as Dina decks rely on card advantage from graveyard recursion and consistent land drops.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Dina with 5+ creatures in the graveyard creates 5/5 Spirits. Pair with Zulaport Cutthroat and sacrifice outlets for lethal poison/damage. Alternatively, attack with a wall of 4/4+ Spirits to close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Dina, a reliable sacrifice outlet, and 3+ creatures in the graveyard. If the opponent has no graveyard hate or board wipes, commit aggressively to overwhelm their defenses.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing heavy graveyard hate with no way to bypass it, if you're locked out by a control deck with no win conditions, or if you're down to a dead hand with no Dina/sacrifice outlets and the opponent is ahead on board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats (Sakura-Tribe Elder, Dina) that are hard to counter effectively. Employ discard or hand disruption if available. Sequence around counters by playing around their removal window and committing multiple threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with sacrifice outlets and Zulaport. Use removal to stop their key ramp creatures. Don't overextend into their finishers; stabilize first and punish their creature-heavy curve with Zulaport.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize board wipes, efficient removal, and early Dina to stabilize. Use Zulaport to punish their aggressive creature curve. Hold up removal for their key threats and trade tokens efficiently to survive the early game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early sacrifice outlets and Dina. Don't give them time to set up; win before they can answer your graveyard strategy. Use graveyard recursion to out-value them and force them to spend resources on removal instead of their own game plan.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-graveyarding them. Prioritize keeping Dina and sacrifice outlets. Use discard or hand disruption to slow their recursion. Trade tokens efficiently and aim for lethal faster by managing your graveyard count carefully.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control/combo, prioritize graveyard hate and additional removal. Against aggro, bring in board wipes and early interaction. In mirrors, focus on hand disruption and extra sacrifice outlets. Always keep at least one copy of Dina and Sakura-Tribe Elder in your main deck if possible, or ensure your sideboard has a clear plan to rebuild the game plan.
notes: Dina decks are highly sensitive to graveyard hate; always have a plan B (like direct damage or combat wins). Avoid sacrificing your entire hand before Dina is on the board. Zulaport Cutthroat shines when paired with multiple sacrifice outlets, so sequence sacrifices carefully. Watch for Blood Moon or similar mana disruption, as Dina requires specific colors. Practice token math to maximize Dina's power and toughness scaling.

philosophy: This deck aims to win by explosively chaining legendary creatures onto the battlefield using Kaalia's enter-the-battlefield ability, bypassing mana costs and tempo disadvantages to overwhelm opponents with high-impact threats. It excels against slower, resource-heavy strategies that cannot answer multiple simultaneous legendary threats or recover from sudden board swings.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one ramp artifact (Sol Ring/Arcane Signet), and Kaalia or a cheap legendary creature. Mulligan if you have no lands, no ramp, or only expensive spells with no viable path to casting Kaalia by turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or play a cheap mana-producing land. Turn 2: Cast Kaalia if you have 3 mana, or continue ramping. If you have a legendary creature in hand, cast Kaalia, let it resolve, and use its ETB to put the legendary onto the battlefield tapped and attacking to apply immediate pressure or trigger ETB/attack abilities.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in targeted removal, life gain, and a board wipe. vs Control: Bring in protection spells, cheap legendary threats, and card draw. vs Combo: Bring in hatebears, discard, and fast win conditions. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, resilient legendary creatures, and a board wipe.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoover mana for chain reactions rather than spending it on single threats. Keep key legendary creatures in hand for Kaalia's ETB, and use the graveyard strategically for recursion or reanimation only if the deck supports it. Prioritize keeping open mana to respond to interaction or chain additional legendaries.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection to answer their interaction. Hold back if you're vulnerable to board wipes or targeted removal, and use Kaalia's ETB to force favorable trades or bypass timing restrictions. Never overextend without a plan to protect your board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for removal spells aimed at Kaalia or your key legendary creatures. Track opponent's mana to time your chain reactions and avoid casting into counterspells or sweepers. Note when they use board wipes, as you'll need to rebuild your legendary presence quickly with cheap threats or recursion.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High-impact legendary creatures like Teferi, Time Raveler, Thassa, God-Eternal Oketra, or Teysa, Enviro that can close the game or lock opponents out. Chain them via Kaalia's ETB or cast them directly when you have open mana and protection.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, removal, or a board state that can withstand their interaction. Commit when you can chain multiple legendaries in one turn to overwhelm their defenses or when you have enough resources to rebuild after a board wipe.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of mana, have no legendary creatures in hand or library, or face a hard counter to your strategy (e.g., mass counterspells or board wipes with no recovery). Concede if you have no way to bypass their interaction or stabilize against an insurmountable board state.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast Kaalia at instant speed when they have no mana, or use it to chain threats that resolve simultaneously. Include protection spells or cheap threats that are hard to counter, and time your plays to avoid their main phase counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to win before they stabilize. Use targeted removal on their mana sources or key ramp creatures. Chain legendaries quickly to close the game before they can deploy their late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, life gain, or board wipes. Use Kaalia's ETB to put a large legendary creature onto the battlefield to trade with their attackers. Win quickly before they overwhelm you with unblockable or numerous threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap legendary creatures and protection spells. Out-value them with card draw and chain reactions. Use Kaalia's ETB to bypass their counterspells by threatening multiple targets or casting at instant speed when they have no mana.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing with better legendary creatures, more removal, or faster chain reactions. Prioritize cards that can answer their legendaries or protect yours. Use board wipes strategically to reset the board in your favor and capitalize on your remaining resources.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your deck to the metagame by adding more interaction against control and combo, more resilience against aggro, and more value in mirror matches. Replace redundant cards with situational answers, ensure you have enough mana sources to support your sideboard additions, and adjust your curve to maintain early-game pressure while preserving late-game finishers.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to a single legendary without answers, leaving you vulnerable to removal or board wipes. Unique interaction: Kaalia's ETB puts the creature onto the battlefield tapped and attacking, which can trigger 'when this creature attacks' effects immediately. Also, you can chain multiple legendaries in one turn if you have enough mana and targets, creating a snowball effect that's hard to answer. Always keep a legendary creature in hand when you cast Kaalia to maximize value.

philosophy: A BRG midrange-graveyard hybrid that uses land acceleration and resilient threats to out-value opponents, leveraging graveyard recursion and spot removal to stabilize early and close out games with unblockable or hard-to-answer threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one untapped green source for Farseek), a 1-2 mana curve, and at least one early interaction or ramp spell. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, 1-2 lands with no ramp/interaction, or hands lacking a turn-1 or turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a 1-mana cantrip or early threat. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Farseek if you have an untapped green land, or play a 2-drop threat. Prioritize untapped lands for Farseek sequencing, and use early spells to dig for key pieces or apply initial pressure.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro (Krenko, Ur-Dragon): +2 early spot removal, +1 graveyard hate, -2 Farseek/land search. Against Control: +2 artifact/enchantment removal, +1 graveyard hate, -1 Farseek. Against Combo: +2 graveyard hate, +1 artifact hate, -2 ramp. Against Mirror: +1 targeted removal, +1 graveyard recursion hate, -1 land search.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for Farseek, removal, and threats. Avoid overcommitting to graveyard recursion if the opponent has hate. Maintain hand size with cantrips and only cast Farseek when you have a clear untapped green land and a target in the library.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Trade efficiently and hold back large threats until you have removal or a board clear. Use Braids, Arisen Nightmare to recur key creatures or answer threats, but be mindful of its ETB effect and graveyard vulnerability. Prioritize clearing sweepers and graveyard hate before committing to an all-in strategy.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, graveyard hate, and combo pieces. Note if the opponent is playing land destruction, counters, or heavy removal. Track their graveyard size and hand size to anticipate Braids' ETB impact and recursion potential.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Large resilient creatures, Braids recursion loops, or land-based value engines. Win by applying consistent pressure, out-valuing the opponent in trades, and leveraging graveyard recursion to bypass hand disruption.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, enough mana for a turn-two response, and the opponent has no sweepers or graveyard hate. Commit when you can safely cast Farseek and develop a board that is difficult to remove.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when the opponent has a hard lock (sweepers + graveyard hate + no draw spells), you are down to 1 life with no recovery tools, or you are consistently drawing dead hands with no interaction or threats.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, split spells if applicable, and cast spells in response to their counters. Rely on land acceleration to out-value and win the resource war. Avoid overcommitting to expensive spells without protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use removal on key ramp sources. Don't let them stabilize; force trades and disrupt their mana development. Use Farseek to keep pace while applying pressure.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early spot removal and hold up mana for interaction. Stabilize with board wipes if available, then transition to midrange value. Use Braids to recur threats and apply pressure while controlling the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure and use graveyard recursion to bypass hand disruption. Win the resource war by out-valuing them in trades and leveraging Farseek to maintain mana advantage. Force them to use removal on multiple threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through value trades, graveyard hate, and targeted removal. Out-value in the late game by leveraging recursion and land acceleration. Prioritize clearing their graveyard and disrupting their Farseek targets.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure ramp to interactive midrange. Prioritize graveyard hate against recursion/graveyard decks, artifact/enchantment removal against control, and early interaction against aggro. Adjust land count if needed, and ensure you have enough removal to handle the metagame's top archetypes.
notes: Farseek requires an untapped green land; plan sequencing carefully to avoid mana screw. Braids' graveyard recursion is powerful but vulnerable to hate; keep a backup plan. Avoid overcommitting to the graveyard if the meta is heavy on hate. Watch for sweepers in control mirrors and adjust your threat curve accordingly.

philosophy: Lorehold is a spell-slinging engine that converts instant and sorcery density into Treasure tokens and mana acceleration. It aims to out-value opponents by casting a high volume of cheap spells, leveraging Treasure generation for card advantage, mana flexibility, and late-game finishers, while controlling the board with efficient R/W removal and counters.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably artifact lands like Lorehold Campus or mana rocks), Sol Ring/Mana Crypt, and at least one cheap instant/sorcery. Mulligan if you have 4+ lands with no ramp/spells, or 3+ spells with no lands/mana acceleration. Prioritize hands that can cast a spell on turn 2 to trigger Lorehold's Treasure generation.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or another mana rock. Turn 2: Cast a cheap spell to trigger Lorehold's Treasure generation, or play a Campus land. Sequence mana rocks first to enable early spell casting. If you have a Campus, consider tapping it for mana on turn 2 if you need it for a key spell, but remember sacrificing it is part of the deck's engine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko): Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Cataclysm), efficient removal (Path to Exile, Anger of the Gods), and artifact hate. Against control/combo: Bring in more counterspells (Negate, Spell Pierce), card draw, and direct damage. Against Ur-Dragon/big combos: Bring in targeted removal, artifact destruction, and early interaction. Mirror: Bring in more counters, targeted removal, and Treasure tax effects.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance land drops with Lorehold's land-sacrifice mechanic. Don't sacrifice lands you need for mana acceleration unless you're casting a high-impact spell. Use Treasures for mana when you're mana-screwed, or for specific effects like casting more spells or activating abilities. Keep hand size high by using Treasures for card draw or spell duplication.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction ready. Use Lorehold's Treasure generation to fuel multiple spells per turn. Hold back if you're low on removal and facing a board-stomper. Prioritize clearing key permanents over casting filler spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana base and hand size. If they're holding up blue mana, assume counterspells. If they're tapping out for a big creature, have removal ready. Track graveyard hate if they're playing reanimation. Note when they start sacrificing lands for mana, as they might be playing a similar engine or ramp.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Hit the Mother Lode combined with a high-impact instant/sorcery like Lightning Helix, Swords to Plowshares, or a board wipe. Treasure token chains can also fuel a win condition like a big artifact or direct damage spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ Treasures, a cleared board, or when the opponent is at lethal range. Commit when you can cast multiple spells in response to their interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out board (e.g., Ur-Dragon with infinite mana, or Krenko with untapped goblins and no wipe), have no interaction in hand, and are below 10 life. Also concede if you're out of cards and the opponent has a guaranteed win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats if available, or overpay with Treasures to cast spells. Use instant-speed interaction to clear their counterspellers. Consider spell-skew or redirect effects to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap spells and Treasures. Use artifact destruction and land destruction to slow them down. Don't trade resources; focus on board wipes and targeted removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with efficient removal and board wipes. Use Treasures to cast multiple spells per turn to outpace their damage. Don't overcommit to a mana sink; prioritize survival.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap, resilient threats. Use Treasures to cast spells around their removal. Force them to use resources on low-impact spells, then close the game with a finisher.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better resource management and more efficient spell sequencing. Use counterspells to protect your key spells. Prioritize clearing their Lorehold and generating more Treasures than they can.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a pure spell-slinging engine to a more interactive midrange/control shell. Add more targeted removal, counterspells, and board wipes. Reduce land count slightly if bringing in more non-land ramp. Adjust based on specific metagame trends (e.g., more artifact hate against Blech, more counters against Prismari/Y'shtola).
notes: Common mistake: Sacrificing too many lands too early, leaving you mana-flooded or unable to cast spells. Always calculate if the Treasure generation is worth the land loss. Synergize Treasures with cards that care about artifacts or treasure tokens. Watch out for land destruction in a deck that relies on land sacrifice. Prioritize casting spells over hoarding Treasures unless a specific finisher requires them.

philosophy: Cast cheap non-creature spells to generate haste Goblin tokens, then swing them all at a single target for lethal. The deck wins by overwhelming opponents with unblockable combat damage while leveraging sacrifice outlets and card draw to out-resource the field.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Zada, and at least 2-3 cheap non-creature spells (rituals, cantrips, or direct damage). Mulligan if you lack early land drops, hold only high-cost spells without protection, or have creatures but no Zada or sacrifice outlets.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or ritual to draw into threats or generate mana. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a non-creature spell to trigger Zada if it's on the board, or play a Goblin token generator. Prioritize establishing Zada by turn 2-3 with protection (Lightning Greaves, Swiftfoot Boots, or hexproof/gain tactics).
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Bring in targeted removal and protection vs. control/combo (e.g., Dismember, Lightning Greaves). Add early removal and life gain vs. aggro. In mirrors and against tribal decks like Krenko or Ur-Dragon, prioritize card draw and Zada protection. Keep Ancestors' Aid and Fists of Flame in all matchups for consistency.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for Zada triggers and combat. Use sacrifice outlets to fuel card draw and mana acceleration. Don't overcommit to board wipes unless necessary; Zada wins via combat, not attrition. Manage hand size to ensure you have answers or finishers when needed.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection for Zada and enough non-creature spells to flood the board. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal; use cantrips to dig for answers. Prioritize establishing a board of Goblins over casting expensive spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (sweepers will reset your engine), targeted removal on Zada (prioritize protection), and counterspells (have evasion or protection). Note opponent's mana base and hand size to predict sweepers or finishers. Track how many non-creature spells they've countered to adjust your casting order.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Fists of Flame combined with Zada's tokens, or swinging all Goblins at a single target. Use sacrifice outlets to cast additional spells for extra triggers before combat. Chain cantrips and rituals to maximize triggers in a single turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Zada protected, a board of Goblins, and enough mana to cast 2-3 non-creature spells in response to blockers or to trigger additional combat damage. Commit when the opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if Zada is permanently removed without protection, you're facing a locked board with no removal, or you're out of resources against a combo that wins on the spot. Concede if you're out of cards and the opponent has a clear path to victory.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use protection (hexproof, shroud, haste), cast spells in response to counterspells, or rely on uncounterable effects. Dig for Lightning Greaves or Swiftfoot Boots. Cast non-creature spells in a specific order to beat counterspells, or use sacrifice outlets to generate value even if countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal for key ramp pieces, and prioritize winning before they stabilize. Bring in Flame Rift or Goblin Bomb for direct damage. Disrupt their mana development with early threats and sacrifice outlets.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal, use Goblins to trade efficiently, and race with Fists of Flame or direct damage. Prioritize survival over combo. Use cantrips to dig for answers and keep your life total high enough to swing for lethal.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with card draw, use protection for Zada, and pressure with early tokens. Dig for Fists of Flame or Goblin Charbelcher for finishers. Force them to use resources on your board rather than your spells.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win via card advantage, protection for Zada, and better sacrifice outlets. Prioritize Ancestors' Aid and cantrips to out-draw. Control the board with early Goblins and use protection to outlast the opponent's removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your removal and protection based on the metagame. Against control/combo, bring in more counterspells or protection. Against aggro, add early removal and life gain. In mirrors, prioritize card draw and Zada protection. Always keep Fists of Flame and Ancestors' Aid for consistency. Adjust your land base if the format demands more or fewer basics.
notes: Zada's ability triggers on cast, not ETB, so sacrifice outlets are crucial for fueling the engine. Protect Zada at all costs; if it dies, the deck loses its primary win condition. Avoid overcommitting to board wipes unless you have a backup plan. Watch for opponent's hand size to predict sweepers or finishers. Remember that tokens created by Zada can be sacrificed to Ancestors' Aid or other outlets to draw cards or generate mana, creating a recursive engine.

philosophy: This deck leverages mana acceleration and card draw to out-resource opponents, transitioning from early tempo to late-game card advantage and combat dominance. It excels against slower, resource-starved decks and struggles against hyper-aggressive or combo strategies that can close the game before your engine stabilizes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2+ lands (including fetches/shocks if applicable) and at least one mana accelerator (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, etc.) or a turn-1/2 play that establishes board presence. Keep hands with Curiosity or Vivi Ornitier if supported by ramp or cantrips. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no acceleration, or 2+ lands with only removal/beatsticks and no draw engines or color fixers.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Curiosity or Vivi Ornitier. Sequence lands to ensure red/blue mana availability by turn 2-3. If opening with acceleration, prioritize casting draw engines to stabilize hand size and dig for answers. If opening with threats, hold up mana for interaction until you establish a baseline of card advantage.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Loafing Pest): +2 Lightning Strike, +2 Dispel, +1 Pyroclasm, -2 Curiosity. vs Control/Combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed): +2 Negate, +2 Spell Pierce, +1 Arcane Denial, -2 Sol Ring. vs Mirror: +2 Mana Leak, +2 Counterspell, +1 Thoughtseize, -1 Curiosity. vs Ramp (The Ur-Dragon): +2 Artifact Hate, +2 Engineered Plague, +1 Pyroclasm, -2 draw engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize maintaining card advantage over board presence early. Use Curiosity to filter into answers or threats rather than hoarding cards. Don't overextend into removal; keep a hand of 3+ cards when facing interactive decks. Manage mana for both casting and countering, and avoid tapping out on turn 3 unless you have a clear win condition or protection.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have card advantage or removal to protect them. Hold back Vivi Ornitier if the opponent has sweepers or targeted hate. Use countermagic to protect key pieces and disrupt opponent's combos. Trade efficiently: use removal on their most impactful threats and save your own creatures for late-game pressure.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Lightning Strike), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for counterspells or have graveyard hate. Track life totals and board state to determine when to pivot from control to aggression or vice versa.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Vivi Ornitier with combat tricks, infinite card draw loops, or overwhelming board presence from Curiosity. Secure victory by trading up in card advantage and closing with unblockable or protected threats. Use mana acceleration to cast multiple threats in a single turn if the board is clear.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, opponent has low life or a swept board, or you've established a clear win condition. Commit when you can protect your finisher or have enough answers to handle interaction. Don't commit if the opponent has unanswered sweepers or targeted hate.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked combo, have no interaction against a fast aggro deck, or are consistently one-shot by removal. Concede if you're out of resources and the opponent has a clear path to victory, or if your deck's win conditions are completely neutralized by hate cards.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats or split your casting into multiple turns. Employ cantrips to dig through counters. Use mana dorks to cast around counters. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than combo pieces.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use artifact hate, and disrupt their mana base. Don't trade cards; trade resources. Force them to use interaction on your threats rather than accelerating their game plan.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early interaction, stabilize with board wipes or removal, then transition to your game plan. Don't overcommit to draw engines before turn 4. Use countermagic to protect your engine and disrupt their early threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats, use cantrips to dig past counters, and win through card advantage. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than combo pieces. Don't let them stabilize; keep pressure on and trade up in card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through better card advantage, superior sequencing, and sideboard cards. Prioritize countermagic and removal to protect your engine. Force them to use resources on your threats rather than drawing into their win condition. Track their hand size and adjust your plays accordingly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards interaction against control/combo, and aggression against slow decks. Adjust land counts if needed, prioritizing basics and duals that support your mana base. Prioritize hate cards against top metagame archetypes, and don't be afraid to run fewer draw engines if the format is control-heavy.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting to Curiosity too early without protection or follow-up. Unique interaction: Vivi Ornitier likely synergizes with card draw; maximize triggers and use cantrips to dig into your engine. Watch for format-specific rules regarding Sol Ring (if banned/limited). Always track opponent's life total and board state to determine when to pivot from control to aggression or vice versa.

philosophy: A B/W attrition and conditional control deck that leverages Silverquill to disrupt opponent strategies while out-grinding them through efficient removal, card advantage, and incremental damage. It excels against combo and ramp archetypes by answering key pieces and winning midrange value exchanges.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics or signets), at least one mana rock (Sol Ring/Arcane Signet), and a mix of early interaction or a turn-2 threat. Mulligan if you have fewer than two lands, no mana acceleration, or a hand full of high-cost removal with no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or Arcane Signet if mana allows. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, cast early threats or targeted removal, and begin sequencing lands to ensure white and black mana for Silverquill’s abilities. Prioritize establishing board presence without overextending.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in lifegain, early removal, and board wipes; remove expensive late-game cards. Vs Control/Combo (Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in counterspells, hand disruption, and graveyard hate; remove redundant early threats. Vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal, hand disruption, and card advantage engines; remove generic removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conservatively manage mana to ensure you can respond to threats while developing your board. Use mana rocks to enable Silverquill’s abilities at optimal times. Avoid hoarding cards if you can safely apply pressure or stabilize.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have interaction to answer their response or when you can safely establish a board. Hold back if the opponent has unanswered removal, a combo piece, or a board wipe. Use Silverquill to force favorable trades or disrupt their strategy.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for combo win conditions (Ur-Dragon, Krenko), big planeswalkers, and board wipes. Note when opponents tap out for key spells or when they start drawing multiple cards. Track graveyard contents if recursion is a threat.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Silverquill’s ultimate ability, incremental damage via lifelink and removal, or card advantage engines that out-value the opponent. Secure the win by protecting your win condition and removing the last key piece of their strategy.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have board control, the opponent is at low life, or you can protect your win condition with interaction. Commit when you’ve answered their key threats and can safely attack or cast your finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face a hard lock with no interaction, are at 1-2 life with no removal or lifegain, or are facing a known unbeatable combo with no sideboard answers. Don’t overextend into a board wipe or counter suite.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use mana rocks to cast at instant speed, split your spells across multiple turns, or cast multiple threats to force counters. Use Silverquill to disrupt their counterspells or force them to spend resources on less important targets.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and interaction to disrupt their mana development. Use discard, targeted removal, or board wipes to stop them from reaching their big spells. Don’t let them stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize early with efficient removal and lifegain. Use board wipes to clear token armies or aggressive threats. Transition to value engines once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient threats and card advantage. Don’t let them out-value you; use Silverquill to disrupt their strategy and force them to spend resources. Attack when they’re tapped out for counters.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the attrition war by trading efficiently and targeting their key pieces. Use hand disruption to prevent them from setting up their strategy. Prioritize card advantage and protect Silverquill to out-value them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction and value ratio based on the metagame. Bring in more disruption vs combo, more removal vs aggro, and more card advantage vs control. Keep your core mana base and Silverquill, but prune redundant cards. Always have a plan for the top 3 archetypes.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board without protection or interaction. Unique interaction: Silverquill’s dispute mechanic likely forces choices or counters; use it to disrupt combo chains and force unfavorable trades. Watch for format-specific rules and adjust your pacing accordingly. Always track graveyard and library contents if recursion or mill is a threat.

philosophy: This deck generates a relentless stream of goblin tokens, sacrifices them for combat tricks and value, and swings for lethal using Edgar Markov as the primary finisher. It excels against control and combo decks that lack early interaction, while struggling against fast aggro or heavy board wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetch/shock or non-rotating duals), Sol Ring or other mana acceleration, and at least one early goblin or token generator. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no acceleration or early threats, or if you lack a way to cast Edgar Markov by turn 4-5.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or a cheap goblin (e.g., Goblin Piker, Goblin Ringleader). Turn 2: Ramp into a token generator or combat trick. Land sequencing: Prioritize non-basic lands that enter untapped or can fetch basics, ensuring you can cast your turn-2/3 plays. If you have a shockland, play it tapped if you can pay 2 life, otherwise fetch.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in Swords to Plowshares, pathfinders, or cheap removal; take out redundant ramp. vs Control/Combo: Bring in more combat tricks, card draw, and protection; take out aggressive goblins. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and board wipes; keep synergy pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Don't overcommit mana to token generation if you can't protect them. Use graveyard for sacrifice outlets or recursion. Keep hand size above 3 when facing sweepers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or combat tricks to protect them. Hold back if the board is clear and you can stabilize with a sweeper or counter.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal on Edgar, and opponent's mana base. Key tell: Opponent holding up mana on their end step often means a counter or removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Edgar Markov with combat tricks, sacrifice outlets for extra damage, or a massive goblin swarm. Get there by establishing board presence, protecting Edgar, and trading efficiently.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, opponent has no removal, or you can swing past blockers with tricks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, opponent has a guaranteed win condition (e.g., infinite combo, unstoppable commander), or you're completely locked out of the game.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, redundant threats. Cast Edgar Markov in response to a counter if possible, or use it as a sacrifice outlet. Rely on non-creature spells or sacrifice outlets that don't target.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on key ramp creatures. Don't trade tokens for their mana dorks.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with Swords to Plowshares, pathfinders, or cheap removal. Don't overextend into combat. Use Edgar Markov as a blocker or trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure the board with tokens and combat tricks. Force them to use removal on low-value targets. Win through attrition and card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades are key. Use better sacrifice outlets, combat tricks, and targeted removal. Prioritize Edgar Markov survival.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on matchup. Against slower decks, add more late-game finishers and card draw. Against faster decks, add early interaction and protection. Always ensure your mana base can handle the expected spell density.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to token generation without a plan to protect Edgar Markov. Unique interaction: Swords to Plowshares creates a Spirit token that can be sacrificed for value or used as a blocker. Always track opponent's graveyard for recursion threats.

philosophy: This deck aims to generate an overwhelming army of 1/1 Human tokens by repeatedly casting spells each turn, leveraging Ashling, the Limitless and Risen Reef as core engines. It wins by out-resourcing opponents through spell density and token multiplication, overwhelming both aggressive and control strategies with unstoppable board presence.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped or fetchable basics), at least one mana rock (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, etc.), and a 1-2 mana cantrip or creature. Mulligan any hand with zero lands, only high-cost spells, or no early interaction/spell generation tools.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or a cheap mana artifact. If possible, cast a 1-mana cantrip or creature to start the 'second spell' trigger. Turn 2: Ramp into dual lands or fetch basics, cast a second spell to trigger Ashling/Risen Reef, or play Ashling directly if mana allows. Prioritize untapped land drops to enable multi-casting.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation) and efficient removal. Against control: Bring in extra cantrips, card draw, and direct damage. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, artifact/enchantment removal, and additional board wipes. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and additional protection for your key creatures.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for multi-casting; avoid overcommitting to one big spell if you can cast two smaller ones to trigger token generation. Manage hand size by cycling through cantrips. Use the graveyard strategically but avoid relying on it since the deck is spell-centric.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have untapped mana for at least 3 spells in a turn. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or if you're low on resources. Prioritize protecting Ashling and Risen Reef with counterspells or protection spells.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal on your key creatures, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells, as you can capitalize on their vulnerability.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ashling, the Limitless (entering triggers + second spell triggers), Risen Reef, and other token generators. Win by casting multiple spells in a single turn to flood the board, then attacking with the token army.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have untapped mana for at least 3-4 spells in a turn, or when opponents have no sweepers in hand. Commit when you can protect your board or have a backup plan if wiped.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard-lock combo, have no lands/spells in hand, or are facing a board wipe with no recovery tools. Concede if you're down to one life source and opponents have multiple sweepers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, redundant spells to force counterspells. Employ mana rocks to cast around counters. Use instant-speed spells to protect your key creatures.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with tokens. Use removal on their mana sources. Don't let them stabilize; keep casting spells to overwhelm their ramp strategy.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Hold up removal/wipes for turn 3-4. Use your early tokens to trade efficiently. Stabilize with board wipes, then transition to your own win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-grind them with card advantage and multi-casting. Use cantrips to dig for answers. Attack with tokens to force them to spend resources on removal instead of their win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having better card advantage, more efficient mana, or additional protection for your key creatures. Prioritize removing their Ashling/Risen Reef and controlling the board with targeted removal.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. If aggro is prevalent, increase board wipes and early interaction. If control/combo is up, add more card draw, protection, and hate cards. Always keep at least one copy of Ashling and Risen Reef, and adjust your mana base for consistency against the current field.
notes: Remember that Ashling's second-spell trigger only counts spells you cast, not tokens or triggered abilities. Risen Reef triggers on every spell you cast, so stacking cheap cantrips is key. Avoid overcommitting to one big spell; the deck wins through volume. Watch for 'summoning sickness' on your tokens—they can attack immediately if they enter on your turn, but be mindful of combat math. In Commander, always be aware of the 'conspiracy' rule regarding sideboards; if playing in a sanctioned environment, use a sideboard from your command zone or adjust your main deck pre-game accordingly.

philosophy: Mono-white voltron-token engine that wins by attacking with Giada to generate a resilient army, then leveraging her tap ability to pump her for lethal or using tokens to control the board and out-value opponents. Excels in midrange and control matchups where it can stabilize and snowball, but must race fast aggro and artifact/ramp decks.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably fetchable or ramp-able), Sol Ring or other mana acceleration, and either Giada or a protective/early threat. Keep hands with removal or bounce if facing aggro/control. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no ramp, or only removal with no threats/land.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, drop Sol Ring or other ramp. Turn 2: Cast Giada if possible, or play a cheap land/ramp to set up for Turn 3 Giada. If you can't cast Giada early, use ramp to accelerate and play cheap white creatures or equipment to stabilize. Prioritize playing lands to ensure you can cast Giada by Turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Loafing Pest): Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), bounce/removal (Swords to Plowshares, Path to Exile), and lifegain. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed): Bring in card draw (Jeska's Will, Arcane Signet ramp), protection (Lightning Helix, Anguished Unmaking), and additional pump sources. Against mirror/ramp (Blech, Ur-Dragon): Bring in artifact hate (Disenchant, Abrade), additional removal, and faster finishers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana should be hoarded for Giada's pump ability or key removal. Don't overcommit to attacking if you can't pump Giada or block effectively. Use tokens as a resource: tap them to pump Giada, or leave them untapped to block/attack. Graveyard is generally less relevant unless using bounce or recursion; prioritize hand and board resources.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear path to lethal or when you can stabilize against an aggressive board. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or can easily remove Giada. Use tokens to trade favorably and preserve Giada's life total.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Krenko's tokens are vulnerable), artifact acceleration (Blech), and combo win conditions. Key tells: Opponent tapping out on non-creature spells often signals a sweeper or combo piece. Opponent keeping mana open on their turn end suggests a counter or instant-speed interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Giada with a full board of tapped tokens for massive +1/+1 counters, or a fully equipped Giada. The goal is to swing with Giada and enough tokens to either go lethal or leave the opponent at 0 life after combat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough tapped creatures to pump Giada for lethal, or when the opponent is low enough that a single combat step will win. Also commit when you can safely ignore their removal (e.g., they have no instant-speed interaction).
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted, if you're down to one life and the opponent has a board wipe ready, or if you're consistently drawing dead hands with no lands or answers. Also concede if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., Ur-Dragon with infinite mana) and you lack removal.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use mana dorks/ramp to cast Giada on turn 2 or 3 before they can comfortably counter. Use cheap, efficient threats or equipment to pressure. If they counter, use bounce or protection to recast.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with tokens and Giada. Use artifact hate and removal to slow their acceleration. Don't let them get too far ahead; trade efficiently and use lifegain to outlast them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal, bounce, and board wipes. Use tokens to block and trade favorably. Prioritize keeping Giada alive and use her lifelink to stabilize life total.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with consistent attacks and token generation. Use card draw to out-value them. Force them to use removal on Giada, then swing with tokens. If they sweep, rebuild quickly with ramp and cheap threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades carefully. Use your tokens to out-value theirs. Prioritize keeping Giada alive. Use removal on their Giada or key pump sources. The deck with better card advantage and removal wins.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against token-heavy formats, prioritize board wipes and targeted removal. Against control/combo, add disruption and card draw. In mirrors, bring in additional removal and protection. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't compromise your mana base or early game consistency.
notes: Common mistake: Attacking with Giada without tapping creatures to pump her, leaving her vulnerable to removal or unable to close the game. Always calculate pump math before committing. Another mistake: Overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers. Use tokens as a buffer, not just for damage. Giada's lifelink is crucial for stabilizing against aggressive decks, so don't neglect blocking. Equipment synergizes well but isn't strictly necessary; focus on the tap-pump engine.

philosophy: A B/W token commander that generates an army of Soldiers and buffs them to overwhelm opponents. It aims to stabilize early with ramp and removal, then transition into a relentless midgame where commander tax reduction allows for repeated lethal swings or efficient value trades.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one that taps for W or B), Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, and Killian or a cheap early threat/removal. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no ramp or Killian, or if you lack a W mana source with only 1 land. Hands with 2 lands, a rock, and Killian are keepers.
