opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or Arcane Signet. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Killian if you have W mana, or play a cheap token producer/removal. If you can't cast Killian T2, use signet to cast it T3. Sequence lands to ensure W on T2 if possible. Tap Killian for a token on T3, then attack or hold for removal protection.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Y'shtola): +1 to 2, +Heroic Intervention, +Swords to Plowshares, -ramp. Against control/combo (Prismari, Ur-Dragon): +Dispel/Counterspell, +Decree of Justice, -ramp. Against mirror/token: +Board wipes, +Targeted removal, -token doublers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for removal on key threats. Don't overcommit to the board if the opponent has sweepers. Use commander tax reduction to cast Killian repeatedly or cast other commanders for value. Manage graveyard by avoiding unnecessary self-mill or sacrificing creatures to opponent effects.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready for their answer or when they've blown interaction. Hold back if they have sweepers or hard removal. Use tokens to trade efficiently, saving Killian for late-game value or as a finisher.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (Decree of Justice, Wrath of God), hard removal (Beast Within, Swords to Plowshares), and combo pieces. Note if they're playing token doublers or commander tax reduction. Track their life total and board state to gauge when to swing.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lethal swings with buffed Soldiers, or using commander tax reduction to cast Killian + other commanders for massive value. Sometimes a big buff spell or equipment seals the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have board presence, removal for their sweepers, and they're low on life. When they've blown removal on one threat. When you can swing for 5+ damage with buffed tokens.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one life, opponent has a board wipe ready, you have no removal, and your hand is empty. If you're locked out of mana or key pieces. If the opponent has a combo that can't be interrupted.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats. Cast Killian on opponent's turn if possible. Use mana rocks to cast spells when they can't respond. Don't overcommit to expensive spells. Have a backup plan if your commander is countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure. Don't let them stabilize. Use removal on their mana dorks. Have a board wipe ready. Force them to trade resources instead of drawing cards.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and early blockers. Don't trade tokens for their creatures. Use commander tax reduction to cast Killian for value. Play defensively until you can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats. Don't let them draw cards. Use commander tax reduction to cast multiple commanders. Have a win condition that's hard to answer. Force them to use removal on your threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades. Use commander tax reduction to cast Killian more times. Have better removal/board wipes. Track their commander tax. Win by out-resourcing them or finding a unique win condition.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against token-heavy meta, bring in board wipes and targeted removal. Against control/combo, bring in counterspells and artifact removal. Always keep a balance of ramp and interaction. Consider bringing in commander tax reduction if the meta is heavy on commanders.
notes: Commander tax is a major factor; include ways to reduce it (Crown of Convergence, Commander's Sphere, etc.). Killian's ability is tap, so you can't attack and generate tokens on the same turn unless you have haste or double strike. Watch out for 'cantrip' effects that let you dig for answers. Common mistake: Overcommitting to board without removal for sweepers. Always track opponent's life total and board state to know when to swing.

philosophy: Vnwxt, Verbose Host is a spell-copy engine that transforms every spell you cast into a double threat, aiming to overwhelm opponents through exponential value generation and precise stack manipulation. It excels against control and combo decks that rely on single-target answers or linear game plans, as it can easily out-value and disrupt their key interactions.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Vnwxt, and at least two 1-2 mana spells (cantrips, cheap counters, or mana sources). Prioritize hands containing Arcane Denial or other flash interaction for early protection. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no spells, only high-cost spells with no early interaction, or no way to cast Vnwxt by turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Cast a 1-mana cantrip or mana accelerator to set up the board. Turn 3: Drop Vnwxt and cast a cheap spell to trigger the copy engine. If opening with interaction, hold up mana for Arcane Denial flash. Land sequencing: Prioritize untapped blue sources early to ensure you can cast Vnwxt and a follow-up spell on turn 3 without stalling.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in bounce spells, additional cheap counters, and early removal. Against control: Bring in card draw, indirect damage, and evasion to bypass board wipes. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional counterspells, and hand disruption. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, spell recursion, and card advantage engines to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until Vnwxt is on the board. Use cheap spells to fuel the copy engine without overextending. Manage your graveyard carefully since Arcane Denial lives there; avoid unnecessary discard or self-mill unless it synergizes with your win condition.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have the copy engine active or sufficient interaction to handle removal. Hold back on casting expensive spells if you lack protection, as the copy will also be vulnerable. Prioritize copying removal or counterspells over raw threats early to maintain board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and counterspells. Note when opponents hold up mana (likely for interaction) and adjust spell timing accordingly. Track graveyard contents for Arcane Denial or other flashback threats, and monitor their hand size to gauge their ability to respond to your key spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Copy a board wipe, a massive draw spell, or a direct damage spell to close out the game. Use Vnwxt to copy a win condition like a large creature or a game-ending enchantment. Arcane Denial can protect your finisher from a last-ditch counter, ensuring your game plan resolves.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Vnwxt on the board, a full hand of spells, and sufficient interaction to handle one answer. Commit when you can copy a spell that creates a board state impossible to recover from, or when you can force the opponent to use multiple answers for a single spell.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are locked out of lands, facing a degenerate combo that resolves past your interaction, or if your hand is entirely high-cost spells with no way to cast them before lethal. Also concede if the opponent has established an unbreakable board and you lack removal or a viable win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash, split second, or layered spells to overcome counters. Copy Arcane Denial to counter their counterspells. Cast spells in response to their counterspells to maximize value and force them to spend multiple resources on a single interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap spells and Vnwxt. Use counterspells to stop their mana rocks or finishers. Do not let them stabilize; copy their ramp spells if possible to disrupt their plan and maintain tempo.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize bounce spells, counters, and early removal. Use Vnwxt to copy removal to clear multiple threats efficiently. Hold up interaction until they commit to an attack, and use cheap cantrips to dig for answers while stabilizing the board.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and spell density. Copy their removal to clear your threats and force them to spend multiple answers. Use indirect damage or evasion to bypass board wipes, and pressure them to make mistakes by threatening multiple lines of play simultaneously.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card draw and spell recursion. Use targeted removal to eliminate their Vnwxt before they can copy key spells. Control the stack by holding up interaction until they commit to a key spell, and leverage your graveyard resources to outlast them.

sideboard_advice: After sideboarding, shift from a pure spell-copy engine to a more tailored plan. Against control, prioritize card draw and finishers to out-grind them. Against aggro, increase early interaction and bounce effects to survive the early game. Against combo, add graveyard hate and additional counters to disrupt their game plan. Always ensure your mana base can support the new sideboard cards, and adjust your mulligan strategy to prioritize the new interaction or finishers.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to casting spells without considering that the copies will also be vulnerable to removal. Always calculate if you can afford to lose both the original and the copy. Unique interaction: Arcane Denial's flashback allows you to cast it from the graveyard, making it a free interaction if you can get it there. Vnwxt copies resolve on the stack, so you can respond to the copy with your own spells. Avoid casting spells you can't afford to lose, and always keep an eye on the stack to maximize copy value.

philosophy: This deck leverages early mana acceleration and artifact synergy to out-value opponents through resilient threats and incremental board advantage, aiming to win against control and combo by establishing an unbreakable board state while outpacing aggro with efficient removal and clock pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with at least two lands and one of Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or a turn-1 playable creature/spell. Prioritize hands with 2-3 lands and two mana rocks or ramp spells. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or 3+ lands with no playable turn-1 or turn-2 spells.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring or Arcane Signet if possible. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Signet or ramp into Toph or a key artifact/creature. If opening with Toph, ensure you have at least two lands and a cantrip or protection spell to protect it. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana availability for Toph's abilities or key removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Krenko/aggro: Bring in board wipes, targeted removal, and artifact hate. Against Ur-Dragon/control: Bring in card draw, artifact destruction, and direct damage. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and life gain. Against mirror: Bring in additional value engines and targeted removal for key artifacts.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize maintaining open mana for interaction and Toph's abilities. Don't overcommit to the board if you lack answers. Use mana rocks to fuel late-game spells and manage hand size by trading resources for card advantage when behind.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or a board wipe ready. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or if you're vulnerable to targeted removal. Use Toph's abilities to stabilize rather than overextend.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), artifact hate (Disenchant, Abrade), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for key spells. Track graveyard contents for reanimation or storm threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Toph with full board support, heavy artifact synergies, or direct damage spells. Win by establishing a board state that requires multiple answers to clear, then pressure for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for key threats, and opponents are low on interaction. Commit when you can swing for lethal or establish a board that opponents can't clear in one turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked board with no answers, have no draw spells, and opponents are comboing off. Concede if you're down to one life and have no viable win conditions or protection.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, redundant threats. Cast spells in response to each other. Use mana rocks to cast multiple spells in a turn. Avoid overcommitting to single high-value spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure. Use artifact hate and targeted removal. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and disrupt their mana base.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize board wipes and efficient removal. Hold up interaction for key threats. Use life gain if available. Stabilize by turn 3-4 and transition to a controlling midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure the board early. Use artifact synergies to bypass control's removal. Out-value them with card draw and resilient threats. Force them to use answers on low-value targets.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and value engines. Use targeted removal for key artifacts. Control the board with wipes when ahead. Out-value them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against token decks, prioritize sweepers and artifact hate. Against control, bring in card advantage and direct damage. Against combo, add graveyard hate and additional removal. Maintain a balance between interaction and win conditions.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Toph without protection or answers. Always keep open mana for interaction. Track opponent's graveyard and hand size. Use mana rocks strategically to enable surprise attacks. Be mindful of the format's power level and adjust accordingly.

philosophy: A five-color control-midrange deck built around protecting Avatar Aang while using Airbender's Reversal for flexible interaction and Toph for board stabilization. It aims to out-value opponents through superior card quality and protection, winning against aggressive strategies via disruption and against control/combo through resilient threats and late-game inevitability.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 3-4 lands (prioritize basic lands or duals that enter untapped), at least one early interaction (Airbender's Reversal) or mana accelerator. Drop hands with 0-1 lands, 4+ lands with no spells, or no way to develop mana by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, optionally cast a cheap mana rock or cantrip. Turn 2: Develop into three mana, play Toph or a low-cost threat, and use Airbender's Reversal to bounce an opponent's early threat or protect your own. Sequence lands to fix colors while preserving untapped lands for protection spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2-3 targeted removal, +2 counterspells, -2 heavy finishers. vs Control: +2 card draw, +1 artifact/enchantment removal, -2 early interaction. vs Combo: +2 hate bears/graveyard hate, +1 extra turn, -2 non-essential threats. vs Mirror: +2 targeted removal, +1 protection spell, -2 generic interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Five-color decks live and die by mana fixing; prioritize fetching/shocking to maintain flexibility without stalling. Hoard interaction until you identify the primary threat, and avoid overcommitting to the board if Avatar Aang is vulnerable to sweepers or targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have answered the opponent's removal or have protection ready. Hold back if they have sweepers, counterspells, or unanswered value engines. Use Toph to lock down key permanents or protect your board from removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for mana pools held until end step (counters/wipes), lands played out of sequence (ramp), and graveyard activity (recursion). Key tells include discarding specific cards, playing multiple lands in a row, or targeting your creatures with spot removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Avatar Aang as the primary win condition, supported by Toph's protection and Airbender's Reversal to bounce blockers or protect Aang from removal. Chain value engines to out-resource opponents.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you control the board, have protection for Aang, and can swing through remaining blockers. Commit when the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells in hand.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you face an unbreakable lock (Stax, empty library, infinite combo), if you're down to one life with no interaction or finishers, or if your mana base is completely broken and you cannot develop plays.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast spells in response to their counters, use cheap/cheap-to-cast threats, or split casting costs. Use Airbender's Reversal to bounce their counterspell or protect your own key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use discard or artifact destruction, and prioritize card advantage to out-value their acceleration. Don't let them stabilize into a massive board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and counterspells, use Toph to protect your board, and transition to a control game plan before they close out the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure, use card draw to out-resource them, and protect Avatar Aang from targeted removal. Go wide or go big depending on their board state.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through superior card quality, targeted removal, and protection spells. Out-value them in the late game by protecting your key pieces while disrupting theirs.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction ratio based on the metagame, bring in hate cards for specific archetypes, and consider additional protection for Avatar Aang. If facing more control, prioritize card draw and finishers; if facing more aggro, prioritize early interaction and board wipes.
notes: Five-color mana bases are fragile; avoid overextending into sweepers. Airbender's Reversal is your most versatile tool—use it to bounce threats, protect Aang, or trigger ETB/untap effects. Toph likely provides static protection or board lock; coordinate your plays around her. Watch for format-specific rules regarding legendary permanents and commander tax if applicable.

philosophy: Zimone, Infinite Analyst is a G/U combo-control deck that generates infinite value or mana through Zimone's engine, using Elementalist's Palette to optimize spell casting and Hydroid Krasis as a high-impact finisher. It wins by out-resourcing control and combo decks while stabilizing against aggro with early interaction and late-game inevitability.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably ramp or duals that enter untapped), Zimone or Elementalist's Palette, and at least one early interaction or cantrip. Mulligan if you have no early plays, are missing both colors, or hold exclusively high-cost cards without a clear path to Zimone or Krasis.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a cheap ramp spell or cantrip to dig for Zimone/Palette. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast Elementalist's Palette if possible, or play Zimone if you have sufficient mana. Sequence lands to ensure G/U access by turn 3-4, prioritizing untapped sources to cast Zimone's activation or Krasis without tapping out prematurely.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in targeted removal, counters, and board wipes. Against control/combo (Prismari, Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in graveyard hate, additional counters, and hatebears. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, card advantage, and graveyard disruption. Keep generic interaction and mana fixing in all games.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for Zimone's engine and Hydroid Krasis. Use the graveyard strategically to cast Krasis with maximum payoff, but avoid overloading it if the opponent has graveyard hate. Don't overcommit to board presence if the opponent has sweepers or removal in hand.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent's hand is empty or they've already used sweepers. Hold back if they have potential removal, counters, or recursion. Use Zimone's value generation to pressure while maintaining board presence, but avoid trading into unfavorable exchanges.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, counterspells, graveyard recursion, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out or hold up mana, as this often signals a counter or removal spell. Track graveyard contents to anticipate Krasis payoffs or recursion threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Hydroid Krasis with full Krasis payoff (exiling key cards from the graveyard for maximum effect), or Zimone's infinite loop for game. Use Elementalist's Palette to maximize Krasis effects or bypass counters when resolving Zimone.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have open mana for interaction, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can safely resolve Krasis or Zimone. Commit when you've established board control and the opponent is out of resources to disrupt your combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if the opponent has multiple sweepers, you're out of resources, or you're facing a locked combo they can't interact with. Concede if you're out of cards, the opponent has established an unbreakable board, or your win conditions are consistently countered or removed.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Elementalist's Palette for alternate casting methods or to split spells. Cast Zimone or Krasis in response to counters, use cantrips to dig past counters, and apply pressure to force interaction before your key spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early interaction, discard, or fast Krasis to disrupt their mana development. Don't let them reach their win condition; stabilize with Zimone's value and close the game quickly.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early removal, counters, and board wipes to stabilize. Leverage Zimone's value generation to out-value their creatures, then close with Hydroid Krasis or Zimone's infinite loop.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Zimone's value generation and use Elementalist's Palette to bypass counters. Win with Hydroid Krasis or Zimone's infinite loop, forcing them to interact with multiple threats simultaneously.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win through card advantage, targeted removal, and graveyard hate. Time your Hydroid Krasis and Zimone activations to out-resource the opponent, and prioritize disrupting their combo pieces while protecting your own.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, prioritize matchup-specific hate cards over generic answers. Adjust your land base if the format demands more fixing or protection against specific colors. Focus on disrupting top meta strategies while maintaining your deck's core engine and finisher consistency.
notes: Zimone's infinite loop requires precise sequencing and mana management; practice the exact activation order to avoid dead turns. Hydroid Krasis needs careful graveyard setup to maximize its payoff, so avoid exiling key cards prematurely. Elementalist's Palette's flexibility is key to overcoming counters and optimizing spell effects. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection, and always track the opponent's graveyard and hand size to anticipate their next move.

philosophy: The deck leverages high-volume spell casting to trigger life loss and card draw engines like Queza and Psychosis Crawler, converting incremental advantage into a lethal life-loss clock while using black and white removal to control the board. It excels against combo and control decks that rely on single big threats, as its cantrip-heavy game plan out-values them while applying steady pressure.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands and at least one of Queza, Psychosis Crawler, or Marauding Blight-Priest, plus a cheap cantrip or interaction. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, three+ non-basic lands without fetches, or only expensive spells with no early interaction. Prioritize hands that can cast a 1-drop or cantrip on turn 1 to stabilize life total and dig for key pieces.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap spell to trigger life loss/card draw, then pass. If holding a 1-drop creature, play it instead. Turn 2: Develop mana, cast another cantrip or deploy Marauding Blight-Priest. Sequence lands to ensure blue and black mana sources are available by turn 2, as Queza requires {U}{B}. If drawing multiple cantrips, chain them to dig for removal or finishers while stabilizing life total.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and life gain; drop cantrips that don't stabilize life. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in graveyard hate, counters, and late-game finishers; keep cantrips to dig through disruption. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and card advantage engines; prioritize trading life-loss triggers efficiently.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a resource for recursion or sacrifice outlets, but avoid overcommitting to it if graveyard hate is present. Mana should be preserved for interaction on opponent's turn; use cantrips to filter into answers. Life total is a resource—track opponent's life loss triggers to calculate when you can safely attack or cast high-cost spells.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have removal or a life-loss clock that outpaces their removal. Hold back on overextending if you lack interaction; Queza and Psychosis Crawler are vulnerable to board wipes, so protect them with counters or spot removal. Prioritize clearing key threats that can disrupt your spell-synergy (e.g., hate bears, counters, or sweepers).
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development and hand size. If they're holding up blue mana, assume counterspells; if they're tapping for multiple colors, they may have sweepers or late-game finishers. Track life loss triggers to know when they're approaching lethal or when you're close to winning. Note any graveyard recursion or sacrifice outlets they may be building toward.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Queza, Psychosis Crawler, and Marauding Blight-Priest (via sacrifice) form the core win conditions. Chain cantrips to trigger life loss repeatedly, then attack with creatures or sacrifice Blight-Priest for the final burst. If ahead, use removal to clear blockers and trade life loss for game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when your life-loss clock is within 3-5 life of the opponent, you have removal for their key threats, and you can protect your engines from sweepers. Also commit when you've drawn into multiple cantrips and a finisher, ensuring you can close the game before they stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1-2 life with no life gain, no removal, and the opponent has a proven win condition (e.g., a combo piece or sweepers in hand). Concede if you're locked out of your key engines and the opponent has established an unbreakable board state with no interaction to disrupt it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target, or split-second spells if available. Cast cantrips in response to their counterspells to dig for answers. If they counter your key spell, use the life loss trigger to pressure them while you dig for a backup plan. Consider sideboarding in spell-skew or similar effects if the format allows.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to force them to play around your threats. Use spot removal to eliminate their mana sources or key creatures. If they stabilize, transition to a control game plan with sweepers and life loss to out-value their big spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize life gain and spot removal to stabilize. Use cantrips to dig for answers while trading life loss for board control. Once stabilized, transition to midrange by deploying Queza/Psychosis Crawler and closing the game with life loss and creature attacks.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total early to force mistakes. Use cantrips to dig through their disruption and find removal for their key threats. If they stabilize, trade life loss triggers for card advantage and close the game with a combination of creature attacks and sacrifice outlets.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by optimizing your spell sequence. Prioritize keeping your life total higher than your opponent's to survive their triggers. Use targeted removal to eliminate their engines first, and sideboard in card advantage engines to out-draw them. Track life loss carefully to avoid accidentally killing yourself.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve based on the metagame. Against slower decks, add more late-game finishers and sweepers. Against faster decks, increase spot removal and life gain. Always ensure your mana base can support the new cards, and consider adding basic lands if you're facing heavy non-basic hate. Keep your core engine consistent, but swap out cantrips for more interaction or finishers as needed.
notes: Be cautious of self-inflicted life loss from Queza and Psychosis Crawler; always calculate triggers before casting spells. Marauding Blight-Priest is a double-edged sword—sacrificing it for life gain and damage can backfire if you're low on life. In formats with graveyard hate, consider reducing recursion spells and focusing on in-the-board synergy. Always track opponent's life total relative to your triggers to avoid accidental losses.

philosophy: Establish a resilient, flying board of Spirit tokens while leveraging Hope Estheim’s recursive engine to out-value opponents. The deck wins by outlasting midrange and combo decks through card advantage, flash combat tricks, and relentless pressure, while stabilizing against aggro with targeted removal and temporary board wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including a fetch or Arcane Signet if possible), Estheim or early ramp (Sol Ring, mana dork), and at least one interaction/removal. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only Estheim and no mana/lands. Keep hands with 4+ mana sources if targeting Estheim on turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Arcane Signet or a mana dork. Turn 2: Ramp into Signet or play a 2-drop enabler like Rhox Faithmender or a cheap Spirit. Prioritize establishing mana acceleration and early board presence. If opening with Estheim, ensure you have 4 mana and a land drop ready for turn 3.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko/Goblins): Bring in board wipes (Decree of Justice, Wrath of God), extra removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and life gain. Against control/combo: Bring in more card draw, protection (Negate, Counterspell), and graveyard hate if needed. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and bounce effects to disrupt recursion.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Estheim’s {2}{W} activations for critical moments: recursion for value, flash for combat tricks, or bouncing to protect from sweepers. Avoid overcommitting mana to bouncing your own permanents unless necessary. Manage graveyard resources by keeping key Spirits in play when possible, and use Rhox Faithmender to untap lands for repeated activations.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or flash protection ready. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently against non-flying blockers, and prioritize trading into removal spells to preserve Estheim and key enablers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Damnation), targeted removal (Path, Terminate), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or have graveyard resources. Track when they use removal on your tokens versus your commander to gauge their hand size and strategy.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Estheim’s recursion engine combined with a wide board of flying Spirits. Win by trading up with flash combat tricks, recuring key Spirits from the graveyard, or using Estheim’s bounce to reset the board while maintaining card advantage. Push damage with flying tokens once the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Estheim in play, sufficient mana for multiple activations, and the opponent has no sweepers or targeted removal in hand. Also commit when you can trade up favorably in combat or when the opponent is at lethal and you have flying tokens to push through.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a fast combo that can’t be answered, if you’re locked out of mana/land, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board (e.g., infinite damage or unstoppable win condition) with no interaction left. Concede if your recursion engine is completely neutered by graveyard hate and you lack alternative win conditions.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use flash combat tricks, split your mana across multiple turns, cast Estheim’s abilities as sorceries (they have flash), or use cheap threats that don’t need to be cast. Bounce your own permanents to protect them from counterspells, and prioritize casting Estheim when the opponent has no blue mana or counterspells available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on mana dorks/rocks, and stabilize with board wipes. Don’t let them get too far ahead in mana. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently and force them to use removal on low-value targets while you preserve your recursion engine.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize life gain, early removal, and board wipes. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently. Don’t overextend into combat if you can’t block. Stabilize with Rhox Faithmender to pump your board and untap lands for Estheim activations to clear the board or bounce threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats, use Estheim’s recursion to out-value their removal, and win the card advantage war. Force them to use answers on low-value targets. Use flash to cast Estheim’s abilities in response to removal, and prioritize graveyard recursion over casting new spells when they have heavy countermagic.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by better resource management, timing your Estheim activations, and using targeted removal to break their recursion engine. Prioritize graveyard hate and bounce effects. Trade up in combat by using flying tokens to remove their key permanents, and conserve mana for flash responses to their removal spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific answers. Against token/ramp decks, prioritize board wipes and targeted removal. Against control/combo, add more counterspells, protection, and card draw. Always keep at least one general answer and a life gain spell. Adjust your land base if needed for specific mana requirements, and consider bringing in additional recursion enablers or graveyard hate depending on the local meta.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Estheim’s bounce ability on your own permanents, which can disrupt your own mana base or combo. Remember that Estheim’s abilities have flash, so you can activate them in response to removal or during combat. Rhox Faithmender is a key enabler for pumping your board and untapping lands for activations. Watch out for graveyard hate like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void, which can neuter your recursion engine. Always track opponent hand size before committing to a large board state.

philosophy: This deck leverages noncreature spells and Curse enchantments to rapidly stack +1/+1 counters on Auntie Ool, Cursewretch, then sacrifices her for game-swinging creature removal or direct damage. It wins by out-resourcing midrange and control decks with efficient value engines while leveraging sacrifice outlets to disrupt aggressive and combo strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals or shocklands, or basics if necessary), a cheap noncreature spell (e.g., Curse of the Pierced Heart, Dismember, Thoughtseize), and a sacrifice outlet or early threat. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no spells, or multiple high-cost spells with no early interaction. Keep hands that can play a land, then cast a Curse or cheap noncreature spell on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap Curse or noncreature spell if mana allows. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another noncreature spell to grow Ool, or deploy a sacrifice outlet like Ignoble Hierarch. If you have Ool in hand, play her on turn 2 or 3 once you have a Curse or noncreature spell ready to trigger her growth. Prioritize establishing a board presence with cheap value creatures or enchantments while setting up the sacrifice engine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in more direct damage, early interaction, and artifact/enchantment hate. Against control/midrange (Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in additional noncreature spells, discard, and extra sacrifice outlets. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional removal, and artifact/enchantment destruction. Against mirror: Bring in extra counters, more sacrifice outlets, and targeted removal for Ool/Massacre Girl.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a resource for recursion or discard protection. Prioritize casting noncreature spells over creatures when possible to maximize Ool's growth. Manage mana to ensure you always have an open mana for sacrifice outlets and removal. Don't overcommit to Ool if the board is stacked against you; preserve value creatures and interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have a clear path to value or when you can trade favorably via sacrifice. Hold back Ool if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal, as she's a high-value target. Use Massacre Girl to clear key blockers or finish games when Ool is on the back foot.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal (e.g., Path to Exile, Fatal Push), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for spells, as this is your window to grow Ool or cast curses. Track graveyard content for recursion or sacrifice triggers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sacrificing a fully grown Auntie Ool for a massive -X/-X effect to clear the board or finish a player. Massacre Girl, Known Killer attacking with +1/+1 counters or sacrificing for targeted removal. Graveyard recursion of Ool or Massacre Girl for repeated value.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can close the game in 1-2 turns. Commit when you can safely grow Ool to 5+ counters and have a guaranteed sacrifice outlet.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo or aggro deck with no interaction, if you're dead to a board wipe with no recovery, or if you're consistently drawing lands with no noncreature spells or outlets. Also concede if the opponent has established an unbreakable lock or infinite combo.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target, or split-second spells if available. Cast noncreature spells in response to threats to minimize counter windows. Use sacrifice outlets to trade value before they can counter. If possible, sideboard in more uncounterable or redundant threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use discard and early interaction to slow them down. Prioritize removal of their ramp sources and use sacrifice outlets to trade efficiently before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Keep early interaction and discard to slow their curve. Use sacrifice outlets to trade favorably against small creatures. Stabilize with removal and then transition to your value engine. Don't overextend into combat; use Ool's sacrifice ability to clear key threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their hand and graveyard to limit their answers. Cast noncreature spells to grow Ool while they're tapped out. Use sacrifice outlets to bypass removal. Force them to use resources on your threats while you dig for more value.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better sequencing of noncreature spells and sacrifice outlets. Prioritize protecting Ool with counters or protection spells. Use discard to strip their key spells. Win trades by sacrificing Massacre Girl or Hierarch to clear their Ool while preserving yours.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to match the matchup. Against slower decks, add more late-game finishers and card draw. Against faster decks, add early interaction and hate bears. Always ensure you have enough sacrifice outlets to handle the opponent's threats. Consider bringing in mana base adjustments if the format allows, but in Brawl, focus on card selection.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Ool without a sacrifice outlet, neglecting to cast noncreature spells to grow her, and failing to track the opponent's removal. Remember that Ool's ability is a sorcery-speed sacrifice, so plan your turns accordingly. Synergize with curses that have beneficial effects or additional value. Always keep an eye on the opponent's graveyard for recursion threats.

philosophy: Chatterfang is a value engine that trades creatures for cards, aiming to out-resource opponents through relentless card advantage and token generation. It wins by stabilizing early, out-drawing control and combo decks, and out-valuing aggro with efficient removal and late-game finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a turn-1 land drop, and either Chatterfang, a cheap creature, or removal. Prioritize hands that can play a land, then a creature/removal on turn 1, and set up the Chatterfang engine by turn 2-3. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, or hands with only draw spells and no creatures/removal.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then a cheap creature or removal. If you have Sol Ring, play it on turn 1, then a land, then a creature/removal. Turn 2: Play Chatterfang if possible, or another creature/removal. If you have Chatterfang, play it to get two Squirrels. If you have a creature and Deadly Dispute, you can sacrifice it for card advantage. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/playing lands that enter untapped to ensure you hit your mana steps for Chatterfang (2BG) and removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in more early interaction (e.g., Fatal Push, Assassin's Trophy, or discard), fewer finishers. Against control/combo (Prismari, Y'shtola, Night's Blessed, Ur-Dragon): Bring in more card draw, protection for Chatterfang (e.g., Mortify, Go for the Throat, or counters if available), and graveyard hate if needed. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and discard to disrupt their engine.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Use Deadly Dispute sparingly; only sacrifice when you can replace the creature or when the card advantage is critical. Manage your graveyard carefully, as it's your primary resource for card draw. Don't overcommit to token generation if you're low on removal; prioritize board presence and card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal. Use Chatterfang's tokens as both threats and sacrifice fodder, but don't flood the board if you can't answer their answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (e.g., Murder, Go for the Throat), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up mana for protection. Track their graveyard if they're using it for value.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Chatterfang itself, often protected or replayed via recursion. Secondary finishers include efficient removal to clear the board and a large creature or direct damage. The goal is to stabilize, draw into a win condition, and close the game before the opponent can reset the board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal to answer their threats, and the opponent is at low life or has no sweepers. Also commit when you can safely sacrifice tokens for Deadly Dispute to draw into your finisher.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted, if you're down to one card against a board wipe, or if you're out of resources and the opponent has multiple sweepers or unblockable threats. Also concede if you're dead to the matchup post-sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple low-cost threats to force counter windows. Employ discard or discard effects to disrupt their hand. Use protection or recursion to get Chatterfang back if countered.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use efficient removal to kill their ramp creatures. Don't let them establish a board; trade efficiently and out-value them.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and removal. Use Chatterfang's tokens to block and trade efficiently. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to value and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and efficient threats. Protect Chatterfang from targeted removal. Use discard to disrupt their hand. Force them to use resources on your threats while you draw into answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize card draw and efficient removal. Use discard to disrupt their Deadly Dispute or Chatterfang. Control the board with fewer, more efficient creatures.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve and interaction based on the metagame. Against faster decks, increase early removal and discard. Against slower decks, add more card draw, protection, and late-game finishers. Always include hate cards against the top combo strategies. Maintain a balance between interaction and value engines.
notes: Chatterfang is vulnerable to board wipes, so don't overcommit to token generation. Deadly Dispute is your best friend, but don't sacrifice your only creature unless necessary. Watch for opponent's discard or hand disruption. The deck thrives on patience and out-valuing, not rushing. Avoid playing around cards you don't need to; play the game as it develops.

philosophy: This deck leverages a high volume of cheap noncreature spells to continuously generate mana and Golem tokens via Ulalek, transitioning into a massive board presence or explosive spell-slinging finisher. It excels against slower, resource-starved decks that cannot handle rapid board development or sudden mana surges.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including duals/signets), Sol Ring or Arcane Signet, and at least one cheap noncreature spell or cantrip. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no ramp/spells, or only creatures with no way to cast them or protect Ulalek.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet if possible, or cast a cheap cantrip/ramp spell. Turn 2: Develop your mana base, cast another noncreature spell to trigger Ulalek if it's on board, or play a cheap creature/spellslinger. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetches/shocks to find basics for signets, then duals for five-color fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes, early interaction, and removal. Against control: Bring in spell acceleration, card draw, and resilient threats. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counters, and disruption. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counters, and card advantage engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard noncreature spells for big turns; use Golem tokens for mana acceleration rather than combat early on. Protect hand resources to maintain spell velocity and ensure you can respond to threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Ulalek and key spellslinger effects early to establish the engine. Hold back on overcommitting to the board if opponents have sweepers; use tokens for mana to answer threats or dig for answers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (sweepers will reset your progress), targeted removal on Ulalek, and graveyard hate if you rely on recursion. Note when opponents start hoarding counterspells or have high life totals with no board presence.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A massive Golem army fueled by a turn of cheap spells, or a high-impact spell (planeswalker, big creature, or combo piece) cast with the generated mana.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 5+ mana open, a clear path to combat, or when opponents are tapped out and lack immediate removal. Trigger Ulalek's upkeep with additional spells to maximize token generation before attacking.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if Ulalek is permanently removed, you have no way to recast it, and the board is wiped with no card draw or recursion in hand. Also concede against locked-out control or infinite combo opponents with no interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target spells; cast threats in response to their counters; utilize mana rocks to cast spells with hexproof or from exile if possible; don't overcommit to single expensive spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with tokens; use removal on their mana sources; don't let them stabilize into a massive board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes; use Golem tokens to block or trade; stabilize before committing to your engine.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with incremental value; use cantrips to dig through their lock; win via combat with a large army or a resilient finisher they can't easily answer.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win via card advantage and efficient removal; prioritize keeping Ulalek alive; use counterspells or discard to disrupt their spell count; trade resources efficiently.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate cards. Against control, prioritize card draw and resilient threats. Against aggro, bring in sweepers and early interaction. Against combo, include graveyard hate and disruption. In mirrors, focus on targeted removal and card advantage engines. Always ensure your mana base remains functional with the new card distribution.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to combat too early without securing the spell engine, or failing to use Golem tokens for mana acceleration. Remember that Ulalek's upkeep trigger creates tokens for the entire turn, so casting spells in response to upkeep can double-dip. Protect your signets and mana rocks, as they are critical for five-color consistency. Avoid casting your most valuable noncreature spells when opponents have visible sweepers unless you have a guaranteed answer.

philosophy: This deck aims to rapidly accelerate mana using ramp spells and mana rocks, deploy Omnath, Locus of Creation to trigger its powerful mana and card-draw abilities, and overwhelm opponents with massive X-spells or game-winning threats. It wins by out-resourcing and out-scaling opponents, excelling against slower decks and combo strategies that give it time to assemble its game plan.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2+ lands (preferably including a fetch/shock or dual land) and at least one ramp spell (Growth Spiral, Arcane Signet, Sol Ring, or Chrome Mox). Keep hands with Swords to Plowshares if paired with a land or early threat. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only removal and no ramp, or hands with 3+ lands and no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Chrome Mox/Arcane Signet, or cast Growth Spiral if you have 2 lands. Turn 2: Ramp further with Growth Spiral or another rock, or cast Omnath if you have 4+ mana. Prioritize fetching basic lands for Omnath's triggers and shock lands for early life loss mitigation.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): +2 Swords to Plowshares, +1 Path to Exile, -2 Growth Spiral, -1 Omnath. Against control/combo (Prismari, Ur-Dragon): +2 Ancient Grudge, +1 Anger of the Gods, -2 Chrome Mox, -1 Sol Ring. Against mirror: +2 Path to Exile, +1 Anger of the Gods, -2 Growth Spiral.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize maintaining mana acceleration over spending on threats early. Use Growth Spiral to dig for answers or Omnath. Don't overcommit to board presence before Omnath is on the field. Manage graveyard by being mindful of discard or graveyard hate, though the deck isn't heavily reliant on it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Hold back on casting expensive threats until Omnath is active and you have a clear path to win. Use removal efficiently on key threats (e.g., Krenko's goblins, Ur-Dragon). Commit to the board once you have 5+ mana available and Omnath's triggers are resolving.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana base (are they playing fetch/shock or dual lands?). Track if they're playing discard or graveyard hate. Note when they tap out for a single big spell vs. holding up mana for interaction. Key tells: Tapping out for a 4-mana spell often means they're out of interaction.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Massive X-spells (e.g., Storm the Festival, Dragon's Rage Channeler, or generic big creatures like Emrakul, the Promised End). Once Omnath is active, you can generate 10+ mana in a turn.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Omnath on the battlefield, at least 2 other permanents, and enough mana to cast a game-ending spell or swing for lethal. Also commit when the opponent has no removal in hand and you can close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 life with no ramp, no Omnath, and the opponent has a board of 3+ creatures. Concede if you're facing a fast combo that can win before turn 5 and you have no interaction. Also fold if you're in a dead mirror with no card draw and the opponent is ahead on resources.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Omnath's card draw to dig past counters. Cast spells in response to their counterspells if you have protection. Use Growth Spiral to find answers or cast threats in response to their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Out-ramp them with more efficient rocks. Use Swords to Plowshares to remove their mana sources. Don't overextend; stabilize and win with bigger threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early ramp to stabilize, use Swords to Plowshares on key threats, then out-value with Omnath's card draw and big spells. Don't trade 1-for-1; use removal on the most threatening creature.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use Omnath to draw into answers, and cast X-spells when they're tapped out. Out-value them with card advantage and mana acceleration.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by card advantage and efficient removal. Prioritize keeping Omnath alive. Use Swords to Plowshares on their Omnath or key threats. Dig deeper with Growth Spiral to find more value.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed (e.g., add more shock lands against control). Swap out ramp for removal against aggro, and bring in more card draw or protection against control/combo. Always keep at least 2 copies of Growth Spiral and 2 of Swords to Plowshares unless facing extreme hate.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to the board before Omnath is active, leaving you vulnerable to removal. Another: Forgetting to fetch basic lands for Omnath's triggers, which can slow down your mana and card draw. Unique interaction: Omnath's ETB triggers can be responded to, so don't assume they're free. Also, Growth Spiral can target your own permanents for value, but be careful not to give opponents triggers if they have relevant abilities.

philosophy: This deck aims to stabilize early with card draw and flexible combat tricks, then snowball into an insurmountable board state using Song of Creation to flood the field with Golem tokens. It excels against combo and control decks that struggle to answer a wide, resilient army, while leveraging Flubs' pump to close out games or trade efficiently.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing dual lands or signets for fixing), a turn-1 or turn-2 land drop, and either Flubs or early ramp/spells. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only single-colored lands with no fixing, or a hand full of high-cost spells without a way to cast them. Prioritize hands that can play a land, then a signet or cheap spell on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then Arcane Signet or a cheap cantrip/ramp spell. Turn 2: If you have a second land, play Flubs, the Fool to draw a card and establish a pump outlet. If you have more mana, look for early threats or ramp to set up Song of Creation by turn 4-5. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast both Flubs and Song of Creation without flooding.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes, removal, and life gain. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Prismari): Bring in card advantage, hate bears, and faster finishers. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and additional pump spells. Against ramp (Ur-Dragon): Bring in artifact hate, counterspells, and early interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Flubs as a flexible pump outlet and card draw engine; don't overcommit it to trades unless necessary. Use Arcane Signet to smooth mana for Song of Creation. Track graveyard and exile threats, as token decks often rely on recursion or board wipes.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have answered or outpaced the opponent's interaction. Hold back Flubs if the opponent has a known board wipe or targeted removal. Use Golem tokens as chump blockers to preserve your strategy while trading up.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (especially ones that don't target artifacts), targeted removal on Flubs, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for a single big spell, as that's your window to push damage or cast Song of Creation.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Song of Creation is the primary win condition, generating a massive board of Golems that can swing combat or block out a game. Flubs pumps key Golems or your main attacker to lethal. Secondary finishers include combat tricks, direct damage, or milling/stealing if the format allows.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no board wipes or removal in hand, or you've forced them to use their interaction on earlier threats. Commit when your Golem army can trade favorably or push through unblocked damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can't be answered, you're locked out of your key pieces by hate cards, or you're down to a single Golem against a board of high-impact threats with no removal or pump outlets.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Flubs' card draw to dig for answers or cheap threats. Cast spells in response to their counterspells if possible, or use signet/mana rocks to ensure you have enough mana to cast around them. If the format allows, use split spells or multi-target effects.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on their ramp sources and board wipes to reset their board. Prioritize keeping your own board state resilient to their big spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal, board wipes, and chump-blocking with Golem tokens. Use Flubs to pump blockers and trade efficiently. Don't overextend into their combat tricks; wait for a favorable turn to cast Song of Creation.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure their life total and force them to use resources on your threats. Use Flubs to out-value their card draw. Cast Song of Creation when they've exhausted their interaction, or use it as a surprise finisher.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Prioritize keeping Flubs alive longer, as it's your primary value engine. Use targeted removal on their Song of Creation or key pump outlets. Trade Golems efficiently and look for the first player to run out of resources.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift toward a more interactive midrange game plan. Increase removal and board wipes against combo/aggro, and add more card advantage or hate cards against control. In the mirror, prioritize targeted removal and additional pump spells to out-value your opponent. Always keep at least one copy of your key win condition (Song of Creation) unless facing a specific hate strategy.
notes: Flubs' pump is sorcery-speed, so plan combat carefully and don't assume you can respond to instant-speed removal. Song of Creation's Golems are 1/1s, so they can be wiped by any board wipe; always have a backup plan. Arcane Signet is crucial for fixing; if it's removed, your late-game mana will suffer. Avoid overcommitting to a single Golem army without tracking opponent interaction.

philosophy: This R/U archetype functions as a reactive control-combo hybrid that leverages card advantage and flexible threats to out-value opponents. It aims to stabilize early, accumulate resources through Curiosity Crafter and Muddle, and close games with adaptable, high-impact plays that exploit matchup-specific weaknesses.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands (including duals/fixing), Muddle the Ever-Changing, and at least one card draw or early interaction. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple unplayable early spells, or no way to establish board presence by turn 2. Prioritize hands that can cast Muddle or Curiosity Crafter by turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or cheap interaction if available. Turn 2: Develop mana base, cast Curiosity Crafter or Muddle if possible, or hold up counters/removal. Land sequencing: Prioritize fetching/drawing duals or shock lands to ensure consistent R/U access; avoid flooding with single-color sources.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2-3 fast removal, +1-2 counters, -2-3 slow finishers. vs Control: +2-3 card draw, +1-2 hatebears, -2-3 combo pieces. vs Combo: +2-3 hand disruption, +1-2 graveyard hate, -2-3 board wipes. vs Mirror: +2-3 targeted removal, +1-2 value engines, -2-3 generic interaction.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Conserve mana for interaction until Muddle enters; use Curiosity Crafter to fuel card advantage without overextending. Track graveyard for recursion threats. Never cast Muddle's cheaper modes if the opponent has sweepers; save the high-impact mode for when it's safe.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have answered or out-value the opponent's board. Hold back Muddle if the opponent has sweepers or targeted removal; use Curiosity Crafter to pressure while preserving Muddle as a finisher or answer.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for opponent's mana development (ramp vs control), graveyard activity (recursion), and hand size (combo vs control). Key tells: holding up blue mana suggests counterspells; tapping for red suggests removal; playing multiple low-cost spells indicates combo or aggro.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Muddle the Ever-Changing's ultimate mode, often combined with Curiosity Crafter's value generation or Enter the Enigma's board impact. Secure these by protecting Muddle with counters, evasion, or timely removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction in hand, when you have lethal in hand/board, or when Muddle's ever-changing ability can bypass their defense. Also commit when you've out-valued them in card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you're locked out of the game (e.g., opponent has a win condition you can't interact with), when your hand is dead against their strategy, or when you've lost your primary win condition and have no backup plan.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use evasion, split casting costs, or cheap threats that don't rely on a single spell. Cast Muddle or Enter the Enigma in response to their counterspells if possible, or use cantrips to dig past their interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use targeted removal on key ramp creatures and counterspells on their acceleration spells. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and punish their overextension.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with efficient removal and counterspells. Prioritize answering their early threats over developing your own board. Once stabilized, transition to value engines and close the game before they can recover.
threat_responses.against_control: Grind out card advantage with Curiosity Crafter and cantrips. Apply incremental pressure with Muddle's flexible modes. Force them to use interaction on low-value threats, then strike with Muddle's high-impact mode.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-valuing in card advantage and protecting your Muddle. Use targeted removal to eliminate their Muddle or key engines. Sideboard in hate that targets their specific interaction, and prioritize board presence over hand disruption.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, pivot to a streamlined plan: against aggro, become a reactive control deck with fast interaction; against control/combo, become a value engine with hand disruption and graveyard hate; against mirror, focus on targeted removal and card advantage. Always keep 1-2 lands in to avoid flooding, and adjust your curve based on the matchup.
notes: Common mistake: overcommitting Muddle too early without protection. Unique interaction: Muddle's ever-changing mechanic allows you to adapt its effect to the current board state, so always evaluate the opponent's most threatening resource before choosing a mode. Avoid playing Enter the Enigma into a known board wipe; wait for a safe turn or use it as a surprise answer.

philosophy: This R/U deck functions as a treasure-fueled midrange-combo hybrid that leverages artifact synergy and spell acceleration to out-value opponents. It aims to win by generating incremental advantage through cantrips and treasure generation, then closing games with high-impact threats or direct damage engines that overwhelm control and combo strategies while out-grinding aggro with efficient interaction.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing untapped or duals), at least one turn-1 or turn-2 play (Sanar, Treasure Hunt, or a cheap artifact), and an early interaction piece. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, 2+ lands with only high-casting-cost spells, or hands lacking any way to establish board presence or answer early threats.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land and cast a cantrip or cheap artifact to establish mana/artifact synergy and draw into your game plan. Turn 2: Deploy Sanar or cast Treasure Hunt to generate treasure, then follow up with a threat or interaction. Prioritize untapped lands for R/U duals; use fetches only if you need a specific basic for shock lands or to smooth a mana flood.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Vs Aggro: +2 Artifact Ensnarement, +2 Pyroclasm/Blasphemous Act, -2 expensive finishers. Vs Control/Combo: +2 Spell Pierce/Force of Will, +2 Arcane Denial, -2 aggressive threats. Vs Mirror: +1 Copy Artifact/Spell Queller, +2 targeted removal, -2 redundant synergies.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard treasure and artifacts for burst turns rather than spending them on minor advantages. Use cantrips to maintain hand size and avoid card starvation. Track graveyard recursion threats and avoid overcommitting mana on single-target removal when board wipes are likely.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have at least one interaction or a board wipe in hand. Hold back if the opponent has open mana for counterspells or sweepers. Use treasure to cast multiple spells in response to maintain tempo and force unfavorable trades.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), hard counters (Mana Leak, Negate), and combo wincons. Note when opponents tap out for lock pieces or finishers, and track how much interaction they have left to determine if you can safely go off.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Magmakin Artillerist (pumped via treasure/artifact synergies), Sanar's ultimate ability, or a high-impact spell fueled by accumulated treasure. Win by overwhelming the board with synergistic threats or dealing direct damage through artifact engines.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ treasure/artifacts, a way to protect your finisher, and the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells. Commit if you can close the game in 1-2 turns without leaving yourself vulnerable to a comeback.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 2 life with no interaction, facing a locked board with no way to break it, or in a hard matchup with no comeback tools. Do not bluff through known sweepers or overextend into combat when you lack answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use split spells, instants, or artifact ramp to cast around counters. Hold up interaction for key spells and use treasure to cast multiple cheap spells, forcing the opponent to trade counters inefficiently.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Prioritize early interaction to disrupt mana dorks and land drops. Use artifact destruction or targeted removal to slow their acceleration. Do not trade resources; win faster or lock them out with efficient interaction.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize cheap removal and board wipes. Use treasure to cast multiple spells to stabilize and regain board control. Avoid overextending into combat; trade efficiently and punish their lack of late-game resources.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with artifacts and cantrips to force them to use interaction on low-impact threats. Win with unblockable damage, combo finishers, or by out-resourcing them through card advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value trades and card advantage win mirror matches. Prioritize keeping interaction for their key spells and use treasure to cast more spells per turn. Win by out-resourcing them and timing your finisher after they blow their interaction.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift toward matchup-specific interaction and hate cards. Against control and combo, increase counterspells and disruption. Against aggro, add sweepers and early removal. In mirrors, prioritize card draw and targeted removal. Always keep at least one copy of your primary win condition and core synergy pieces, and adjust your land count if the format demands more or fewer basics.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting treasure on small threats instead of saving for finishers. Unique interaction: Treasure can be used to cast spells with additional costs, so time your activations to bypass counters or trigger enter-the-battlefield effects. Watch for artifact hate; keep a backup plan if your synergy is targeted. Always track opponent's mana and interaction to avoid walking into hard counters or sweepers.

philosophy: This deck aims to rapidly accelerate mana and flood the board with creatures using green ramp and black recursion, then capitalize on a specific board state with High Perfect Morcant or Imperious Perfect to overwhelm opponents. It excels against slower, resource-heavy decks that cannot handle early creature swarms, but struggles against fast aggro and hard control.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands (preferably including a fetchable source or mana rock) and at least one early creature like Elvish Archdruid, Imperious Perfect, or a cheap two-drop. Keep hands with 3+ lands if they contain a turn-1 or turn-2 ramp spell. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or 3+ lands with no early plays. Prioritize hands that can legally play a land and a creature by turn 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a basic land. If you have a cheap creature or ramp spell, play it. Turn 2: Play a second land, then cast Elvish Archdruid or a mana-producing creature. If you have two lands, play Archdruid and tap for mana, or play a two-drop creature. The goal is to establish a mana base that produces 3-4 mana by turn 3 to cast your key finishers or board flooders.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Loafing Pest): Bring in board wipes, targeted removal, and life gain. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Prismari): Bring in card draw, protection spells, and additional ramp to outpace them. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells (if available), and graveyard hate. Against ramp (The Ur-Dragon): Bring in artifact/enchantment removal and early pressure.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana acceleration over card advantage early. Use graveyard recursion sparingly to protect your win conditions. Keep a hand of 3-4 cards to handle interaction. Don't overcommit to a single threat if the board is unstable.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit creatures when you have a clear path to damage or when you can stabilize against aggro. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or hard removal. Use creatures as blockers only when necessary; your win condition relies on board presence.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (board wipes), targeted removal on your key creatures, and combo pieces. Note if the opponent is playing lands that produce unusual mana or drawing extra cards, as this indicates control or combo. Track graveyard content for recursion threats.

endgame_tactics.finishers: High Perfect Morcant and Imperious Perfect are your primary win conditions. Once you have 3+ mana and a board of creatures, cast them to trigger their 'perfect' conditions or scale with your board state. Follow up with creature pumps or additional attackers to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear board advantage, the opponent has no sweepers in play or in hand, and you can attack for lethal or trigger win conditions. Also commit when you've stabilized against aggro and have a clear path to victory.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast aggro deck with no removal or life gain, if you're locked out of lands with no draw spells, or if the opponent has established an unbreakable board state (e.g., multiple sweepers, indestructible threats, or combo locks).

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use multiple cheap threats to force counter spells, then cast your finisher with protection or on an opponent's turn. Utilize mana rocks to ensure you have enough mana to cast around counters or use instant-speed interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their land drops. Use removal on their key ramp creatures. If they get ahead, prioritize board wipes and life gain to survive until you can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize life gain and early removal. Use creatures as blockers to buy time for your ramp. Once stabilized, transition to your win condition quickly before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early creatures to force interaction. Use card draw to out-value them. If they lock the board, use removal to clear the way for your finisher. Don't overextend into sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing and better sequencing. Prioritize removing their key creatures first. Use graveyard hate to disrupt recursion. Control the board with targeted removal and win by having the higher total power/toughness or better late-game resources.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve to be more interactive against control and aggro. Increase the ratio of removal and life gain against fast decks, and add more card draw and protection against control. In the mirror, prioritize targeted removal and graveyard hate. Always keep at least one land ramp spell to ensure consistency.
notes: Be mindful of the 'perfect' condition on your signature cards; it likely requires a specific board state or creature count. Don't overcommit to a single turn if the opponent has sweepers. Track opponent's graveyard for recursion threats. In the Unknown format, meta shifts quickly, so be ready to adjust your sideboard based on local play patterns. Avoid playing around cards that aren't in the format; focus on the current metagame.

philosophy: The deck wins by casting a high volume of instants and sorceries to grow Licia, Sanguine Tribune and leverage Enduring Tenacity to continuously buff a core of resilient artifact creatures. It out-scales opponents through spell-driven value, converting early tempo into an overwhelming late-game board presence that trades efficiently and closes games quickly.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (preferably duals or shocklands) plus Licia, Enduring Tenacity, or 2+ cheap instants/sorceries. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, 3+ lands with no spells, or hands lacking Licia/Enduring Tenacity and only expensive spells. Prioritize keeping any hand with Licia + 2 lands + 1 cheap spell.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap instant/sorcery (draw, spot removal, or minor buff) to trigger Licia. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a second spell to grow Licia further, then deploy an artifact creature or Enduring Tenacity. If opening with Licia + 2 lands, play Licia on turn 2, then sequence spells to build counters. Ensure B/W/R mana availability by turn 3 to cast your key spells and activate Licia's ability.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and artifact hate. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed, Ur-Dragon): Bring in more removal, card advantage, and hate bears. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and artifact destruction. Generally, side out redundant cheap spells and extra lands; bring in flexible answers and hate cards tailored to the local metagame.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for Licia's sorcery-speed buff and key instants/sorceries. Avoid overcommitting to casting spells if you lack targets or board presence.
