Manage your graveyard by avoiding unnecessary discard or self-mill unless synergistic. Use Enduring Tenacity to maximize value from each spell cast, ensuring you have enough resources to sustain pressure.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit artifact creatures when you have Licia with 3+ counters and Enduring Tenacity online. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or can easily remove your key pieces. Prioritize trading efficiently and maintaining spell density to keep Licia growing. Use Licia's ability to block or trade favorably when threatened.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods), targeted removal on Licia, and combo win conditions. Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells or spot removal. Track graveyard hate if they play it, and adjust your spell sequencing to avoid feeding it.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully grown Licia (5+ counters) with Enduring Tenacity, backed by a board of 3+ artifact creatures, can close the game in 1-2 turns. Use Licia's activated ability to push lethal or block key threats. Enduring Tenacity ensures your creatures remain unblockable or lethal even after trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Licia with 4+ counters, Enduring Tenacity, and at least two artifact creatures on the board. Also commit when the opponent has no sweepers or removal in hand, or when you can force them to use interaction on non-lethal threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no Licia/Enduring Tenacity, and the opponent has a board of sweepers or a combo that resolves next turn. Also concede if you're consistently drawing dead lands or removal with no targets, or if the metagame heavily favors their archetype and you lack sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target, or split-second spells if available. Cast threats in response to counterspells, or use Licia's ability to protect creatures. Rely on Enduring Tenacity to make threats resilient enough to trade through counters. Sequence spells to force counterspells on non-critical targets.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Licia and artifact creatures. Use spot removal to stop key ramp creatures. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and stabilize with board wipes if available. Force them to interact with your board rather than letting them dig for their win condition.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early spot removal and board wipes. Use Licia's ability to block or trade favorably. Stabilize by turn 3-4, then transition to value with Enduring Tenacity and artifact creatures. Don't let them race you; control the board and out-value them in the midgame.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Licia and artifact creatures to force interaction. Cast spells to grow Licia and out-value their card draw. Use Enduring Tenacity to make threats unblockable or lethal. Don't let them stabilize; keep the pressure on and force them to use resources on your board rather than their combo.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better spell sequencing and artifact synergy. Prioritize removing their Licia or Enduring Tenacity. Use targeted removal to break their board. Control the spell slot economy and win the attrition war by maintaining card advantage and board presence.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your curve by removing redundant cheap spells and adding flexible answers or hate cards. Against combo, prioritize disruption and hate. Against aggro, add early interaction and sweepers. In mirrors, bring in targeted removal and artifact destruction. Always ensure your mana base can handle the new card distribution, and consider adding basic lands if the format demands it.
notes: Common mistake: Overcasting spells without targets, leaving Licia vulnerable to removal. Always sequence spells to grow Licia before committing artifact creatures. Remember Licia's ability is sorcery-speed, so plan attacks and blocks accordingly. Enduring Tenacity synergizes best with instant-speed spells, so keep your hand flexible. Watch for artifact hate in the metagame and sideboard accordingly. Practice spell sequencing in practice games to optimize turn order and mana efficiency.

philosophy: This deck leverages arthropod tribal synergies to generate endless 1/1 Spider tokens, using death triggers to create larger threats and apply targeted -X/-X to neutralize key opponents' creatures. It wins by overwhelming board presence, chipping life totals through relentless pressure, and leveraging commander damage or attrition.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches/duals if available), at least one early Spider (e.g., Arasta, Webweaver Knight, or a cheap 1/1), and ramp or early interaction. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only high-cost spells, or no early threats to establish board presence.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap Spider or ramp spell (e.g., Rampant Growth). Turn 2: Ramp into a 2/2 Spider or Arasta. Sequence lands to ensure consistent drops; prioritize fetching if your mana base is complex. If you have a 1/1 and a 2/2, you can begin triggering death effects early to clear early blockers or apply pressure.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Assassin's Trophy, Toxic Deluge), and life gain. Against control: Bring in resilient threats (Shelob, Dread Weaver), card draw (Spider Web, Arachnoform), and protection. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Grafdigger's Cage) and early interaction. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counterspells if available, and extra token generators to out-value trades.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for key death triggers and removal rather than overcommitting to token generation. Use the graveyard strategically; sacrifice creatures only when you need card draw, a specific -X/-X payoff, or to clear a blocker. Avoid unnecessary sacrifices that give opponents free triggers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or board wipes ready to protect your swarm. Hold back if opponents are holding sweepers or targeted removal. Use -X/-X triggers to clear blockers before attacking, prioritizing the most dangerous opponent's key creature.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Toxic Deluge), targeted removal (Path, Trophy), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents are saving removal for your commanders. Track graveyard size for potential recursion threats and monitor life totals to gauge when commander damage or attrition becomes viable.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Overwhelming token swarms, commander damage, or chipping life with stacked -X/-X triggers. Achieve this by protecting your commanders, using card draw to refill your hand, and timing death triggers to clear the board of blockers before attacking.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a board wipe ready, opponents are low on life, or you've exhausted their removal. Attack with everything if you can't be swept, and use -X/-X triggers to remove multiple blockers simultaneously.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of the game (stax, heavy control), have no comeback potential, or are at 1 life with no board presence and opponents have sweepers/removal ready. Also concede if you're the primary target with no way to break through.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats if possible, or cast spells in response to their counterspells. Leverage mana dorks and ramp to outpace them, and time your big plays when they've exhausted interaction.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on their key ramp spells or creatures. Board wipes are essential to reset the board before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and life gain. Use -X/-X triggers to clear their early threats and don't overextend into combat. Prioritize surviving until you can establish a token wall.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and use card draw to out-value them. Time your big plays when they've exhausted removal, and use -X/-X triggers to clear their board when they commit to a sweep.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value the player with better removal and board wipes. Prioritize trading tokens efficiently and use -X/-X triggers to remove their commanders. Control the graveyard and force unfavorable trades where their creatures die to your triggers.

sideboard_advice: Post-board adjustments should focus on matchup-specific hate and table dynamics. Against sweepers-heavy metas, bring in protection or recursion. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and early interaction. In multiplayer, adjust based on table dynamics (e.g., target the most aggressive player first). Always maintain a balance of removal, card draw, and board wipes, and track cards to include/exclude in future games since this format typically uses a singleton structure.
notes: Common mistakes include over-sacrificing your own creatures without a payoff, ignoring commander damage, failing to track opponent's removal, and overcommitting to token generation without protection. Unique interactions: Shelob's -X/-X triggers stack, so timing sacrifices can clear multiple blockers. Arasta's entry creates a massive board swing, so protect it. Remember that this archetype thrives on attrition and timing; patience often beats aggression.

philosophy: This deck leverages a steady stream of 0/1 Zombie tokens to fuel sacrifice outlets, growing Sephiroth into a massive combat threat or funneling damage through The Masamune. It aims to close games quickly with recursive pressure and sacrifice synergies, out-grinding control and combo while stabilizing against aggro through token trading and targeted removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 1-2 lands, Sol Ring, and either Sephiroth or The Masamune. Drop any hand with only lands, only removal, or no early mana acceleration. Prioritize keeping 2-3 mana sources by turn 2 to cast your commander or key artifacts.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Sol Ring if available. Turn 2: Play second land, cast Sephiroth or The Masamune. If Sephiroth, begin token generation on upkeep. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your commander by turn 3 at the latest, prioritizing black mana sources.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in Damnation, Toxic Deluge, and artifact hate. vs Control/Combo (Prismari, Y'shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in card draw, protection spells, and additional sacrifice outlets. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and extra token generators.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard low-cost removal and sacrifice outlets. Avoid overcommitting to board wipes unless necessary. Manage the graveyard by using recursion sparingly; prioritize keeping Sephiroth alive and maintaining a steady token pipeline.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough tokens to trade favorably or when The Masamune can finish a player. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or if you are low on resources. Use Sephiroth's +1/+1 growth to pressure without overextending.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Damnation, Wrath), targeted removal (Murder, Fatal Push), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for counters or big spells, and track how many tokens they have to anticipate their next move.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Combat damage with a heavily buffed Sephiroth, or sacrificing a large creature/token via The Masamune for lethal. Recursion of Sephiroth with accumulated +1/+1 counters also closes games quickly.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ tokens, a sacrifice outlet, and enough mana to cast Sephiroth's ability or Masamune's trigger. Also commit if an opponent is at 5 or less life or if you have a clear path to combat damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard-lock combo, have no removal or sweepers against a massive board, or are consistently one-shot by aggro with no acceleration or interaction.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, uncounterable threats like tokens, split your spells across turns, and rely on instant-speed sacrifice outlets or protection to bypass counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use artifact hate to disrupt their mana base, and prioritize sweepers to stop their big creatures before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal, board wipes, and life gain if possible. Use Sephiroth's tokens to block and trade favorably, then pivot to a win condition once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use card draw to out-value them, and exploit their lack of interaction by committing to combat and leveraging sacrifice synergies for value.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better token generation, superior sacrifice outlets, and timing your sweepers and removal to break their board first. Prioritize keeping Sephiroth alive longer than your opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from pure acceleration to interaction. Add more removal, sweepers, and hate pieces tailored to the local metagame. Adjust land count if needed based on opponent speed, and prioritize cards that synergize with sacrifice and token generation while answering top threats.
notes: Common mistake: Over-sacrificing tokens too early without a payoff. Always calculate Masamune damage versus Sephiroth's combat value. Remember that 0/1 tokens die at end of turn, so you must sacrifice them or use them before then. Sephiroth's recursion is key; don't let it die to removal unless you have a way to bring it back. Track opponent life totals carefully, as Masamune can close games unexpectedly.

philosophy: Generate a swarm of flying Zombie Warrior tokens, sacrifice them en masse to Wilhelt to pump him into a lethal threat, and close out the game quickly. This deck excels against slower, control, and combo archetypes that lack immediate answers to a massive flying attacker in a single turn.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or signets for UB), a turn-1 land drop, and either Wilhelt or a key mana/sacrifice enabler (Arcane Signet, Dimir Signet, or a cheap outlet). Drop hands with 4+ lands and no early plays, or multiple early spells with no mana acceleration.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Arcane Signet or a mana rock. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Wilhelt or a key sacrifice outlet/token generator. Sequence mana carefully to ensure you can activate Wilhelt’s ability and sacrifice on the same turn if possible. Prioritize establishing your mana base and enablers over rushing combat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in targeted removal, board wipes, and artifact destruction. Against control/combo (Y’shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in protection spells, additional sacrifice outlets, and faster win conditions. Against the mirror: Bring in targeted removal, hate cards against sacrifice synergies, and extra protection for Wilhelt.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Manage mana to keep activation options open; avoid overcommitting to pump Wilhelt if you lack sufficient sacrifice outlets to close. Use the graveyard strategically for recursion or reanimation if your deck runs them, but be mindful of graveyard hate.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have enough sacrifice outlets and protection to ensure at least one survives to combat. Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal you cannot answer, and use your tokens as chump blockers or sacrifice fodder to stabilize.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on Wilhelt, and opponent’s combo pieces. Key tell: Opponent holding up mana with multiple lands suggests a counter or removal spell. Track their sacrifice outlets and protection spells to time your pump accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Lethal combat damage via Wilhelt’s +2/+2 pump and sacrifice outlet. Rooftop Storm amplifies this by granting +1/+1 to all attacking creatures with flying, turning a single pump into a board-wiping blow.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have enough tokens to sacrifice, sufficient mana to pump, and no immediate answer in the opponent’s hand. Ensure you have a backup plan if Wilhelt is removed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if Wilhelt is removed, you have no outlets, and the board is wiped. Also concede if you are locked out of mana, facing an unstoppable combo, or if your win condition is mathematically impossible to reach.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap enablers, split your combo across multiple turns, or use protection/removal to clear the way. Cast Wilhelt and outlets in response to counterspells when possible.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, disrupt their mana rocks, and close before they stabilize. Use removal on their key ramp pieces and avoid overextending into their late-game board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use removal and board wipes to stabilize early. Don’t overextend into combat; use your tokens as chump blockers or sacrifice fodder to survive until you can establish your win condition.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use protection spells, and close quickly before they can answer. Force them to use multiple resources to deal with your pump, and exploit their lack of early interaction.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the race by having better sacrifice outlets, more protection, or faster combo pieces. Target their Wilhelt early with removal, and prioritize establishing your mana base and enablers faster.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against control/combo, bring in more protection and faster win cons. Against aggro, bring in removal and sweepers. In mirrors, bring in targeted removal and hate against sacrifice synergies. Always keep your core mana base and key enablers, and avoid overloading on situational cards that dilute your combo consistency.
notes: Common mistakes include pumping Wilhelt without enough sacrifice outlets, overcommitting to combat without protection, and ignoring graveyard hate. Unique interactions: Rooftop Storm’s flying synergy is crucial for maximizing damage. Sacrifice outlets can target your own tokens for value, but watch out for “sacrifice” hate like Rest in Peace or Leyline of the Void. Always track opponent’s hand size and mana to time your pump correctly.

philosophy: Atraxa is a +1/+1 counter midrange-combo deck that stabilizes early, ramps into its commander, and overwhelms opponents by continuously pumping its board. It wins by out-scaling aggressive tribal decks, out-grinding control, and leveraging graveyard synergies for late-game finishers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks if applicable), Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, and at least one ramp spell or early creature. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no ramp/mana rocks, only removal with no threats/mana, or unplayable colored mana requirements. Prioritize hands that can cast a land and a ramp spell on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or ramp spell (Llanowar Elves/Rampant Growth). Turn 2: Develop mana with ramp creatures/spells, potentially play a second land, and set up early threats like Sylvan Caryatid or Karametra's Acolyte. Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for removal (W/B) and ramp (G/U) by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Goblin/Aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in Heroic Intervention, Beast Within, Path to Exile, and Fertid Groundskeeper. Against Control/Combo (Prismari, Y'shtola): Bring in Exsanguinate, Cauldron Familiar, Phyrexian Altar, and Swords to Plowshares. Against Midrange (Night's Blessed, Ur-Dragon): Bring in Thran Dynamo, Mana Crypt, Growth Spiral, and Kodama's Reach. Keep Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, and core removal in all games.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for ramp, removal, and casting Atraxa. Don't overcommit to the board if you lack answers. Use the graveyard effectively for Exsanguinate or Cauldron Familiar combos. Keep hand size optimal by trading removal for threats when ahead and holding counterspells/protection for key threats.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal to handle opposing answers or when opponents are tapped out. Hold back if the board is stacked or if you lack interaction. Prioritize keeping Atraxa alive over pumping weak creatures, and use Heroic Intervention or Path to Exile to protect it from targeted removal.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Day of Judgment), targeted removal, and counterspells. Note opponent's mana base and whether they're going wide or tall. Track graveyard content for combo threats and monitor life totals to calculate Exsanguinate or combat damage requirements.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Atraxa with multiple +1/+1 counters, Cauldron Familiar + Phyrexian Altar + Ashnod's Altar for infinite life/mana, or Exsanguinate for lethal. Push combat with pumped creatures when you have protection or removal to handle answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Atraxa on board with 3+ counters, removal to handle answers, and a clear path to lethal. Also commit when opponents are tapped out, low on life, or have exhausted their interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock (e.g., Blood Moon, Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) with no way to break it, and you're behind on board/life. Also concede if you're out of resources against a combo that can win on the spot or if you have no comeback against a stabilized control deck.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats (Sylvan Caryatid, Karametra's Acolyte) and split your investment. Cast Atraxa in response to counterspells if possible, or use protection (Heroic Intervention, Path to Exile on self). Avoid overcommitting to a single large threat.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal to kill ramp creatures before they stabilize. Out-value them with card draw and efficient removal, and prioritize keeping Atraxa alive to out-scale their late-game.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and Heroic Intervention. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and preserve Atraxa. Pivot to Atraxa once stabilized, and use Fertid Groundskeeper to close out games against wide boards.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with multiple threats to force inefficient block trades. Use Exsanguinate or combo finishers to bypass control. Force them to use resources on multiple fronts and punish overextended boards with combat damage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by resource efficiency and card advantage. Prioritize keeping Atraxa alive, use removal to clear opposing threats, and leverage Cauldron Familiar combos if ahead. Track counters and protect your own, and be willing to trade creatures to maintain board presence.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against fast decks, prioritize interaction and early threats. Against slow decks, bring in combo pieces and late-game finishers. Always keep a balance of removal, ramp, and protection. Adjust land count if needed (usually 36-37 lands in EDH) and consider bringing in additional hate cards against specific archetypes like Goblin tribal or control.
notes: Common mistakes include overextending without answers, neglecting to protect Atraxa, mismanaging mana for colored requirements, and ignoring graveyard synergies. Unique interactions: Cauldron Familiar + Phyrexian Altar + Ashnod's Altar = infinite life/mana. Exsanguinate can win games from the graveyard. Watch out for Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void, or Blood Moon. Always track opponent's life total for Exsanguinate and combat math.

philosophy: Accelerate into massive mana by casting cheap noncreature spells, leveraging Kinnan’s self-damage to fuel a high-impact creature finisher. Wins by out-resourcing midrange and combo decks, and racing aggressive token or ramp strategies before they stabilize.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2–3 lands (including basics for Kinnan’s damage or fetchable sources), at least one mana accelerator (Sol Ring, Llanowar Elves, or similar), and Kinnan. Mulligan hands with 0–1 lands, multiple copies of the same ramp spell, or no way to cast Kinnan by turn 2–3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Sol Ring or Llanowar Elves. Turn 2: Play land, cast Kinnan (or another ramp spell if Kinnan is in hand). Sequence lands and noncreature spells to maximize mana output while monitoring life total. Prioritize casting cheap cantrips and mana rocks to build your engine before deploying threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in early removal, life gain, or combat tricks. Against control/combo: Bring in card advantage, protection, or faster finishers. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, counters, or hate cards that disrupt noncreature spell chains.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for casting noncreature spells versus holding up for your finisher. Avoid over-committing to cantrips if you cannot follow up with a win condition. Manage life total carefully, as Kinnan’s damage accumulates quickly.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you have sufficient mana to cast your finisher and a plan to protect it or follow up. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers, heavy interaction, or a clear path to stabilization.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), counters, and targeted removal. Note when opponents play cheap interaction versus late-game answers, and track life totals to avoid dying to Kinnan’s damage or combat.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Deploy high-impact creatures like Krenko, Mob Boss or similar token generators when you have 10+ mana. Use remaining mana to pump tokens, cast additional spells, or swing for lethal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to casting your finisher, the opponent has no sweepers, and you can win before they stabilize or counter your plan.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are behind on board, the opponent has a hard lock (sweepers plus counters), you are at 1 life with no stabilization, or the matchup is inherently unwinnable post-sideboard.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple spells to beat counters, or cast your finisher when the opponent has no mana up. Utilize protection, evasion, or split spells if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Race them by deploying threats earlier. Do not get bogged down in a mana race; use removal on their key ramp pieces and pressure their life total.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal or life gain, then pivot to your game plan. Avoid overextending into combat and focus on shutting down their early threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats and use cantrips to dig past counters. Win before they can set up their late-game plan or find their sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing, having better interaction, or deploying your finisher first. Track each other’s noncreature spell counts, life totals, and interaction to time your plays correctly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, generally add targeted removal, life gain, and hate pieces against popular aggro and combo strategies. Reduce redundant ramp spells and adjust based on metagame shifts, such as adding evasion or protection if sweepers become prevalent.
notes: Common mistakes include forgetting Kinnan’s damage, over-casting noncreature spells without a finisher, and mismanaging life total. Unique interactions: Kinnan’s damage can be mitigated with early life gain or damage prevention. Synergizes heavily with cheap cantrips and mana rocks. Always track opponent interaction to time your finisher.

philosophy: Generate a relentless stream of flying Spirit tokens by casting cheap non-creature spells, leveraging card advantage and tempo to overwhelm opponents or close out games quickly. Wins by out-valuing opponents through spell density and token pressure, particularly against decks that struggle with flying threats or lack efficient removal.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for R/U/W), Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, and at least one cheap cantrip/draw spell. Keep Kykar only if paired with a mana accelerator and a cheap non-creature spell. Mulligan to lands if you have no acceleration or cantrips, or if you draw 4+ lands with no play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet if possible. Turn 2: Cast a cheap cantrip to dig for Kykar or more spells. If Kykar is on the battlefield, immediately cast a cheap non-creature spell to generate a token. Sequence mana rocks first, then cantrips, then Kykar.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in cheap counterspells, bounce spells, or targeted removal. Against control: Bring in more cantrips, cheap threats, or combo pieces. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counterspells, or targeted removal. Against mirror: Bring in extra interaction, card advantage, or token doublers.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize maintaining card advantage; never cast Kykar without a plan to follow up with non-creature spells. Manage mana to keep open for interaction or to chain cantrips. Use the graveyard strategically if the deck utilizes recursion or storm, but avoid flooding it if it becomes a liability.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit tokens when you have card advantage or removal to clear blockers. Hold back if you are low on resources or facing sweepers. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently against non-flying decks and preserve your commander.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation), targeted removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents are holding up mana for counterspells or removal to time your Kykar casts and follow-up spells accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A board full of flying Spirits, often boosted by pump spells or doublers. Alternatively, a storm combo finish or a direct damage spell. Get there by chaining cantrips to generate tokens and stabilize the board.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, protection for Kykar, and no immediate sweepers in play. Also commit when you can close the game before the opponent's next turn or when you have out-resourced them in spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are facing a hard lock (Stax, heavy counterspell) with no way to break it and are losing life to zero. Concede if you are down to one life, have no flying threats, and lack answers to the opponent's win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple spells to out-value counterspells. Employ protection (Hexproof, Shroud, Flash), or cast Kykar during your opponent's turn if possible. Use cantrips to dig through counterspells and maintain spell density.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with tokens. Use cheap removal or bounce to neutralize their mana rocks. Do not get bogged down in a long game; race them to lethal before their late-game threats come online.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap counterspells, bounce, or early token generation to block flying threats. Use removal on key attackers. Dig for board wipes if available, and prioritize surviving the early turns to stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw and cheap spells. Use protection for Kykar. Pressure the board to force them to use removal on tokens rather than your key spells, and dig for combo pieces or direct answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by card advantage and interaction. Protect your Kykar. Use counterspells to protect your own spells. Trade tokens efficiently and prioritize keeping your commander alive to out-value them over the long game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your 99 to address the current meta. Bring in more interaction against control/stax, more removal against aggro, and more card draw against grindy decks. Remove redundant cards or those that do not fit the current meta. Always keep at least one mana accelerator and a cantrip in your main deck.
notes: Kykar triggers on any non-creature spell, including instants, sorceries, artifacts, enchantments, and lands with ETB effects. Synergizes well with cheap cantrips, storm, and token doublers. Remember that token generation is a trigger, so it goes on the stack and can be responded to. Common mistakes include casting Kykar without a follow-up spell, overcommitting to the board without protection, and ignoring the opponent's mana state.

philosophy: Derevi operates as a tempo-control engine that trades card disadvantage for massive tempo swings and resource generation. The goal is to stabilize early with cheap interaction, then leverage untap effects and combat tricks to out-value opponents and close games through incremental advantage or overwhelming combat steps.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep any hand with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks) and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or a cheap creature/spell). Prioritize hands with early interaction or mana acceleration. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no early plays, or 2+ lands with only high-cost spells. Keep hands with discard spells if facing control/combo.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet, or drop a cheap threat/interaction. Turn 2: Develop mana base, cast a one-mana spell, or establish board presence. If you have Derevi, aim to cast her by turn 3-4 once you have WU mana and a land to untap for tempo. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast her without stalling, and always leave mana open for protection or combat tricks.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), early interaction (Dovin's Veto, Swords to Plowshares), and discard. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in more counterspells, card advantage (Mystic Remora, Fact or Fiction), and protection. Against mirror: Keep interaction, bring in extra value engines or protection for Derevi. Always keep at least one versatile answer for flexibility.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Manage mana carefully; don't overcommit to untap effects if you'll be tapped out for combat tricks. Use the upkeep draw/discard as a filter—keep high-impact spells, discard low-impact ones. Utilize discard outlets or card draw engines to mitigate the upkeep downside and maintain hand size. Prioritize untapping artifacts or creatures that enable immediate value over untapping lands unless you're mana-flooded.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction or untap outlets ready to protect them or enable combat tricks. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or counterspells. Derevi herself is fragile; protect her or use her untap ability to bounce threats. Don't overextend into known removal; trade efficiently and preserve resources for the late game.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Cyclonic), counterspells, and graveyard strategies. Note when opponents tap out for big spells—this is your window to attack or cast Derevi. Track their hand size; if they're low on answers, pressure harder. Pay attention to mana development; opponents running ramp or combo will often show early acceleration, signaling a need for early disruption.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Derevi with combat tricks, untapped artifact/creature combos, or a sudden board wipe + value engine. Win through incremental combat damage or overwhelming value from untap synergies. If running token or golem synergies, untap multiple threats for a lethal attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Derevi untapped, a combat trick ready, and the opponent has no sweepers or counterspells. Also commit when you can untap multiple threats for a lethal attack, or when you've forced the opponent to use their last interaction on a cheaper threat.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to one life, have no interaction, and the opponent has a proven win condition (e.g., Ur-Dragon combo, Krenko token storm). Also concede if you're locked out of mana, have a dead hand against a fast deck, or are facing a mirror where you're clearly losing the value exchange.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multi-target spells, or cast Derevi when they're tapped out. Utilize counterspell protection or force them to use resources on cheaper threats first. If they're holding up counters, apply pressure with multiple small threats to drain their resources.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use discard to hit their ramp spells, and bring in artifact hate or early interaction to slow their acceleration. Don't let them stabilize; punish their mana development with efficient removal or board wipes.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early interaction and board wipes. Don't overextend; stabilize with cheap blockers or bounce effects, then close the game with Derevi's value. Use discard to hit their key threats and disrupt their curve.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card advantage and incremental threats. Protect Derevi, use counterspells to protect your key plays, and pressure when they're low on resources. Force them to interact with your cheaper threats to exhaust their hand.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the value exchange. Protect your Derevi, force them to use interaction on your cheaper threats, and leverage the upkeep draw/discard to out-resource them. Prioritize untap synergies that out-value theirs, and track their hand size to know when to commit or hold back.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against fast decks, prioritize early interaction and sweepers. Against control/combo, increase counterspells, card advantage, and hate cards. In mirrors, focus on protection and value engines. Always keep at least one versatile answer (like Cyclonic Rift or Swords to Plowshares) for flexibility. Track opponent tendencies and adjust your sideboard strategy accordingly.
notes: Derevi's upkeep draw/discard is a double-edged sword; mitigate it with card draw engines or discard outlets. Don't overuse her untap ability on lands if you need mana for combat tricks. Watch for 'tap out' windows—opponents often leave themselves vulnerable after casting big spells. Common mistake: casting Derevi too early without protection or untap targets, leaving her vulnerable to removal. Always prioritize protecting her over overcommitting to a single untap effect.

philosophy: Leverage Dragonspeaker Shaman and Savage Ventmaw to rapidly pump Atarka, World Render into a massive, unblockable threat that closes out games before control or combo decks can stabilize. It functions as a tempo-aggro dragon deck that wins by overwhelming the board with a single, game-ending dragon.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (Forests/Mountains), Dragonspeaker Shaman, Savage Ventmaw, or Atarka. Prioritize hands that can play a Shaman on turn 1 or 2, or Ventmaw on turn 3. Mulligan if you have 3+ lands with no threats, or only Atarka with no mana/support.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Forest or Mountain. If you have Shaman, play it. Turn 2: Play a Mountain, cast Shaman if you didn't, or play a 2-drop dragon. Use Shaman's enter-the-battlefield counter or sacrifice to pump Atarka. Turn 3: Cast Savage Ventmaw or Atarka, pump with remaining resources, swing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes or removal. Against control/combo (Ur-Dragon, Prismari): Bring in more threats, card draw, or direct damage. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and counterspells if available, or additional pump spells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Don't overcommit to board if you can't win the exchange. Save pump spells/shamans for Atarka. Manage mana to ensure you can cast Atarka and pump it in the same turn if possible.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Atarka only when you can protect it or swing for lethal. Hold back pump resources if the opponent has sweepers. Use Ventmaw as a finisher or pump source depending on board state.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (like Wrath of God equivalents), targeted removal (Lightning Helix, etc.), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for answers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Atarka with +3/+3 from Shaman or +4/+4 from Ventmaw. Sometimes a pumped Ventmaw itself wins.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have Atarka on board with a pump source, or when you can swing with a 7/7+ for lethal. Go all-in if the opponent has no sweepers or answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one card, opponent has a board wipe ready, or you're facing a combo deck that's already at 10 life. Also if you're out of threats and they have control elements.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats (Shaman) to pressure before they can counter. Use pump spells in response to counters if possible. Don't overextend into known counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure. Don't let them stabilize. Use removal on their mana sources if available.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Play removal, use Atarka as a clock-ender, but be careful of sweepers. Sometimes you need to trade efficiently.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early, use pump spells to bypass removal. Have a backup plan if Atarka is removed.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange of pump spells. Shaman is key. Try to out-value them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame. Against fast decks, bring in more early interaction. Against slow decks, bring in more threats and card advantage. Generally, keep the core dragon/pump package but add format-specific hate.
notes: Common mistake: Pumping the wrong dragon. Always prioritize Atarka. Watch out for sacrifice outlets. Shaman's ETB counter is free value. Ventmaw can be a finisher or pump source. Don't forget to check for flying/trample synergy.

philosophy: This deck leverages Mishra's token generation and Emry's graveyard recursion to flood the board with Golem artifacts, winning through attrition and overwhelming combat steps. It excels against midrange and combo decks that overcommit to single threats or lack efficient sweepers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize duals/signets for Grixis), at least one artifact ramp (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or cheap artifacts), and a path to casting Mishra or Emry by turn 3-4. Against aggro, keep early removal or blockers; against control, keep card draw or protection.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet or a cheap artifact. Turn 2: Play land, cast another artifact or ramp, potentially casting Mishra if ahead on mana. Prioritize establishing artifact count and mana base while preserving hand for Mishra triggers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Krenko/Blech/Loafing Pest: Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift), early removal (Murder, Go Blank), and artifact hate. Against Prismari/Y'shtola/Ur-Dragon: Bring in card draw (Factoid, Mind's Desire), protection (Negate, Counterspell), and finishers. Against mirrors: Bring in recursion enablers, extra value artifacts, and targeted removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve artifacts in hand for Mishra triggers; use Emry to recycle high-CMV artifacts for value. Don't overextend into sweepers. Manage graveyard by protecting key artifacts or using them as fuel for Emry's recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or a board wipe ready. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or targeted removal. Use Golem tokens as blockers to preserve key artifacts and protect Emry.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Cyclonic Rift), targeted removal (Path, Murder), and combo wincons. Note when opponents tap out for big spells or hold up interaction, as this dictates when to cast Mishra or push combat.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Flood the board with Golems, pump them with +1/+1 counters or combat tricks, or use Emry to replay a high-impact artifact (like Thopter Foundry or Mindslaver) for a lethal turn. Mishra's trigger can also generate a final wave of tokens to close out games.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ artifacts in play/graveyard, enough mana to cast Mishra and follow-up, and opponents are low on removal or sweepers. Commit when you can trade tokens for their key permanents.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of artifacts, opponents have multiple sweepers, or you're facing a fast combo that can't be disrupted. Concede if your hand is empty and you lack a path to casting Mishra or Emry.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Cast artifacts in response to their spells, use cheap artifacts to bait counters, or rely on Mishra's trigger (pay {2} to make a token) which can't be countered. Use protection or haste to bypass counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, disrupt their mana sources with artifact hate or targeted removal, and win before they stabilize. Use Emry to out-value their ramp by recycling artifacts for incremental advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use early removal, board wipes, and blockers. Don't overcommit to value; stabilize first, then rebuild with Mishra's tokens. Protect Emry from targeted removal.
threat_responses.against_control: Value out with Emry recursion, use Mishra's tokens to pressure, and win through combat or combo finishers. Don't let them stabilize with sweepers; force trades and pressure their life total.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win via card advantage, better recursion, and protecting your commander. Use Emry to out-value their graveyard, time Mishra triggers to maximize token generation, and sideboard in targeted removal to handle their key artifacts.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends: against wide boards, bring in targeted removal and sweepers; against control, add card draw and protection. Always keep artifact ramp and recursion core. Swap in format-specific hate based on top archetypes, and prioritize cards that disrupt opponent's game plan while preserving your artifact synergy.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to Mishra without protection, ignoring graveyard protection for Emry, and mismanaging mana for the {2} trigger. Unique interaction: Golem tokens are artifacts, so they can be replayed via Emry if they hit the graveyard. Watch for 'destroy artifact' effects and plan accordingly. Always track opponent's mana to time your Mishra casts and combat.

philosophy: Jodah is a five-color value engine that converts mana acceleration into explosive spell casting from the top of your library. It wins by out-ramping opponents, casting high-impact spells for reduced cost, and leveraging card advantage to overwhelm the board or close out games with singular massive threats.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks for fixing), at least one mana rock or ramp spell (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, Farseek, Cultivate), and Jodah if possible. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no ramp, or only cheap spells with no way to cast them.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Sol Ring/Arcane Signet or play a ramp spell like Farseek/Cultivate. Turn 2: Play another land, cast a mana dork or second ramp spell. Prioritize getting Jodah online by turn 3-4. Sequence fetches into shock lands for five-color fixing.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes (Wrath of God, Damnation), removal (Path to Exile, Swords to Plowshares), and life gain. Against control/combo: Bring in protection (Counterspell, Negate, Teferi's Protection), card draw, and direct damage. Against mirror: Bring in extra value engines, more ramp, and targeted removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Mana is your primary resource; always have 5+ mana available to trigger Jodah's full potential. Manage hand by holding up mana for protection or big spells. Graveyard is generally a liability, so consider graveyard hate if needed, though five-color ramp usually doesn't run it.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection or can follow up. Use Jodah to cast board wipes when opponents have too many creatures. Don't overcommit to a single massive spell if you can't protect it.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (opponents will try to wipe your board before you can cast your win con). Track mana development (are they ahead on ramp?). Note when opponents hold up mana (likely holding counters or removal).

endgame_tactics.finishers: Jodah casting a massive creature (e.g., Emrakul, The Aeons Torn; Teferi, Hero of Dominaria; or a five-color win con like Worldslayer) from the top of the deck. Sometimes combo finishes with cards like Storm the Festival or specific commander synergies.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have 5+ mana, Jodah is safe, and you can cast a game-ending spell or combo. Also commit when you can protect the spell (counters, protection, or opponent has no interaction).
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're behind on mana development, have no way to protect Jodah, and the opponent has multiple sweepers or lock pieces. Also if you're dead to aggro and can't stabilize.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Jodah's ability to cast spells with multiple targets or split effects. Cast spells in response to counterspells if possible. Use protection spells. Sometimes just out-value by casting multiple smaller spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Match or exceed their ramp. Use Jodah to cast their ramp spells if they're on top of your deck. Prioritize board wipes to stop their big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and board wipes. Use Jodah to cast a big creature or sweeper. Gain life if possible. Don't overextend.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value with Jodah's card advantage. Cast spells that are hard to counter (multikind, split effects). Pressure with threats they can't answer. Use Jodah to cast their own control spells if they're on top of your deck.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Out-ramp and out-value. Prioritize protecting Jodah. Use targeted removal on their Jodah or key ramp. Win by casting more high-impact spells per turn.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and disruption. Against aggro, prioritize sweepers and removal. Against control, bring in protection and card advantage. Always keep 2-3 lands and 1-2 ramp pieces. Adjust your main deck if you're consistently losing to a specific archetype.
notes: Jodah's ability only works on the top card, so deck consistency is key. Use fetch lands and cantrips to dig for Jodah or key spells. Be careful of 'top-deck' variance; don't rely on it for critical turns. Common mistake: overcommitting to a single massive spell without protection. Another: neglecting five-color mana fixing early on.

philosophy: A resilient midrange strategy that leverages sacrifice outlets and -1/-1 counter mechanics to exponentially grow a board of zombies, culminating in a massively pumped Skullbriar that swings for lethal. It wins by out-grinding resource-heavy decks with recursive threats and out-swinging aggressive decks with resilient, growing attackers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one black and one green source or reliable fixing), a turn-1 land, and either a cheap zombie generator, Corpsejack Menace, or a key sacrifice outlet. Mulligan if you have no lands, only single-color lands with no fixing, or a hand full of high-cost spells without a turn-2 play.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Develop mana and drop a cheap zombie or Corpsejack Menace if possible. Turn 3-4: Cast Skullbriar, then use sacrifice outlets to feed it or generate tokens. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Corpsejack Menace by turn 3-4 and Skullbriar by turn 4-5. Prioritize fetching/drawing fixing if your mana base is clunky.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in targeted removal, board wipes, and life gain to stabilize. Against control: Bring in more resilient threats, card draw, and protection spells. Against combo: Bring in graveyard hate, discard, and fast interaction. Against mirror: Bring in additional sacrifice outlets, +1/+1 counter boosters, and efficient removal to out-resource.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana between casting Corpsejack Menace, pump spells, and sacrifice outlets on the same turn to maximize growth. Avoid overcommitting to Skullbriar if you lack protection; maintain a board of backup zombies. Use your graveyard strategically—don't sacrifice everything if you need recursion for the late game.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Skullbriar when you have a clear path to lethal or when the opponent has no sweepers or targeted hate. Hold back if they have board wipes or removal in hand. Use smaller zombies to block or trade, preserving Skullbriar for the kill swing.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (sweepers), targeted removal (e.g., Lightning Helix, Doom Blade), and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void). Note if the opponent is ramping or locking you out. Track their life total to calculate lethal with Skullbriar's current size and anticipate their interaction windows.

endgame_tactics.finishers: A fully pumped Skullbriar (often 10+ power) swinging with backup zombies or using Invigorating Surge to swing for lethal. Sometimes a mass pump or sacrifice outlet combo wins the game outright.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers, and you have enough resources to rebuild if Skullbriar dies. Also commit when you're at low life and need to swing immediately to close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of the game (no lands, graveyard hate, no removal), if the opponent has a guaranteed win condition on their next turn, or if you're facing a mirror with no way to break their board or out-value them.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter (e.g., mana rocks, sacrifice outlets). Save removal for their counters. Use pump spells in response to counters if needed. Consider sideboarding in more uncounterable threats or protection.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap zombies. Use removal on their mana sources. Don't let them stabilize; trade efficiently and go wide to outpace their late-game threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and life gain. Use Skullbriar as a blocker early, then pivot to offense. Don't overextend; wait for a clear path to swing and use Invigorating Surge defensively if needed.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats. Use card draw to out-value them. Save removal for their key pieces. Go wide with zombies to overwhelm their single-target removal and force them to use sweepers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by having more efficient sacrifice outlets, better card draw, or more protection for Skullbriar. Trade efficiently and aim to out-resource them in the late game. Prioritize board wipes and targeted removal to break their momentum.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, bring in more card draw and protection. Against aggro, prioritize removal and life gain. In mirrors, focus on efficiency and board wipes. Always keep a balance between threat generation and interaction, and ensure your mana base can support the shifted spell distribution.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting Skullbriar without protection, ignoring graveyard hate, and mismanaging mana for Corpsejack Menace timing. Unique interactions: Corpsejack Menace combined with sacrifice outlets creates exponential growth. Invigorating Surge can be used defensively or offensively. Remember that Skullbriar dies to combat damage, so timing swings is crucial. Always track the opponent's life total and interaction to avoid swinging into a sweeper or removal.

philosophy: Cast noncreature spells to generate a swarm of 1/1 flying Spirits, using them to block, attack, or sacrifice for card advantage. Win by overwhelming opponents with board presence and out-drawing them through spell synergy, particularly against spell-heavy combo and token/artifact archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Abigale or early token generators (Lecturing Scornmage/Scolding Administrator), and at least one cheap noncreature spell (cantrip, removal, or tutor). Mulligan if you have no lands, only expensive spells, or no way to generate initial value. Prioritize hands that can cast a noncreature spell on turn 1 or 2.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or removal to trigger token generation. Turn 2: Develop board with another land + spell, or play a Scornmage/Administrator to stabilize. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast your first noncreature spell on curve. Use early tokens to block aggressively or trade into opponent's threats.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in more removal (Fatal Push, Go for the Throat), board wipes (Damnation, Vindicate), and protection. Against control/combo (Prismari, Y'shtola): Bring in more card draw, disruption (Thoughtseize, Duress), and finishers. Against mirror: Bring in more targeted removal and protection for Abigale.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting noncreature spells over attacking with tokens unless necessary. Use Spirit sacrifice for card draw when you're low on resources or facing a threat. Manage mana to ensure you can respond to threats while maintaining spell flow. Avoid overcommitting to the board without card advantage.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have card advantage or removal to handle responses. Hold back if you're vulnerable to board wipes or if you need to preserve Abigale for late-game value. Use flying Spirits to control the air and force unfavorable blocks.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on Abigale, and opponent's mana sources. Key tells: Opponent tapping for multiple colors often signals a big spell or wipe. Track how many noncreature spells they've cast to anticipate their board state and plan your sacrifices accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Abigale + token swarm, or a big removal/wipe to clear the board and attack with flying Spirits. Commander damage is also viable. Use card draw to find finishers when needed.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, opponent is low on removal, or you can safely attack with multiple flying Spirits without risking Abigale. Commit when you can trade tokens for value or close the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of lands, opponent has a win condition you can't interact with (e.g., infinite combo), or you're consistently one-shot by aggro without stabilization. Concede if Abigale is removed and you lack follow-up.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: B/W lacks hard counters, so use cheap, efficient spells, split casts, or sacrifice tokens to draw into answers. Use Thoughtseize/Duress to remove key counterspells. Cast spells in response to counters or use instant-speed cantrips to maintain pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on mana dorks, and don't overcommit to the board. Use board wipes to reset. Force them to interact with your board while they develop mana.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and board wipes. Use flying Spirits to block efficiently. Protect Abigale from early aggression. Trade tokens for their threats and slow the game down.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with spell synergy, use card draw to out-value them, and threaten with flying Spirits. Don't let them stabilize with cheap removal. Force them to use interaction on your spells while you build a board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key. Use targeted removal on their Abigale or key engines. Prioritize card draw and board presence. Protect your commander. Win by out-resolving them in spell wars and trading efficiently.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against spell-heavy decks, bring in more disruption and card draw. Against artifact/token decks, bring in artifact hate and board wipes. In mirrors, prioritize protection and targeted removal. Always keep a balance between interaction and value generation.
notes: Abigale's ability triggers on cast, not on resolution, so it works with instant-speed spells. Synergizes well with cheap cantrips and removal. Avoid casting too many spells in a single turn if you can't follow up, as it leaves you vulnerable to wipes. Common mistake: forgetting to sacrifice Spirits for draw when you're low on resources. Unique interaction: Sacrificing a Spirit for draw can be done in response to a removal spell to replace it.

philosophy: A flexible G/U/W midrange/control hybrid that leverages Ms. Bumbleflower as a versatile engine for mana acceleration, token generation, and anti-token disruption. Wins by out-valuing opponents through efficient removal, card advantage, and strategic Saproling floods that overwhelm board states or enable favorable sweepers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including fetches/shocks if in deck), or 1 land plus 2+ mana accelerants (Arcane Signet, Sol Ring, etc.). Keep hands with turn-2 interaction (Swords to Plowshares, early removal, or countermagic) and a turn-3 play (Ms. Bumbleflower or a 2-drop). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, pure curve decks, or no early interaction/mana sources.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land. Turn 2: Cast a 2-drop or Arcane Signet. Turn 3: Cast Ms. Bumbleflower. Sequence mana carefully: if ahead, tap for G/U to develop threats; if behind, consider early sacrifice to flood the board with Saprolings for blocking or to enable removal. Land sequencing prioritizes fetching/shocking into untapped duals when possible, otherwise standard 3-color basics are fine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against token decks (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in board sweepers (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift, Mass Polymorph), extra removal, and anti-token hate. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed, Ur-Dragon): Bring in card draw, protection spells, and resilient threats. Against aggro: Bring in early removal, life gain, and board stalls. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, hand disruption, and additional token generators to out-value.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve Ms. Bumbleflower for both mana generation and token generation; avoid overcommitting to Saproling floods without removal or sweepers ready. Use Swords to Plowshares efficiently on key threats, and manage hand resources to maintain interaction through the midgame.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or sweepers ready to handle their response. Hold back if they have heavy interaction or sweepers in hand. Use Saprolings as flexible blockers or to trade favorably with key creatures, preserving your board state for value plays.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Cyclonic), targeted removal (Swords, Go for the Throat), and combo wincons. Note when opponents are holding up mana for counterspells or instant-speed interaction. Track graveyard for reanimation or recursion threats, and monitor life totals for aggressive pushes.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ms. Bumbleflower token floods combined with board sweepers, or a late-game resilient threat protected by Saprolings. Card advantage engines that out-value opponents and force unfavorable trades.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their threats, and they lack sweepers. Commit when you can safely flood the board knowing you can clear it or trade favorably, or when you can close the game before they stabilize.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when you are down to one life, the opponent has a combo wincon in hand or graveyard, you have no interaction, and your board is wiped. Also concede when consistently losing attrition wars with dead hands or when the matchup is mathematically unwinnable.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use mana rocks to cast spells at instant speed when they are tapped out. Avoid overcommitting to single large spells; instead, use smaller threats, token floods, and split strategies to overwhelm counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure and use removal on their mana sources. Do not let them stabilize; trade efficiently and force them to spend resources on board development rather than acceleration.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal, life gain, and using Saprolings as blockers. Hold back Ms. Bumbleflower for mana or blocking until needed, and stabilize before attempting to go off.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and card draw to out-value. Force them to use removal on low-value targets, and use token floods to clog their board and enable favorable trades.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is paramount. Use targeted removal on their key pieces, out-value with superior token synergies, and control the board with well-timed sweepers. Prioritize card advantage and hand disruption to out-resource them.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards matchup-specific hate while maintaining core flexibility. Against token-heavy metas, prioritize sweepers and anti-token effects. Against control/combo, add card advantage and protection. Maintain a balance of removal and interaction, and avoid overcommitting to one side to preserve adaptability.
notes: Common mistakes include sacrificing Ms. Bumbleflower too early without a plan, overcommitting to token floods without removal, and ignoring opponent mana states. Unique interactions: Ms. Bumbleflower's sacrifice triggers go on the stack, allowing responses. Synergizes well with token doublers, sweepers, and card draw. Watch for sacrifice hate or destroy effects, and remember that Saprolings can be used to enable sacrifice outlets or block key creatures.

philosophy: Terra, Herald of Hope is a spell-synergy engine that generates a relentless swarm of flying Spirits with every spell cast. The deck aims to out-value opponents through card advantage, recursive threats, and resilient board presence, winning by overwhelming the board with evasive tokens or replaying Terra via recursion to close out games.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for B/R/W), Arcane Signet, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play. Prioritize hands with 3+ lands if they include a turn-2 play. Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, or 4+ lands with no early impact or mana fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cantrip or disruption if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast Terra if mana allows, or play a cheap spell to start the token engine. If holding Terra, consider playing a cheap spell first to generate a token, then cast Terra to maximize triggers on your opponent's turn.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes, spot removal, and artifact hate. Against control/combo (Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in more disruption, graveyard hate, and additional recursion protection. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and card draw.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Keep mana flexible for instant-speed responses and casting spells to fuel Terra. Don't overcommit to the board if you can't protect it. Use the graveyard strategically; keep Terra or key threats in hand if the graveyard is unsafe.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or a board wipe ready. Hold back if opponents have sweepers or targeted hate. Use flying tokens to trade efficiently against non-flying blockers and preserve your key creatures.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on Terra, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents save removal for your key threats. Track graveyard contents for Reanimate targets and be aware of opponent's mana development.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Reanimating Terra to cast multiple spells in a single turn, generating a lethal swarm. Also, direct damage spells or combat with flying tokens when opponents lack blockers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have graveyard protection, enough mana to cast multiple spells, and opponents lack sweepers. Also when you can close the game in 1-2 turns with a clear path to combat or spell triggers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to 1 life with no recovery options, if your key recursion targets are permanently removed, or if you're facing a combo that will win before you can stabilize or generate enough value.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells, cantrips, and instant-speed recursion. Cast Terra after clearing the stack or with protection. Bait counters with low-value spells before committing your engine.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, disrupt mana sources, and use removal on key ramp creatures. Don't let them get too far ahead; force them to interact with your board before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Use flying tokens to block, spot removal on key attackers, and stabilize with board wipes. Don't overextend into combat; prioritize survival and out-value them in the late game.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with spell density, use recursion to bypass graveyard hate, and pressure with flying tokens. Force them to use resources on multiple threats rather than focusing on one.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize graveyard protection, use targeted removal on opponent's Terra, and out-cast them with better card draw. Control the stack and force unfavorable trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. If aggro is prevalent, increase sweepers and artifact hate. If control/combo is up, add more disruption and graveyard hate. Always keep 1-2 recursion targets and disruption in. Tailor your sideboard to the specific top archetypes you face most often.
notes: Terra's ability triggers on any spell, including instants and sorceries, so use cheap cantrips and removal to fuel it. Reanimate is a key win-con but vulnerable to graveyard hate; have backup plans like direct damage or combat. Avoid overcommitting to the board without protection. Watch for board wipes; keep a hand ready to rebuild. Don't forget to use Terra's activated ability to bounce tokens for card draw when you're low on resources.

philosophy: This deck leverages the synergy between Eriette of the Charmed Apple, Sram, Senior Edificer, and All That Glitters to generate a relentless swarm of flying Angels and Soldiers by casting noncreature spells. It wins by outpacing combo and control with incremental value and evasion, while overwhelming aggro with early board presence, targeted removal, and combat advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals or signets for fixing), at least one commander (Eriette/Sram) or All That Glitters, and 2-3 cheap noncreature spells (instants, sorceries, artifacts, or enchantments costing ≤3 mana). Mulligan if you have only lands, only creatures, or no way to cast noncreature spells in the first three turns.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap mana accelerator or instant/sorcery if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another noncreature spell or a cheap artifact/enchantment. If you have Eriette or Sram on the battlefield, prioritize casting a noncreature spell the turn after they enter to immediately trigger token generation. Sequence mana rocks and ramp spells to ensure you can cast multiple noncreature spells per turn by turn 3-4.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Loafing Pest): Bring in more targeted removal, board wipes, and life gain. Against control/combo (Y'shtola, Prismari): Bring in additional interaction, counterspell hate, and direct damage. Against artifact/enchantment decks (Blech, Ur-Dragon): Bring in artifact/enchantment hate and protection spells. Against mirror: Bring in more commander protection and efficient removal.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize casting noncreature spells over developing creatures. Use card draw and mana acceleration to maintain hand size and mana availability. Don't overextend into sweepers; use the token army to trade favorably or apply pressure. Manage mana to have priority for instant-speed noncreature spells to trigger Eriette/Sram at key moments.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or board wipes ready to handle opponent responses. Hold back if facing sweepers or if you can't protect your commander. Use the token army to block aggressively or trade into larger threats. Prioritize board wipes when opponents have established significant board states.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes (especially black/white ones), targeted removal on Eriette/Sram, and opponent's combo pieces. Note when opponents hold up mana for counterspells or removal. Track graveyard contents for reanimation or recursion threats. Pay attention to when opponents cast noncreature spells, as they may be setting up their own token engines or win conditions.

endgame_tactics.finishers: The token army itself, often boosted by enchantments or equipment. Direct damage spells or combat via flying Angels. Sometimes a specific win-con like All That Glitters + Eriette + Angel's Grace or similar. Close the game by attacking with 5+ Angels or leveraging a board wipe + token generation combo.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have enough removal to handle threats, or when the board is clear enough to attack with 5+ Angels. Go all-in when you can close the game in 1-2 turns or when you've out-resourced the opponent in noncreature spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're down to one life, facing a locked-out control/combo deck with no interaction, or if your commander is permanently removed with no way to recover. Concede if you're consistently drawing dead hands or if the metagame matchup is insurmountable without specific sideboard cards.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, redundant noncreature spells. Cast multiple spells in a row to overwhelm counters. Use instant-speed spells to trigger Eriette before the counter resolves. Consider artifact/enchantment ramp that doesn't target or uses alternative costs.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Early interaction (removal on mana dorks/rituals). Board wipes when they have few threats. Don't overextend into their big finishers. Use the token army to trade favorably and apply pressure before they stabilize.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early removal, board wipes, and life gain. Use tokens to block and trade. Stabilize before going wide. Prioritize protecting your commander and maintaining card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with cheap noncreature spells. Don't let them stabilize. Use card advantage engines. Attack with flying Angels to bypass ground blockers. Force them to use resources on your tokens rather than their win condition.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key. Prioritize keeping Eriette/Sram alive. Use removal efficiently. Win by having more noncreature spells in hand or better token synergies. Track graveyard and hand size to out-value the opponent.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your 10-card sideboard to target the top metagame threats. Against token/goblin decks, prioritize board wipes and targeted removal. Against control/combo, bring in more interaction and direct damage. Against artifact/enchantment decks, bring in hate spells and protection. Always keep core noncreature spell generators and commander protection in the main deck.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to creatures instead of casting noncreature spells, forgetting that Eriette triggers on any noncreature spell (including instants/sorceries from hand or graveyard), and mismanaging mana for instant-speed triggers. All That Glitters is redundant with Eriette but provides backup and can be used for specific synergies. Watch for 'cast without targeting' effects to bypass counters. In multiplayer, consider commander damage and political dynamics when committing to the board.

philosophy: A four-color artifact synergy deck that leverages Breya’s mana generation and +1/+1 pumping abilities to fuel token swarms, combat tricks, and artifact synergies. It aims to stabilize early, out-value opponents through card advantage and board presence, and close games via combat damage or triggered abilities.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one mana rock (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, Mind Stone), and Breya or a key synergist (Myr Battlesphere, Thopter Assembly, Skullclamp). Prioritize hands with protection or value engines. Mulligan on 4+ lands with no ramp, or 3+ rocks with no lands.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, drop a mana rock if possible. Turn 2: Cast Breya or a synergist. Sequence lands to ensure Breya drops by turn 2 or 3. Tap Breya for mana and pump a key artifact to trigger ETB effects, stabilize, or set up a lethal threat. If you have a token doublers or artifact synergist, prioritize setting those up before overcommitting to combat.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in targeted removal (Swords to Plowshares, Go for the Throat), a board wipe (Wrath of God, Toxic Deluge), and life gain.
