Against control: Bring in card draw (Factoid, Mystic Remora), extra turns, or combo pieces. Against combo: Bring in artifact hate (Disenchant, Abrade) and fast removal. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, artifact hate, and extra value engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard mana for Breya’s pumping abilities and key synergies. Avoid overcommitting to pumps without a clear plan or protection. Keep interaction in hand for sweepers or targeted hate. Graveyard is secondary but watch for recursion or sacrifice outlets.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have protection (hexproof, indestructible, counters) or removal ready. Hold back if facing sweepers or heavy artifact hate. Use Breya to pump blockers for favorable trades or to block key threats.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath, Toxic Deluge), artifact hate (Disenchant, Abrade), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for big spells. Track if they’re specifically targeting Breya or your key synergists.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Pumping a high-impact artifact (Myr Battlesphere, Thopter Assembly, or a token generator) for lethal. Using Skullclamp + Breya to draw cards and stabilize. Triggering win conditions via artifact ETB or combat damage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, the opponent lacks interaction, or pumping will trigger a win condition. Commit when you can trade up or force a favorable combat math.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Concede if facing a dedicated artifact hate deck with no fallback plan, or if you’re down to a dead hand with no way to interact or win. Also fold if the opponent has a locked board and you lack removal or a win condition.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, efficient threats. Pump artifacts in response to counters. Use cantrips to dig through counters. Favor artifact-based win cons that don’t rely on casting spells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure. Use removal on their mana dorks. Don’t let them stabilize. Use sweepers if they get too big.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and board wipes. Use Breya to pump blockers. Gain life if possible. Don’t overextend into combat.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with efficient artifacts. Use Breya to pump and trade. Dig for combo pieces or extra turns. Force them to use interaction on low-value threats.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value engines and card draw win. Prioritize removing their Breya or key synergists. Use targeted removal over sweepers. Control the board with efficient pumps.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against artifact-heavy formats, bring in more hate. Against control, bring in card draw and extra turns. Against aggro, bring in removal and life gain. Generally, keep the core engine intact but swap in matchup-specific interaction.
notes: Common mistake: over-pumping without a plan, ignoring opponent’s interaction, or failing to protect Breya. Unique interaction: Breya’s abilities can target your own artifacts in response to triggers. Use Skullclamp for card advantage. Watch for artifact hate like Disenchant or Abrade. Protect Breya at all costs, as it’s the deck’s primary engine and win condition enabler.

philosophy: This deck aims to cast high-impact spells at drastically reduced costs by leveraging Morophon's ability to discount colored mana, supported by heavy generic mana acceleration and card draw. It wins by out-scaling opponents through exponential value generation, closing games with massive threats or game-ending spells that opponents cannot afford to answer.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one generic mana accelerator (e.g., Arcane Signet, Sol Ring, Mind Stone), and a cheap draw or cost-reduction spell. Drop hands with 3+ lands and no acceleration, 0-1 lands, or only colored mana sources without ramp. Prioritize hands that enable Turn 2 acceleration or early Morophon casting.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a generic mana rock or cheap ramp spell. Turn 2: Cast another accelerator or cheap spell, sequencing to ensure you have enough generic mana in play to maximize Morophon's discount. If you have Morophon and enough mana rocks, cast a mid-tier creature or spell at a steep discount. Always prioritize establishing a robust mana base that produces both colored and generic mana.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in early removal, board wipes, and life gain to stabilize. Against control/combo: Bring in card advantage engines, protection spells, and faster finishers to close games before they stabilize. Against mirror/ramp: Bring in artifact destruction, counterspell hate, and additional cost reducers to disrupt their acceleration and win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Hoard generic mana sources (mana rocks, ramp spells) to maximize Morophon's discount on colored spells. Use card draw engines to maintain hand size and find key pieces. Avoid overcommitting to Morophon without backup threats or interaction. Manage your graveyard carefully if opponents run hate, though the deck primarily relies on the stack and battlefield.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have 4+ mana rocks in play and enough card draw to sustain the turn. Hold back if opponents have sweepers, targeted removal, or if you're low on resources. Prioritize casting your biggest spells when opponents are tapped out or have no interaction available. Use Morophon to cast sweepers or large blockers when the board state demands stabilization.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal on Morophon, and opponents building up mana for their own win conditions. Note when opponents play cheap interaction versus late-game answers, and track their graveyard and library if they run combo or mill strategies. Pay attention to when they tap out for counterspells or protection, as this is your window to cast game-ending spells.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Cast massive creatures (e.g., Emrakul, Ulamog, Phyrexian Obliterator, or massive tribal finishers) or game-ending spells (e.g., Terminus, Wrath of God, or combo pieces) at near-zero cost. Use Morophon to cast these when you have 5+ mana rocks in play, effectively making them free or nearly free.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have sufficient mana rocks in play to cast your finisher at a massive discount, enough card draw to sustain the turn, and opponents have no clear interaction. Also commit when you can swing for lethal, cast a game-ending spell, or when you've forced all of your opponent's answers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're locked out of mana, Morophon is removed with no way to recast it, or you're facing a degenerate combo that resolves before you can stabilize. Also concede if you're down to a dead hand with no acceleration, or if you're consistently out-resourced in a mirror/ramp matchup with no comeback path.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap spells to trade counterspells, cast Morophon and big spells in response to their counters, or use protection/enlightened mentor effects. Cast multiple small spells to force counterspells, then drop the big one. If they have heavy counter suites, prioritize faster win conditions or artifact destruction to remove their mana rocks.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap threats, use artifact destruction to slow their mana base, and prioritize faster win conditions. Don't get out-scaled; close the game before they stabilize. Use Morophon to cast sweepers or large blockers to disrupt their board development.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early removal and board wipes. Use Morophon to cast a large blocker or sweeper. Gain life if possible. Don't overcommit to big spells early; stabilize first, then pivot to your win condition once the board is clear.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with multiple threats, use card draw to out-value them, and cast your finisher when they're tapped out or out of answers. Use cheap spells to force interaction. If they have heavy removal, use Morophon to cast your biggest spells in response to their answers or when they're tapped out.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Focus on resource denial (artifact destruction, counterspell hate), faster win conditions, and out-drawing them. Prioritize keeping Morophon safe and casting your biggest spells first. Track their mana rocks and discard or destroy them to slow their discount engine. Win the resource war by drawing more cards and casting more spells per turn.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against combo, bring in graveyard hate and faster interaction. Against control, bring in more card draw and protection. Against aggro, bring in sweepers and early removal. Always keep core mana acceleration and Morophon. In Commander, treat sideboarding as postboard adjustments; carry 10-15 flexible cards that address the most common threats while preserving your core game plan.
notes: Morophon's cost reduction only applies to the colored mana portion of spells; generic mana costs remain unchanged. Be careful of commander tax if Morophon leaves the battlefield, as recasting it will be expensive. Synergize with cards that reduce generic costs (e.g., Mind Stone, Sol Ring, mana rocks) and cards that generate generic mana. Avoid overcommitting to Morophon without backup threats. Watch for opponents running artifact hate or counterspells, as your engine relies heavily on keeping mana rocks on the battlefield.

philosophy: This deck leverages creature sacrifice outlets to fuel The Gitrog Monster’s card draw and discard, creating a self-sustaining engine of card advantage and graveyard recursion. It wins by out-valuing opponents through relentless resource generation, milling them out, or overwhelming them with resilient threats like Golgari Grave-Troll.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritize basics or fetchable duals), at least one sacrifice outlet (Icetill Explorer, Golgari Grave-Troll, or ritual/discard spells), and early interaction. Mulligan if you have zero lands, only non-basic lands without fetch support, or a hand of 3+ high-cost spells with no early plays.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap discard or sacrifice outlet if available. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast The Gitrog Monster or a sacrificial creature. Sequence mana to ensure black availability for discard/sacrifice and green for mana acceleration or threats. Prioritize establishing the sacrifice outlet before committing to the Monster.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in board wipes, efficient removal, and early discard. Against control/combo: Bring in graveyard hate, counterspells, and faster threats. Against mirror: Bring in additional recursion, targeted removal, and discard to win the resource war.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat your graveyard as a secondary hand; cycle creatures through sacrifice outlets to fuel Gitrog’s draw/discard loop. Balance mana between casting spells, playing lands, and holding creatures for sacrifice. Avoid over-investing in recursion if the board state allows you to simply sacrifice for immediate value.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have card advantage to answer responses or when opponents are low on interaction. Hold back if you’re resource-starved or facing a sweep. Use discard to disrupt key combo pieces or counterspells before they resolve.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Monitor opponents for graveyard hate, board wipes, and combo assembly. Note when players are protecting their graveyard or hoarding removal. Watch for mana bases that indicate ramp or control, and adjust your discard/removal targets accordingly.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Golgari Grave-Troll (provides a 4/4 body and recursion on death), milling opponents to zero, or attacking with a board of value. Use recursion to keep Grave-Troll or other threats in play while continuing to sacrifice for card advantage.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a card advantage lead, can answer responses, or opponents are at low life/mill threshold. Commit when you’ve established a board that can absorb one or two removal spells.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold when your engine is completely shut down (graveyard hate + board wipe), you’re out of resources, and opponents have established an unbreakable board or combo. Also concede if you’re consistently drawing dead hands with no interaction or sacrifice outlets.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to remove counterspells from hand, cast spells in response to counters (if possible), or rely on uncounterable threats and recursion. Don’t over-commit to one spell; spread risk across multiple threats.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and removal. Don’t let them get too far ahead in mana; use board wipes and efficient removal to reset the board. Prioritize targeting their mana sources and key ramp spells.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize early with discard, board wipes, and efficient removal. Don’t over-commit to the engine until you’ve stabilized the board. Use sacrifice outlets to fuel Gitrog while answering threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply pressure with sacrifice outlets and discard to disrupt their hand. Out-value them with card draw and recursion. Don’t over-commit to one threat; force them to use multiple answers.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing graveyard protection, discard, and targeted removal. Out-value them in card advantage and recursion. Use discard to remove their key threats and recursion spells.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, tailor your interaction to the metagame. Against control/combo, prioritize graveyard hate, counters, and faster threats. Against aggro, bring in board wipes, early removal, and discard. In mirrors, focus on recursion, targeted removal, and discard to win the resource war. Keep the core engine intact and adjust your land base if needed for mana fixing.
notes: Common mistakes include sacrificing too early without a plan, ignoring graveyard hate, and over-committing to The Gitrog Monster without protection. Unique interaction: Golgari Grave-Troll’s sacrifice can trigger The Gitrog Monster’s draw/discard, then you can use its death trigger to return itself or another creature, creating a recursive loop. Fetch lands can tutor for basics to enable Gitrog’s exile ability. Always track opponents’ graveyard sizes to know when milling becomes a win condition.

philosophy: This deck operates as a midrange-control hybrid that trades life for card advantage and board presence, aiming to out-grind opponents through efficient spell synergy, resource denial, and calculated trades. It wins by stabilizing early, leveraging Patron of the Vein and Sanctum Seeker for mana and pressure, and closing out games with Strefan's flying tokens and removal when opponents lack answers.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing duals/shocklands if available), at least one early interaction (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push, Go Blank, or Lightning Strike), and a key piece (Strefan, Patron, or Sanctum Seeker). Keep if you can play a land and a spell on turn 1, or two lands and a threat on turn 2. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with only high-cost spells, or hands with multiple Strefans but no early interaction.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize to disrupt combo/ramp, or drop Patron if you expect a slow game. Turn 2: Play a land, cast Sanctum Seeker for flexible mana, or play Strefan if you have the mana. Sequence spells to trigger Strefan's token generation while preserving creatures for Patron's sacrifice outlet. Avoid overextending if you lack removal.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): Bring in 3-4 direct damage spells (Lightning Strike, Go Blank), 2 artifact hate cards, and 1-2 life gain or board wipes. Against control/combo (Prismari, Y'shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in 2-3 graveyard hate (Liliana of the Veil, Rest in Peace), 2 additional Thoughtseizes, and 1-2 finishers (Bloodtithe Harvester or direct damage). Against mirror: Bring in 3-4 interaction spells and 2 hate bears to disrupt their key pieces.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Monitor your life total closely; Strefan's life loss is manageable early but becomes critical late-game. Use Patron's sacrifice outlet to draw cards and pump threats, but avoid sacrificing to opponents' enter-the-battlefield triggers or graveyard synergies. Use Sanctum Seeker to cast non-creature spells efficiently, and hoard removal for sweepers or key threats rather than trading into small blockers.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal ready or when the opponent's hand is empty. Hold back if they have sweepers, counterspells, or heavy interaction. Use Strefan's flying tokens to trade efficiently against ground-based aggro or to pressure planeswalkers. Prioritize clearing their most disruptive permanent before developing your own board.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Wrath of God, Anger of the Gods, Cyclonic Rift), counterspells, and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for big threats or when they start hoarding removal. Track their life total and board state to anticipate when they will go all-in or stabilize.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Strefan with flying tokens, Patron of the Vein pumped to lethal, Sanctum Seeker mana for late-game spells, and removal to clear the path. Sometimes a late-game creature like Bloodtithe Harvester or direct damage spells can close out the game.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have card advantage, removal for their blockers, and they lack sweepers or counterspells. Commit when your life total is stable and you can trade efficiently, or when you can force them to use resources on your small threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're at 2 life with no removal, facing a board with multiple sweepers, or if your key pieces are dead and you have no way to stabilize. Also concede if you're locked out of your game plan by hate cards (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) and lack sideboard answers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Split your spells across turns, use cheap interaction to clear the way, and cast Strefan's non-creature spells when they can't respond. If they counter your key threats, pivot to Patron's pump and token pressure.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on mana dorks, and stabilize with removal. Don't get outpaced; use Patron to pressure and force them to use resources on your threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Early interaction, removal, and using tokens to block. Stabilize by turn 3-4. Use Sanctum Seeker to cast removal efficiently and Patron to trade into their threats.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with card draw, apply pressure with tokens, and use removal to clear their threats. Force them to use resources on your small threats and close out the game before they can stabilize.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Use Thoughtseize to disrupt their key pieces, trade efficiently, and leverage card draw. Prioritize clearing their removal and sweepers, and use Patron's pump to out-value their threats.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Generally, increase interaction against combo, add hate bears against control, and bring in more removal against aggro. Keep the core game plan intact and avoid overcommitting to niche hate cards. Prioritize cards that improve your win conditions or disrupt the opponent's primary strategy.
notes: Watch your life total carefully; Strefan's life loss can be fatal if you don't stabilize. Patron of the Vein's sacrifice is a key resource, so avoid giving opponents free targets if they have graveyard synergies. Sanctum Seeker provides flexible mana, so use it to cast non-creature spells efficiently. Avoid overcommitting to tokens if you lack removal. Common mistake: overusing Strefan's ability early and dying to a late-game sweeper. Always track opponent's hand and board state before committing.

philosophy: The deck aims to cast a high volume of cheap noncreature spells to rapidly grow Narci into a massive threat, leveraging graveyard recursion and card advantage to out-value opponents. It excels against control and combo decks that rely on single-target interaction, as its recursive value engine and escalating board presence overwhelm static defenses.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, Narci, Anikthea, or multiple cheap noncreature spells (cantrips, ramp, removal). Mulligan if you lack Narci/Anikthea and only have expensive spells, or if your mana base is severely broken (requires untapped B/G/W sources or fast mana).
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast a cheap cantrip or mana accelerator to establish tempo. Turn 2: Play a second land, cast another noncreature spell to grow Narci, or drop Narci if mana allows. Prioritize establishing a functional mana base and getting Narci on the board as early as possible to start the counter engine.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro: Bring in targeted removal, protection spells, and early-game blockers. Against control/combo: Bring in graveyard hate, additional card draw, and spells that can't be countered. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal, card advantage engines, and recursion protection.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for Narci growth versus answering threats. Use Anikthea to cast spells from the graveyard to trigger Narci again without depleting your hand. Avoid overcommitting to casting spells if Narci is at risk of removal; conserve resources for protection or recursion.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Narci to the board when you have protection (Archon of Sun's Grace, counters, or hexproof) or enough counters to survive removal. Hold back noncreature spells if the board is unstable or if you need to answer a major opponent threat first.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for targeted removal (e.g., Murder, Doom Blade), board wipes (e.g., Wrath of God), and graveyard hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Grafdigger's Cage). Note when opponents tap out for interaction, as this is your window to cast Narci or Anikthea safely.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Narci with 10+ counters attacking unblocked, or using Anikthea to cast a game-ending spell from the graveyard. Archon of Sun's Grace can also close games if it survives long enough to trade or attack.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have protection, sufficient card advantage to rebuild after a wipe, or when the opponent has exhausted their removal/counter spells. Commit when Narci is large enough to trade favorably or go unblocked.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if Narci is repeatedly removed without recursion, you are out of card draw, or you are facing a combo that can win before turn 10 with no interaction. Also concede if your mana base is completely broken and you cannot cast noncreature spells to grow Narci.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap, multiple spells to force out counter spells, then cast Narci or Anikthea when they are tapped out. Utilize protection, hexproof, or instant-speed recursion to bypass counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Narci and use removal to stop their big threats before they stabilize. Do not let them reach their win conditions; pressure them with recursive value and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with targeted removal and Archon of Sun's Grace, then transition to Narci once you have card advantage. Use Anikthea to recycle spells and maintain pressure while out-grinding their early aggression.
threat_responses.against_control: Out-value them with Anikthea recursion and graveyard spells. Avoid overextending; wait for them to use interaction, then cast Narci or key spells when they are tapped out. Use card draw to out-resource their removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing card draw, targeted removal, and graveyard hate. Control the board with Archon of Sun's Grace and win on Narci's size. Force trades that favor your recursion engine and out-value them in the late game.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift focus based on the metagame. Against control/combo, bring in more interaction and recursion protection. Against aggro, prioritize removal and early-game stabilization. In mirrors, emphasize card advantage and targeted removal. Always keep Narci and Anikthea as core pieces, and adjust your noncreature spell ratio based on how much interaction you expect.
notes: Common mistakes include overcommitting to casting spells without protection, ignoring graveyard hate, and mismanaging mana for Narci's growth. Unique interactions: Anikthea allows you to cast Narci from the graveyard to trigger its own death trigger again, creating a recursive value loop. Archon of Sun's Grace can protect Narci or act as a secondary win condition. Watch for 'when you cast' triggers from opponents and plan your spell sequencing accordingly.

philosophy: This deck operates as a midrange-control hybrid that stabilizes early with disruption and mana acceleration, then deploys Etali, Primal Conqueror as a game-ending threat that leverages the graveyard to generate overwhelming board presence. It wins against combo and control by out-resourcing them and punishing slow games, while relying on efficient spot removal and land disruption to close out aggro matchups before they snowball.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including basics for G/R), at least one mana acceleration source (Sol Ring, Arcane Signet, or equivalent ramp), and a turn-1 or turn-2 disruption spell (Thoughtseize, Lightning Strike, or Go for the Throat). Mulligan hands with 3+ lands and no early plays, multiple high-cost cards without acceleration, or only removal with no way to develop mana or disrupt.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, then cast disruption (Thoughtseize on combo pieces or early threats) or drop Sol Ring/Arcane Signet if opening light. Turn 2: Develop your second land, cast acceleration if available, and evaluate whether to drop a mid-game threat or hold for Etali. Prioritize untapped lands for flexibility and ensure you can cast your disruption spells in response to opponent's opening moves.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: Bring in Lightning Strike, Go for the Throat, Blood Moon, or similar spot removal/land destruction to stabilize early. vs Control/Combo: Bring in additional disruption (Thoughtseize, Negate), graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), and card advantage to out-value them. vs Mirror: Bring in extra removal, graveyard hate, and sideboard cards that disrupt recursion or protect your own Etali.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Balance mana for Etali (6 mana) against early threats and disruption. Avoid overextending into sweepers; keep hand size optimal for answering key threats. Use the graveyard strategically—Etali's trigger is powerful but can be answered, so don't dump your entire graveyard if you can close the game faster with direct damage or a board of creatures.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when the opponent has no sweepers or spot removal in play or hand. Hold back Etali if they have potential board wipes (Wrath of God, Cyclonic Rift) or if you lack protection. Use disruption to remove their answers before committing your win condition.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, spot removal, and graveyard hate. Note if the opponent is playing combo pieces that need to be disrupted early. Track their mana base to predict when they can cast their own threats or answers. Pay attention to graveyard contents if they run recursion or mill strategies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Etali attacking with graveyard recursion, or comboing with bounce/flicker effects to generate multiple triggers. Sometimes a board full of creatures from the graveyard triggers wins the game instantly. Direct damage spells can also close out games if the opponent is at low life.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when the opponent has no sweepers, you have mana open for protection (bounce, flash, or counterspell), or you can close the game in one combat step. Commit when you've disrupted their key answers and have established board control.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're down to 1 life, the opponent has a sweeper in hand, you have no removal or protection, and your hand is dead. Also concede against combo decks that can combo off before you can stabilize, or if you're facing a mirror where you're clearly behind in resources and graveyard advantage.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap disruption to remove counterspells, or cast Etali in response to their counter to potentially win before they can respond. Use bounce/flicker effects to bypass counterspells, or play around them by holding up protection until they've used their answers.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with disruption and spot removal on their ramp creatures. Don't let them stabilize; use land destruction and efficient removal to keep them behind on mana. Force them to answer your threats while you develop your own board.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and hold up mana for protection. Use Etali as a late-game finisher once the board is clear. Don't overextend into early aggression; prioritize survival and resource denial until you can close the game.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with mid-game threats and use disruption to remove their answers. Close the game quickly before they can draw into sweepers or card advantage. Force them to use resources on your threats while you develop your own board and graveyard advantage.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by out-sequenceing and out-valuing. Use graveyard hate to disrupt their recursion, and protect your own Etali with bounce or flash. Track their hand and graveyard to predict their plays, and don't overcommit to Etali if they have removal or sweepers ready.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift towards more disruption and graveyard hate against combo/control, and more spot removal/land destruction against aggro. Adjust land counts if you're facing decks that heavily disrupt your mana base (e.g., Blood Moon, Field of the Dead). Prioritize keeping your core disruption and acceleration while swapping in matchup-specific hate cards.
notes: Etali's graveyard trigger is a win-more card but can close games; don't overcommit to it if you can win faster. Watch for Leyline of the Void or Rest in Peace, which can neuter your strategy. Mana base is crucial; ensure you can cast Etali reliably without flooding. Common mistake: casting Etali into a sweeper without protection. Always track opponent's hand and graveyard to predict their answers.

philosophy: Fire Lord Azula is a value-control archetype that leverages targeted damage and card draw to out-resource opponents, winning through attrition, precise removal, and chipping away at life totals while maintaining card advantage.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (ideally including one of each color), Sol Ring or Arcane Signet, and at least one early interaction or turn-2 play. Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, no color fixers, or only high-cost cards with no way to cast them before turn 3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, drop Sol Ring/Arcane Signet if possible. Turn 2: Cast a cheap cantrip or removal, or play a second land. Prioritize stabilizing the board and setting up mana for Azula by turn 4-5. Avoid overextending into potential sweepers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in board wipes, cheap removal, and life gain. vs Control/Combo (Ur-Dragon, Y'shtola): Bring in counterspells, discard, and hate bears. vs Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and card advantage.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize keeping mana open for Azula's abilities and interaction. Use card draw to maintain hand size and dig for answers. Don't overcommit to the board if you can stabilize with removal or if you need to preserve mana for interaction.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Azula when you have protection (counters, hexproof, or board state advantage) or can trade effectively. Hold back if facing sweepers or if you need to preserve mana for interaction. Use the 1-damage ability to clear small threats or chip life, and the 2-damage ability for key creatures or planeswalkers.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers, targeted removal, and combo pieces. Note when opponents tap out or hold up mana for counterspells. Track life totals closely, as Azula's win condition relies on consistent damage output.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Fire Lord Azula's 2-damage ability targeting players, combined with burn spells or repeated taps, to close out games. Use the 1-damage ability to draw cards and maintain pressure while chipping away at life totals.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: When you have protection (counters, hexproof, or board state advantage) and opponents are at low life or have no sweepers. Go all-in when you can safely tap Azula repeatedly without fear of removal or sweepers.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: If you're locked out of lands, facing a fast combo that can't be answered, or if your hand is dead against control. Fold if you have no way to stabilize or close out the game within a reasonable timeframe.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap cantrips to dig through, cast Azula at end step, or use mana rocks to cast it when they're tapped out. Bait counterspells with cheaper threats before committing your key pieces.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure, use removal on key ramp pieces, and don't let them get too far ahead. Disrupt their mana development with discard or targeted removal.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal, life gain, and board wipes. Transition to Azula once threats are cleared. Don't overcommit to the board early; focus on survival and card advantage.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with early threats, use Azula to draw into answers, and exploit their mana denial. Bait counterspells and play around their sweepers by spreading threats across the board.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the card advantage war, use targeted removal efficiently, and time Azula's abilities to out-value them. Prioritize protection for your Azula and force unfavorable trades.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your mana base if needed, prioritize hate cards against specific archetypes, and consider adding more interaction or protection depending on the meta. Tailor your sideboard to the local metagame, focusing on the most common threats.
notes: Common mistakes: Over-tapping Azula without considering protection, ignoring mana screw, failing to track opponent's life totals. Unique interactions: Azula's abilities can target yourself to draw cards, but be careful with life total. Synergizes well with artifact ramp and burn spells. Always track opponent's life totals and plan your damage output accordingly.

philosophy: Green tribal beatdown focused on overwhelming the board with trampling Bear tokens and applying relentless pressure. Wins by out-scaling opponents through token generation, direct damage, and unblockable threats that punish reactive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 Forests and a turn-1 or turn-2 threat (Ayula, Werebear, or a cheap bear generator/pump). Mulligan hands with 0-1 lands, multiple high-cost spells without early threats, or hands that cannot cast Ayula/Werebear by turn 2-3.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a Forest. Turn 2: Cast Werebear or Ayula if mana allows; otherwise, play a land and hold for turn 3. Sequence lands to hit 3 mana on turn 2 or 4 mana on turn 3. Attack immediately to establish board presence and trigger token generation.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in removal, board wipes, and life gain. Against control/combo (Ur-Dragon, Prismari): Bring in more threats, card draw, and protection. Against mirror: Bring in targeted removal and hate bears/pump spells.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Prioritize mana efficiency; avoid overcommitting to one side of the board if you cannot follow up. Use card draw to maintain hand size, as bear tribal decks often run thin. Protect key threats with pump spells or protection if available.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or protection ready for the opponent's answer. Hold back if the opponent has a board wipe or hard counter in play. Use tokens to trade efficiently and force unfavorable blocks.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for board wipes, targeted removal, and counterspells. Note if the opponent is playing sweepers or hate cards. Track their mana development to anticipate when they can cast their answers.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Ayula attacking with multiple tokens, often boosted by pump spells. Unblocked trampling damage is the primary win condition.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have established a board of 3+ creatures, the opponent has no sweepers, or you can clear their blockers with removal/pump. Also commit when you have card advantage and can out-value their removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you are down to 1-2 lands with no threats, the opponent has a locked board (e.g., multiple sweepers, hard counters, or a combo finisher), or you have drawn into a dead hand with no way to interact or win.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use cheap threats, multikicker, or split the cast. Attack with multiple small creatures to force counter decisions. Use pump spells on resolution or cast threats in response to counterspells.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race them before they hit their big mana. Use removal to answer their ramp targets or finishers. Consider hate bears or discard if the format allows.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with removal and board wipes. Play defensively until you can establish your own board, then transition to offense. Use life gain to survive early damage.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure consistently to force them to use resources on interaction rather than combo/finishers. Use unblockable threats and pump spells to bypass board wipes. Out-value them with card draw and efficient removal.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win by out-resourcing with better card draw, more efficient removal, or superior pump spells. Trade creatures efficiently and prioritize clearing their Ayula/Werebear first.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw beatdown to a more interactive or resilient strategy. Against control/combo, prioritize hate cards, additional threats, and protection. Against aggro, lean into removal and sweepers. Always ensure your mana base can support the new sideboard cards.
notes: Ayula's token generation scales with combat, so attacking with multiple creatures maximizes value. Watch out for cantrips or cheap removal that can trade favorably against your tokens. Avoid overcommitting to a single attack if you suspect a board wipe; use your tokens to probe for answers.

archetype: Rograkh, Son of Rohgahh // Silas Renn, Seeker Adept
philosophy: A Grixis control-combo deck that uses early discard and spot removal to stabilize, then transitions into a recursive mana engine with Springleaf Drum and Silas Renn to repeatedly pump Rograkh or zombie tokens for lethal, while leveraging graveyard synergy for sustained value.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize/Duress), and either Springleaf Drum or a creature to cast it on. Prioritize hands with 2+ mana sources and early interaction. Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early play, 0-1 lands, or hands lacking any way to disrupt or start your engine.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Thoughtseize or Duress to strip the opponent's key combo piece or removal. Turn 2: Play a land, cast a creature or Springleaf Drum on a creature. If you have a creature and mana, tap the creature for mana, cast Silas Renn, then tap Silas to pump Rograkh. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast interaction on turn 1 or 2 without flooding your board.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in +2 Fatal Push, +1 Anger of the Gods, -1 Springleaf Drum, -1 extra threat. vs Control/Combo (Prismari, Y'shtola, Ur-Dragon): Bring in +1 Negate, +1 additional discard, -1 mana rock. vs Mirror: Bring in +1 targeted removal, +1 card draw, -1 discard.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve mana for interaction until your engine is online. Use Springleaf Drum to fuel multiple spells or pump sequences. Don't overcommit creatures if you can stabilize with removal. Manage your graveyard carefully; Silas Renn needs creatures there, but avoid giving opponents graveyard synergies.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats only when you can protect them or have a clear path to combo off. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use discard proactively to strip combo pieces or removal before they can be cast.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Damnation, Anger, etc.), targeted removal (Fatal Push, Murder), and combo win conditions. Note when opponents tap out for a single spell—they're vulnerable to discard or pump sequences. Track graveyard contents to anticipate Silas Renn recursion or opponent graveyard synergies.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Silas Renn pumping Rograkh (or a large zombie) for lethal, or repeatedly bouncing Silas/Rograkh to generate extended value. Zombie tokens can also close games if the opponent is low on life or board space.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have a clear path to lethal, the opponent has no sweepers, or you've stripped their key answers via discard. Commit when you can safely cast Silas Renn and pump without fear of immediate removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a hard lock with no draw engine, have no interaction against a combo deck, or are down to 1 life with no mana/creatures to combo off. Also concede if the opponent has multiple sweepers and you're out of card advantage.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to strip counterspells. Cast threats in response to their counterspells, or use Springleaf Drum to generate mana for multiple threats. If possible, use cheap threats or pump in response to counters.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with discard and spot removal. Don't let them establish a large board. Use your engine to out-value their ramp, and target their mana sources if possible.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize spot removal and discard to disrupt their curve. Use Springleaf Drum to pump your own creatures or generate mana for multiple removal spells. Don't overcommit to a single threat; stabilize first.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with discard to strip their answers. Use your combo to go over the top of their removal. If they try to grind, use your zombie tokens and card advantage to outlast them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war. Prioritize discard to strip their combo pieces or removal. Protect your engine. Use targeted removal to kill their threats before they can combo off. Card advantage and precise sequencing will decide the match.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust based on metagame trends. Against combo-heavy formats, prioritize additional discard and counterspells. Against aggro, bring in board wipes and cheap removal. In mirrors, focus on targeted removal and card draw. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't dilute your core combo or mana base.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Springleaf Drum on non-essential creatures, leaving you without a pump target. Remember that Silas Renn's ability returns a creature from graveyard to hand, not directly to the battlefield, so plan your mana accordingly. Rograkh's death trigger creates tokens, which can be used as additional mana sources or pump targets. Watch out for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Blood Moon) as it can disrupt Silas Renn's recursion.

philosophy: This deck leverages Codie, Vociferous Codex as both a flexible mana source and a spell-copying engine to amplify high-impact instants and sorceries, particularly Mizzix's Mastery. It aims to outvalue opponents through spell synergy and flexible interaction, winning against control and combo decks by overwhelming them with copied damage or board wipes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with Codie, at least two lands (or a land plus a mana fixer/rock), and early interaction (Lightning Axe, Fatal Push, or similar). Mulligan on 3+ lands with no Codie or interaction, or hands with only one color of mana and no fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, hold up interaction if possible. Turn 2: Play Codie if mana allows, or play a second land and hold. If opening with Codie, play it on turn 2 or 3, then use its tap ability to fix mana for Mizzix's Mastery or Increasing Vengeance. Prioritize establishing Codie before committing to big spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro: +2 Lightning Axe, +2 Fatal Push, -2 Mizzix's Mastery, -1 Increasing Vengeance. vs Control: +2 Mizzix's Mastery, +1 Increasing Vengeance, -2 early interaction, -1 land. vs Combo: +2 Dispel/Counterspell, +1 Ancient Grudge, -2 non-essential interaction. vs Mirror: +2 Counterspell, +1 Ancient Grudge, -2 Mizzix's Mastery.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Codie as a conditional mana source; avoid sacrificing it unless you have a guaranteed win or need to copy a game-ending spell. Track graveyard contents to avoid self-targeting with Mizzix's Mastery. Manage hand size by prioritizing high-impact spells over cantrips.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit Codie early to stabilize, but hold back on sacrificing it until you have a clear target for Mizzix's Mastery or a board wipe. Use cheap removal to clear early threats, then transition to copying Mizzix's Mastery for board sweeps.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for counterspells (hold up interaction or use Increasing Vengeance to copy your own spells), board wipes (save Codie for post-sweep value), and combo pieces (use discard or targeted removal). Note when opponents tap out for counterspells vs. when they hold up removal.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Copied Mizzix's Mastery (via Codie or Increasing Vengeance) is the primary win condition. Secondary finishers include copied Lightning Axe or Dragon's Fire for targeted removal.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have Codie on the battlefield, sufficient mana for Mizzix's Mastery plus a copy effect, and a clear path to lethal or board control. Commit when the opponent has no interaction left or is locked out of responding.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that outpaces your stabilization, if you have no interaction against a lock (e.g., mass counterspells or artifact hate), or if your hand is dead against a resilient board.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Increasing Vengeance to copy your own instants, cast spells in response to counterspells, or leverage Codie's mana to cast multiple spells in a single turn.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with cheap interaction, use Codie to copy removal, and prioritize board wipes to neutralize their big threats.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with cheap removal and board wipes, then use Codie's mana to cast Mizzix's Mastery or Increasing Vengeance to clear the board and stabilize.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with Codie, force interaction, and use Mizzix's Mastery to clear their board. Copy their spells if possible, or use your own high-impact spells to outvalue them.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value exchange is key. Protect Codie, use counterspells to protect your Mizzix's Mastery, and prioritize copying their Mizzix's Mastery for value. Trade efficiently and look for the first mistake.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, adjust your interaction curve based on the metagame. Against control-heavy formats, bring in more counterspells and hate cards. Against aggro, prioritize early removal and discard. Always ensure your sideboard plan preserves your Codie/Mizzix's Mastery synergy while addressing specific matchup weaknesses.
notes: Codie is legendary, so you cannot have two in play. Sacrificing Codie to copy a spell is a one-time use, so prioritize copying Mizzix's Mastery or game-winning instants. Be cautious of cantrip interaction that can out-value your spells. Mizzix's Mastery damages all other creatures, so avoid having multiple copies of it in play. Watch for artifact hate and bring in non-artifact threats if needed.

philosophy: Grixis Disa is a midrange-control hybrid that uses discard to dictate the early game, stabilizes with Disa’s exile ability, and closes out games using flying Spirit tokens or synergistic creatures. It excels against combo and control by disrupting their plans and outvaluing them, while relying on efficient interaction and late-game resilience against aggressive strategies.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one discard spell (Thoughtseize, Duress, or Inquisition), and either Ignoble Hierarch or Barrowgoyf. Keep Disa if paired with a discard outlet or early interaction. Mulligan pure land hands, hands with multiple copies of low-impact cards, or hands lacking early interaction when facing aggro.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Ignoble Hierarch or Barrowgoyf if mana allows, or hold for a discard spell. Turn 2: Cast a discard spell targeting the opponent’s key early threat or combo piece. If ahead, develop board with Hierarch/Goyf; if behind, use discard to disrupt and stabilize. Land sequencing: Prioritize duals (Steam Vents, Blood Crypt, Stomping Ground) if available, otherwise basics that tap for needed colors. Avoid overextending into potential sweepers.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech, Loafing Pest): +1 Bloodtithe Harvester, +1 Grim Flayer, +1 Fatal Push, +1 Lightning Strike, -2 Disa, -2 discard outlets. vs Control/Combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed, Ur-Dragon): +1 Disa, +1 Duress, +1 Inquisition, -2 aggro interaction. vs Prismari: +1 Disa, +1 Lightning Strike, +1 Fatal Push, -2 discard outlets.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Preserve discard spells for high-value targets (combo pieces, key aggro threats, or control hand disruption). Use Disa’s +1 sparingly; only draw when you need to find an answer or close the game. Manage graveyard resources carefully—Barrowgoyf and discard outlets thrive here, but avoid over-sacrificing if you need creatures for board presence.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to protect them. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Disa’s -1 to exile unblockable or game-winning threats, but don’t waste it on chump blockers. Maintain card advantage to outlast their interaction.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Supreme Verdict, Anger of the Gods), counterspells (Spell Pierce, Arcane Denial), and graveyard recursion. Note when opponents tap out for big spells—this is your window to attack or cast discard. Track if they’re playing around your discard by keeping multiple threats or sideboarding in hate cards like Rest in Peace.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Disa the Restless (via +2 tokens or -1 exile to clear path), Barrowgoyf (sacrifice for flying +X/+X), and pumped Hierarchs. Get there by stabilizing with discard/removal, then attacking with flying threats or using Disa’s loyalty to draw into closure.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you’ve removed their sweepers, they’re at low life, or you have a clear path to combat. Also commit if you’ve established board control and they lack answers, or if you can force multiple counterspells with multiple threats.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you’re facing a locked-out control deck with no removal, have a dead hand against aggro, or the opponent has established an unassailable board with no comeback tools. Also concede if you’re out of resources and they have multiple sweepers or unblockable finishers.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use discard to hit their hand before casting threats. Cast spells in response to their counterspells if possible. Use Disa’s -1 to exile threats they can't counter, or attack with multiple threats to force multiple counters. Avoid overcommitting to a single spell.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to race their mana. Use discard to hit their ramp spells or key threats. Disa’s -1 is crucial to exile their finishers. Don’t let them stabilize; pressure relentlessly and force them to interact early.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize early discard to disrupt their curve. Use Fatal Push/Lightning Strike for 1-2 drops. Disa’s -1 clears boards. Hold back on overcommitting; stabilize and close out with flying threats. Don’t trade up unnecessarily.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure early to force interaction. Use discard to hit their removal/counters. Disa’s +1 outvalues their card draw. Attack with flying threats to bypass ground removal. Force them to use multiple answers to close out the game.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Value discard efficiency and creature synergies. Prioritize keeping hands with multiple discard spells and early creatures. Use Disa’s -1 to exile their key threats. Win by out-resourcing and forcing them to overcommit. Track their sideboarded hate and adjust discard targets accordingly.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a midrange-control balance to a more focused game plan. Against aggro, bring in more early interaction and discard outlets to stabilize. Against control/combo, bring in Disa, additional discard, and late-game threats. Always carry at least one board wipe or sweepers if the meta demands it. Adjust land counts if facing heavy mana bases, but Grixis duals usually handle it. Prioritize sideboarding in cards that disrupt their specific win conditions.
notes: Common mistake: Overusing Disa’s +1 too early, leaving you with no card advantage when you need it. Another: Sacrificing Barrowgoyf too early without a clear payoff. Remember that Ignoble Hierarch’s -1/-1 effect can be used to sacrifice to Grim Flayer/Bloodtithe Harvester for value. Disa’s -1 exiles from all zones, making it excellent against recursion. Watch for opponent’s sideboarded hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void) and adjust discard targets accordingly. Always track their graveyard and library if they run graveyard strategies.

philosophy: This Black Zombie midrange deck aims to out-value opponents through relentless token generation, death-trigger card draw, and efficient sacrifice outlets. It wins by stabilizing early with lifelink/vigilance swarms, then grinding out games with K'rrik's upkeep tokens and Sheoldred's death triggers, overwhelming control, combo, and slower midrange archetypes.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands and at least one of Sheoldred, K'rrik, or Dark Ritual. Prioritize hands with 2+ mana sources and a clear turn-1/2 play. Mulligan to hands with 3+ lands and no early threats, or hands with 0-1 lands. Keep hands that can play a land and cast Dark Ritual or a 2-drop on turn 1.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Dark Ritual if possible to accelerate into a 2-drop, or play a cheap Zombie/land. Turn 2: If you have Dark Ritual, cast K'rrik or a 3-drop. If not, play a land and a 2-drop. Sequence lands to ensure you can cast Dark Ritual on turn 1 if possible, as it enables aggressive early plays. If opening with multiple lands, consider holding a land for turn 2 to cast K'rrik or Sheoldred on curve.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: Against Aggro/Token (Krenko, Blech): Bring in Graveyard hate (e.g., Rest in Peace, Leyline of the Void), spot removal (Fatal Push, Dismember), and lifelink/vigilance enablers. Against Control/Combo (Prismari, Ur-Dragon): Bring in card advantage (Thoughtseize, Duress), graveyard recursion, and board wipes that spare your tokens (e.g., Damnation, though be careful). Against Mirror: Bring in targeted removal and discard to disrupt their K'rrik/Sheoldred engines.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Treat Dark Ritual as a tempo accelerator, not just mana. Prioritize keeping K'rrik on the board for steady token generation and removal. Use sacrifice outlets sparingly until you need to trigger Sheoldred's draw or remove a key threat. Manage your graveyard carefully; avoid unnecessary sac-fills if you're facing graveyard hate. Draw cards on death to maintain hand advantage, but don't overcommit to drawing if you're at low life.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have removal or discard to answer their interaction. Hold back if you're facing sweepers or heavy removal; instead, build a board of 1/1s and 2/2s to force unfavorable trades. Use K'rrik's sacrifice ability to remove key threats when you have a board of zombies to spare. Prioritize trading your tokens into their removal to preserve your value engines.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (e.g., Wrath of God, Damnation) and graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline). Track their discard spells; if they've used Thoughtseize/Duress, they likely lack interaction for your late-game. Monitor their mana development; if they're ramping, prioritize early pressure. Note when they save removal for your key threats (Sheoldred/K'rrik) versus their board.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Sheoldred, the Apocalypse is your primary finisher. Once you have a board of zombies and can safely cast her, her ETB tokens and death triggers will out-draw and out-grind most decks. K'rrik can also close games by repeatedly sac-fueling removal to clear the board while your tokens attack. Combine token swarms with discard to lock them out of interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have 3+ zombies on board, a clear path to Sheoldred, and no immediate sweepers in play. Commit when your opponent has exhausted their interaction (discard, removal, counters) and you can safely trade tokens into their blockers. Also commit when you're ahead on life and can afford to trade down to trigger Sheoldred's draw.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a fast combo that can win before turn 4, or if you're locked out by Leyline of the Void/Rest in Peace with no graveyard recursion. Concede if you're at 2 life, have no cards in hand, and your opponent has a board wipe ready. Also concede if you're in a hard mirror and they've established a K'rrik/Sheoldred lock with no way to break it.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use Dark Ritual to cast threats in response to counterspells if possible, or sequence your plays to hit non-interactive lands first. Rely on discard (Thoughtseize/Duress) to remove their counterspells. If they counter your key threats, use K'rrik's sac ability to remove their blockers and trade into their removal, forcing them to use more resources.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure with Dark Ritual acceleration. Use discard to hit their ramp spells or lands. If they outpace you, stabilize with K'rrik's tokens and sacrifice removal to clear their threats. Prioritize graveyard hate if their ramp is combo-adjacent.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Prioritize lifelink/vigilance tokens to stabilize. Use spot removal to hit their key threats. Trade your 1/1s into their removal to preserve your value engines. If they go wide, use K'rrik's sac ability to remove their biggest threats. Don't overcommit to drawing cards if you're at low life; focus on stabilization.
threat_responses.against_control: Apply consistent pressure with K'rrik's upkeep tokens and Sheoldred's ETB. Use discard to remove their counterspells and key answers. Force them to use removal on your tokens to preserve your value engines. If they sweep, rebuild with K'rrik and draw into Sheoldred. Out-grind them with death-trigger card draw.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the mirror by controlling the board with K'rrik's tokens and using discard to disrupt their hand. Prioritize removing their K'rrik first to stop their token generation. Use Sheoldred's ETB to swing the board and trigger draw on death. Trade your tokens into their removal to force unfavorable trades. If they go wide, use K'rrik's sac ability to remove their threats while you rebuild.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from raw value to matchup-specific disruption. Against control/combo, prioritize discard and graveyard recursion to maintain hand advantage. Against aggro/token, bring in lifelink/vigilance enablers and spot removal to stabilize. In mirrors, prioritize targeted removal and discard to disrupt their engines. Always ensure your sideboard doesn't overcommit to one archetype; keep a balanced mix of hate, removal, and card advantage.
notes: Dark Ritual is your tempo engine; never treat it as a dead draw. K'rrik's sacrifice ability is a pseudo-removal spell, so use it to clear key threats rather than just generating tokens. Sheoldred's death triggers are powerful but can backfire if you're at low life; manage your life total carefully. Avoid over-sacrificing zombies if you're facing graveyard hate. In unknown formats, test your land base carefully to ensure you can consistently cast Dark Ritual on turn 1.

archetype: Atreus, Impulsive Son // Kratos, Stoic Father
philosophy: The deck leverages a dual-state engine to fluidly shift between explosive tempo aggression and resilient control, using Iroas, God of Victory as a combat and value anchor. It aims to out-resource and out-adapt midrange, control, and combo archetypes by dictating pace, trading efficiently, and closing out games through combat advantage or late-game inevitability.

opening_strategy.mulligan_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands (including at least one Arcane Signet or a dual land capable of producing R/U/W), Kratos/Atreus, and a mix of turn 1-2 interaction or a finisher. Mulligan if you have zero lands, only single-color sources with no Signet, or a hand composed entirely of high-cost spells with no early plays or mana fixing.
opening_strategy.early_game_plays: Turn 1: Play a land, cast Arcane Signet if available, or deploy a cheap creature/spell to establish board presence. Turn 2: Cast Kratos/Atreus, choosing Stoic mode to draw/filter or Impulsive mode to attack/trigger effects. Sequence lands to ensure R/U/W availability by turn 3, prioritizing Signet drops to accelerate into key spells.
opening_strategy.sideboard_plan: vs Aggro (Krenko, Blech): Bring in targeted removal, counters, and board wipes. vs Control/Combo (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed): Bring in card advantage, hatebears, and graveyard/artifact hate. vs Midrange/Control (Prismari, The Ur-Dragon): Bring in threat protection, additional removal, and artifact hate. vs Mirror: Bring in hand disruption, targeted removal, and cards that punish dual-state guessing.

midgame_priorities.resource_management: Maintain a steady R/U/W mana base and use Arcane Signet to accelerate into pivotal spells. Manage your hand by trading early threats for interaction, saving Iroas for late-game combat or value triggers. Avoid overcommitting to one state; keep options open to adapt to the board state.
midgame_priorities.board_control: Commit threats when you have interaction to clear blockers or answer responses. Hold back if the opponent has sweepers or heavy removal. Use Kratos's defensive capabilities to stabilize, then pivot to Impulsive aggression when it is safe to do so.
midgame_priorities.threat_tracking: Watch for sweepers (Krenko's goblin tokens, Ur-Dragon's board presence), counterspells (Prismari), and combo pieces (Y'shtola, Night's Blessed). Key tells include opponents holding up mana on their turn, playing cheap instants/artifacts, or sacrificing creatures for incremental value.

endgame_tactics.finishers: Iroas, God of Victory as a combat finisher or value engine. Kratos/Atreus in Impulsive mode with pumped stats. Late-game card advantage engines to out-value control and close out games through incremental damage or combat tricks.
endgame_tactics.when_to_commit: Go all-in when you have removal for their answer, have established board presence, or when the opponent is at lethal range. Commit when you can trade efficiently or when you've drawn into your win condition and they lack interaction.
endgame_tactics.when_to_concede: Fold if you're facing a locked-out control/combo deck with no interaction, if your hand is dead against their specific hate, or if you're down to one life with no board presence and they have sweepers or removal ready to shut you down.

threat_responses.against_countermagic: Use layered spells (impulsive mode first, stoic mode second) and cast around counters with cheap threats. Use Arcane Signet to cast multiple spells in a turn, creating resolution windows. Employ uncounterable elements or protection if available.
threat_responses.against_ramp: Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on key ramp creatures and hold up interaction for their big threats. Don't overextend into their late-game finishers.
threat_responses.against_aggro: Stabilize with early removal and defensive Kratos plays. Don't overextend; trade efficiently and survive to turn 4-5. Post-sideboard, prioritize board wipes and direct damage to handle token or swarm strategies.
threat_responses.against_control: Pressure with resilient threats and card advantage to out-value them. Force them to use removal on multiple targets. Pivot to a longer game if they have sweepers, and use Iroas to win combat exchanges they can't answer.
threat_responses.vs_mirror: Win the resource war by prioritizing hand disruption and targeted removal. Exploit the dual-state mechanic by forcing them to guess your mode. Win combat exchanges with Iroas advantage and maintain better card selection.

sideboard_advice: Post-sideboard, shift from a balanced midrange/control shell to a specialized game plan. Against fast decks, prioritize removal and early interaction. Against slow decks, add more card advantage and late-game finishers. Always keep at least one copy of Kratos/Atreus and Iroas unless specifically targeted. Adjust land counts if facing heavy artifact or non-basic hate, and consider adding utility lands that produce R/U/W.
notes: Common mistake: Overcommitting to Impulsive mode without backup, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers. Always maintain a balance between the two states. Iroas synergizes best with combat-heavy or +1/+1 counter strategies, so track opponent's board state carefully. Arcane Signet is crucial for tempo; don't leave it untapped if you can cast a spell. The 'Unknown' format may have specific rules about the dual-state mechanic; verify timing, priority, and stacking order before tournament play.
